blocks:
Ayahs

أَتَىٰٓ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١)
Allah’s edict is coming! So do not seek to hasten it. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (1)
يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ بِٱلرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱتَّقُونِ (٢)
He sends down the angels with the spirit of His command to whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn [the people] that there is no god except Me; so be wary of Me.’ (2)

Content of the Surah

Although some commentators consider all the verses of this Surah to be Meccan, most commentators believe that a portion of its verses was revealed in Mecca and a portion in Medina. Considering what we know of the content of Meccan and Medinan Surahs, this view seems more accurate, especially since some of its verses, such as Verse 41 (وَ الَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللَّهِ...) and Verse 110 (ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَ صَبَرُوا...), explicitly discuss "migration" (Hijrah) or both "migration and Jihad." We know that these two subjects are consistent with the events following the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration.

Even if we consider the migration mentioned in Verse 41 to refer to the first migration—namely, the migration of a group of Muslims led by Ja'far ibn Abi Talib from Mecca to Abyssinia—it is highly unlikely that the migration and Jihad mentioned together in Verse 110 refer to that event; they can only be applied to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration to Medina.

Furthermore, it is well-known in the commentary of Verse 126 (وَ إِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ...) that it was revealed during the Battle of Uhud, and the Battle of Uhud took place after the Hijrah.

Based on these points, some commentators have stated that the first forty verses of this Surah were revealed in Mecca and the rest in Medina, while others consider all its verses Meccan except for three verses which they say were revealed concerning the Battle of Uhud.

What is certain is that this Surah must be considered a combination of "Meccan and Medinan" verses, even if it is not possible—except in specific cases—to precisely determine the Meccan or Medinan status of every single verse.

In any case, the verses of this Surah contain both the specific discussions of Meccan Surahs (such as decisive arguments regarding Monotheism and the Resurrection, and a fierce struggle against polytheism and idolatry) and the specific discussions of Medinan Surahs (such as social laws and matters related to Jihad and migration).

Generally, it can be said that the content of this Surah consists of the following matters, which are beautifully and proportionately interwoven:

1- More than anything else, this Surah discusses the blessings of Allah, describing their intricacies in such a way that it awakens the sense of gratitude in every free-spirited human being, thereby drawing them closer to the Creator of all these blessings and bounties.

These blessings include those related to rain, sunlight, various types of plants, fruits, and other foodstuffs, animals that serve humans and the benefits and blessings derived from them, various means of living, and even the blessing of children and spouses—in short, they encompass "all types of pure things" (Tayyibat).

It is precisely for this reason that some have named it the Surah of "Ni'am" (the plural of Ni'mah, meaning blessing).

However, the most famous and well-known name for this Surah is "Surah al-Nahl" (The Bee), because amidst the enumeration of various divine blessings, it makes a brief, profound, and amazing reference to the honeybee, specifically emphasizing the important food substance humans obtain from it and the signs of Monotheism present in the life of this insect.

2- Another part of it discusses the proofs of Monotheism, the greatness of God's creation, the Resurrection, and warnings to the polytheists and criminals.

3- Another section speaks of various Islamic laws, such as the command to justice, kindness, migration, and Jihad, and the prohibition of indecency, evil, oppression, and breach of covenants. It also calls for gratitude for His blessings, and in this regard, it mentions Abraham, the hero of Monotheism, as a grateful servant in several verses.

4- Another part speaks of the innovations (Bid'ah) of the polytheists and, in this context, provides striking sensory parables.

5- Finally, in another section, it warns humans against the whisperings of Satan.

Virtue of the Surah

In some narrations from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is reported that he said:

من قراها لم يحاسبه اللَّه تعالى بالنعم التي انعمها عليه فى دار الدنيا:

"Whoever recites this Surah, Allah the Exalted will not call him to account for the blessings He bestowed upon him in the life of this world." [1] It is clear that reciting these verses, which enumerate a significant portion of divine blessings—especially a recitation accompanied by reflection, followed by decision-making and action, and stepping onto the path of gratitude—causes one to use every blessing exactly for the purpose for which it was created. In such a state, what accounting will be required of him, since he has utilized the blessings correctly?!

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 6, p. 347.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

أَتى‌ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ سُبْحانَهُ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (1) يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلاَّ أَنَا فَاتَّقُونِ (2)

Commentary

The Command of Punishment is Near

As previously mentioned, a significant portion of the opening verses of this Surah was revealed in Mecca, during the days when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in intense conflict with the polytheists and idolaters. Every day, they would resort to excuses against his life-giving and liberating call. Among these was that whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله threatened them with divine punishment, some of the stubborn ones would say: "If this punishment and retribution you speak of is true, then why does it not come upon us?!"

Perhaps they would sometimes add that if there were indeed a punishment, they would cling to the idols to intercede before Allah to remove this punishment from them—for are they not intercessors in His presence?! The first verse of this Surah draws a line of cancellation through these delusions and says: "Do not be in a hurry; the command of Allah for the punishment of the polytheists and criminals has certainly arrived" (أَتى‌ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ‌).

And if you think the idols are intercessors in His presence, you are gravely mistaken: "Glorified and Exalted is He above what they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَهُ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

Therefore, "أَمْرُ اللَّهِ‌" (the command of Allah) in the above verse refers to Allah's decree regarding the punishment of the polytheists. Although the word "أتى" (has come) is a past-tense verb signifying the realization of this command in the past, its meaning is that of a future event that will certainly occur. It is common in the Quran for an event that is certain to happen to be mentioned using the past tense.

Some commentators have also suggested that "Amr Allah" refers to the punishment itself rather than the command for it, and some have taken it to mean "the Day of Resurrection," but the commentary we mentioned seems closest to the truth.

And since no punishment or retribution occurs without sufficient explanation and a just completion of the proof (Itmam al-Hujjah), the next verse adds: "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whom He wills of His servants" (يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌) [1]

[1]: "Min" in "min amrihi" is in the sense of "bi" (with/by) and here conveys the meaning of causality.

"And He commands them: 'Warn [mankind] and frighten them against polytheism and idolatry, and say that there is no deity except Me'" (أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا).

"'Therefore, fear only My opposition' and feel a sense of responsibility toward Me" (فَاتَّقُونِ‌).

Regarding what is meant by "the Spirit" (al-Ruh) in this verse, commentators have discussed it extensively, but it appears that it refers to revelation, the Quran, and prophethood, which are the source of life and vitality for human beings.

Although some commentators have separated revelation from the Quran, and both from prophethood here, presenting them as three different interpretations, it appears they all return to a single reality.

In any case, "the Spirit" here has a spiritual dimension and refers to anything that is the source of life for hearts and the cause for the cultivation of souls and the guidance of intellects, just as we read in Verse 24 of Surah al-Anfal: "O you who have believed, respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life."

And in Surah Ghafir, Verse 15, we read: "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whom He wills of His servants." And in Surah al-Shura, Verse 52, it is stated: "And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit of Our command. You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith"! It is clear that "the Spirit" in these verses means "the Quran," "the content of revelation," and "the mandate of prophethood."

Although "Spirit" appears in other verses of the Quran with different meanings, given the context mentioned, the concept of Spirit in the verse under discussion is the Quran and the content of revelation.

It is also necessary to note that the phrase "upon whom He wills of His servants" (عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌) by no means implies that the gift of revelation and prophethood is arbitrary, for the divine "Will" (Mashiyyah) is never separated from His "Wisdom" (Hikmah). By virtue of His being All-Wise, He places this gift in the appropriate and worthy place:

"Allah knows best where to place His message" (Al-An'am - 124).

One should also not overlook the fact that if the first divine command to the prophets, according to the phrase "أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا" (that you warn), is warning and cautioning, it is because nothing is more effective for awakening a lost people mired in polytheism and corruption than a warning—a warning that is awakening, enlightening, and movement-generating!

It is true that humans both seek benefit and repel harm, but experience has shown that encouragement affects those who are more prepared, while the effect of threat is greater on those who are more corrupted. For the beginning of prophethood, striking warnings must be at the forefront of the program.

Ayahs

خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ تَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٣)
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. He is above having any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (3)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ (٤)
He created man from a drop of [seminal] fluid, and, behold, he is an open contender! (4)
وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ خَلَقَهَا ۗ لَكُمْ فِيهَا دِفْءٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٥)
He created the cattle, in which there is warmth for you and [other] uses, and some of them you eat. (5)
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا جَمَالٌ حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ (٦)
There is in them a beauty for you when you bring them home for rest and when you drive them forth to pasture. (6)
وَتَحْمِلُ أَثْقَالَكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَلَدٍ لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ بَـٰلِغِيهِ إِلَّا بِشِقِّ ٱلْأَنفُسِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧)
And they bear your burdens to towns, which you could not reach except by straining yourselves. Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful. (7)
وَٱلْخَيْلَ وَٱلْبِغَالَ وَٱلْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوهَا وَزِينَةً ۚ وَيَخْلُقُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٨)
And horses, mules and asses, for you to ride them, and for pomp, and He creates what you do not know. (8)

Commentary

The Diverse Benefits of Animals

Since the preceding verses discussed the negation of polytheism, these verses approach the uprooting of polytheism and the direction of attention toward the One God through two paths: first, through rational proofs by way of the wondrous system of creation and the grandeur of existence; and second, through the emotional path by mentioning the various blessings of Allah toward man, so that his sense of gratitude may be stimulated and ultimately draw him closer to God.

At the beginning, He says: "He created the heavens and the earth with truth" (خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ).

The truthfulness of the heavens and the earth is evident both from their extraordinary system and organized, calculated creation, and from the purpose and benefits inherent within them.

Following this, He adds: "Exalted is He above what they associate with Him" (تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

Are the idols they have set up as His partners ever capable of such creation? Can they even create a tiny gnat or a grain of dust? Given this, how can they associate them with Him?! It is interesting that the polytheists themselves attributed this wondrous system and exquisite creation—which manifests the knowledge and power of the Creator—only to "Allah," yet they would prostrate before idols during worship.

After referring to the creation of the heavens and the earth and their infinite secrets, He speaks of man himself—the being who is closer to himself than anyone else. He says:

"He created man from a [mere] drop of fluid; then behold, he stands as an open adversary" (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ).

"Nutfah" (نطفه) originally means a small amount of water or clear water; subsequently, it was applied to the drops of fluid that become the source of human origin through "fertilization." In truth, by using this expression, He intends to illustrate the grandeur of Allah's power: from a worthless drop of water, what a wondrous creation He has fashioned, such that there is so vast a distance between his arc of descent and ascent.

This interpretation holds if we take "Khasim" (خصيم) to mean "a defender and articulator of one's inner self," just as we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 105: وَ لا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً: "O Prophet, do not be an advocate for the treacherous."

However, in contrast to this interpretation accepted by a group of commentators, another interpretation has been expressed by others: that "Allah created man with His perfect power from a worthless drop, yet this ungrateful human rose in open dispute and contention against God" (taking verse 77 of Surah Ya-Sin as evidence for this interpretation).

But the first meaning seems more correct, as the above verses are in the position of describing the greatness of Allah's creation, and that greatness becomes manifest when He fashions a highly valuable being from a seemingly insignificant one.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we also read: خلقه من فطرة من ماء منتن فيكون خصيما متكلما بليغا: "Allah created man from a drop of foul-smelling water, and eventually he became an eloquent speaker." [1]

After the creation of man, He points to another important blessing, namely the creation of livestock and the various benefits derived from them, saying: "And the livestock He has created for you; in them is warmth and [other] benefits, and from them you eat" (وَ الْأَنْعامَ خَلَقَها لَكُمْ فِيها دِفْ‌ءٌ وَ مَنافِعُ وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ).

In this verse, He first addresses the creation of livestock, which is a proof of Allah's knowledge and power, and then proceeds to describe the various blessings found in them. Among these blessings, He mentions three parts: first, the issue of "Dif’" (دِفْ‌ءٌ), which means any kind of covering (using their wool and skins), such as clothing, quilts, shoes, hats, and tents; second, "benefits" (مَنافِعُ), which refers to milk and its derivatives; and third, meat, expressed by the phrase وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ (and from them you eat).

It is noteworthy that among all these benefits, He mentions the issue of clothing and shelter before anything else, because for many people (especially desert dwellers), both their clothes are prepared from wool, hair, or skin, as are their tents which protect them from heat and cold. In any case, this is evidence of the importance of clothing and shelter and their priority over everything else.

Another point is that He mentions it before "benefits," indicating that clothing is actually for repelling harm, and repelling harm takes precedence over seeking benefit.

Those who oppose meat-eating might also derive from the above verse that Allah did not count the eating of animal meat as part of their "benefits" (منافع), hence it was mentioned after "benefits" with the phrase وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ (and from them you eat). At the very least, one can infer from this phrasing that the importance of dairy products is significantly greater.

Interestingly, He does not limit Himself to the common and ordinary benefits of these useful livestock, but emphasizes the psychological aspect of utilizing them in the next verse, saying:

"And for you in them is beauty when you bring them in [for the evening] and when you send them out [to pasture]" (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها جَمالٌ حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَ حِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ).

"Turihun" (تريحون) is from the root "Irahah" (اراحه), meaning to return the animals at sunset to their pens and resting places; hence, the place where they rest is called "Murah" (مراح).

"Tasrahun" (تسرحون) is from the root "Suruh" (سروح), meaning to send the livestock out in the morning toward the pasture.

The beautiful sight of the collective movement of sheep and livestock toward the wilderness and pasture, and then their return toward the pen and resting place—which the Quran describes as "Jamal" (beauty)—is not merely a superficial or ceremonial matter. Rather, it expresses a reality deep within society and speaks to the fact that such a society is self-sufficient; it is not poor, destitute, or dependent on others; it produces for itself and consumes what it possesses!

This is, in fact, the beauty of self-reliance and societal self-sufficiency; the beauty of production and securing the products needed by a nation; and in more expressive terms, the beauty of economic independence and the abandonment of all forms of dependency!

Villagers and those raised in rural areas understand this reality better than city dwellers—how observing the coming and going of these useful livestock gives them peace of mind; a peace that arises from the feeling of independence; a peace that results from the effective fulfillment of a social duty.

And it is interesting that in the above verse, He first mentions their return from the wilderness, because upon their return, their udders are full of milk, their bellies are full, and signs of contentment are seen in their faces. For this reason, there is no sign of that greed and haste they have in the morning when moving to the wilderness; they walk calmly and confidently, approaching the resting place, and everyone feels a sense of self-sufficiency from observing the sight of their full udders.

In the next verse, He points to one of the important benefits of these animals, saying:

"And they carry your loads to a land you could not have reached except with great hardship to yourselves" (وَ تَحْمِلُ أَثْقالَكُمْ إِلى‌ بَلَدٍ لَمْ تَكُونُوا بالِغِيهِ إِلَّا بِشِقِّ الْأَنْفُسِ).

This is a sign of Allah's mercy and compassion, that He created these livestock with such strength and power, and made them submissive and surrendered to you, "Indeed, your Lord is Kind and Merciful" (إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

"Shiqq" (شِقّ) is from the root "Mashaqqah" (hardship), but some commentators have suggested it might mean splitting or halving, meaning you could not carry these loads yourselves and reach the destination unless half of your strength was gone—becoming, so to speak, "half-dead." However, the first interpretation seems closer.

In this way, these livestock primarily produce clothing and defensive means for man against heat and cold; secondarily, their dairy products are used; then their meat; then the psychological effects they leave in hearts are considered; and finally, their role in transport.

It is noteworthy that even in our era—the era of machines and mechanical means—in many cases, one must still use these very livestock, as there is no other way.

Then He moves to another group of animals used for human riding, saying: "And [He created] horses, mules, and donkeys for you to ride and as adornment" (وَ الْخَيْلَ وَ الْبِغالَ وَ الْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوها وَ زِينَةً).

It is obvious that "adornment" here is not a ceremonial matter, and this is clear to anyone familiar with the content of Quranic teachings. Rather, it is a type of adornment whose effect appears in social life. To realize this truth, it is enough to compare the sight and state of someone who has traveled a long desert path on foot and reached the destination exhausted, battered, and weak—such that for a time he is capable of nothing and no vitality is seen in him—with the sight of someone who has ridden a swift mount, reached the destination sooner, has not lost his strength, has fully preserved his vitality, and is ready to pursue his goals and utilize his time and energy. Is this not adornment?

At the end of the verse, He points to a more important matter and directs minds toward the various means of transport and mounts that will be placed at man's disposal in the future, which he can use better and more effectively than these animals, saying: "And He creates that which you do not know" (وَ يَخْلُقُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

Although some earlier commentators took this phrase to refer to animals that would be created in the future and tamed by man.

However, as stated in Tafsir al-Maraghi and Tafsir Fi Zilal, understanding the concept of this phrase is simple and easy for us who live in the age of machines and high-speed means of transport.

And if you notice that He used the expression "He creates" (يخلق), the reason is clear, because man's work in these inventions is in truth nothing but assembling and joining parts together; their foundation, which constitutes the raw materials, exists only through Allah's creation. Furthermore, the innovation that man employs in inventing these means is also the product of the talent that Allah has given him.

The Importance of Animal Husbandry and Agriculture

Even today, when mechanical production systems have expanded so much that they overshadow everything, animal husbandry and agriculture still constitute the most important sector of human life production. For we know that the primary basis of nutrition lies in these two parts, and for this reason, reaching the level of self-sufficiency in animal husbandry and agriculture is not only a guarantee of economic independence but political independence is also largely related to it.

Therefore, it is no surprise that all nations of the world strive to expand their agricultural and livestock industries as much as possible and seek help from modern industries for this expansion.

The need for these two is so fundamental that sometimes so-called superpower countries like Russia, to meet their needs in this field, are forced to extend their hand of need toward countries that are exactly at the opposite pole by granting political concessions! For this reason, in Islam and its life-giving teachings, extraordinary importance has been given to the issue of animal husbandry and agriculture, and Muslims have been encouraged toward these two at every opportunity:

In the above verses, we saw with what encouraging tone He speaks of the subject of livestock, enumerating their benefits—including nutritional and clothing benefits—and even describing their movement to the wilderness with a beautiful expression that is very inspiring.

Likewise, regarding the importance of cultivation, various fruits, and agriculture in general, it will be discussed in future verses.

In Islamic narrations, interesting expressions are seen regarding animal husbandry and also regarding agriculture, of which we mention the following hadiths from various sources as examples:

  1. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to one of his relatives: "Why do you not bring 'blessing' into your house?" He asked: "What do you mean by blessing?" He replied: "A milking sheep!" Then he added: "Whoever has in his house a milking sheep, or a ewe, or a cow, they are all blessings." [1]

  2. It is also narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the importance of sheep: "What a good wealth the sheep is." [1]

  3. In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, under the verses being discussed, we read from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام: "The best thing a man acquires in his home for his family is the sheep; whoever has a sheep in his home, the angels sanctify him twice every day." Let there be no mistake: the conditions for raising a sheep in the house may not be available for many, but the main goal is that the production and raising of sheep be carried out continuously in proportion to the number of families (take note).

  4. Regarding the importance of agriculture, it suffices that Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام says: "Whoever finds water and soil and then remains poor, may Allah distance him [from His mercy]." [2] It is obvious that this great saying, just as it is true for an individual, is also true for a nation; a people who have sufficient water and soil yet are still dependent on others are certainly far from Allah's mercy!

  5. It is also narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "It is your duty to engage in sheep-rearing and cultivation, for both return with good and go out with good." [3]

  6. It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "There is no deed more beloved to Allah than agriculture." [1]

Finally, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The farmers are the treasures of mankind; they sow the pure [food] which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has brought forth, and on the Day of Resurrection, they will have the best station among people and the closest position, and they will be called 'the blessed ones'." [2]

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 39. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 14, p. 686 (old edition). In the above hadith, in addition to "Shah" (sheep) and "Baqarah" (cow), "Na'jah" is also mentioned, for which several meanings are given in linguistics: wild cow, mountain sheep, and ewe. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 14, p. 686 (old edition). [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 23, p. 19. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 14, p. 304. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 23, p. 20. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 13, p. 194.

Ayahs

وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ قَصْدُ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمِنْهَا جَآئِرٌ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩)
With Allah rests guidance to the straight path, and some of the paths are devious, and had He wished He would have guided you all. (9)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً ۖ لَّكُم مِّنْهُ شَرَابٌ وَمِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ (١٠)
It is He who sends down water from the sky: from it you get your drink and with it are [sustained] the plants wherein you pasture your herds. (10)
يُنۢبِتُ لَكُم بِهِ ٱلزَّرْعَ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلنَّخِيلَ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبَ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١١)
For you He makes the crops grow with it and olives, date palms, vines, and fruits of all kinds. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect. (11)
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ وَٱلنُّجُومُ مُسَخَّرَٰتٌۢ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (١٢)
He disposed the night and the day for you, and the sun, the moon and the stars are disposed by His command. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (12)
وَمَا ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٣)
And [He disposed for your benefit] whatever He has created for you in the earth of diverse hues—there is indeed a sign in that for a people who take admonition. (13)

Commentary

Everything is Subject to Your Command, O Human

Following the various blessings mentioned in the previous verses, another portion of significant divine favors is presented in the verses under discussion. First, it points to one of the most important spiritual blessings, stating: "It is upon Allah to make available to His servants the straight path and the right way, in which there is no deviation or crookedness" (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ). The word qasd means the straightness of a path; therefore, qasdu al-sabil means the straight path—a way in which there is no deviation or misguidance. [^1] Regarding whether this "straight path" refers to the cosmic (ontological) aspect or the legislative (religious) aspect, commentators have offered various interpretations, but there is no obstacle to it encompassing both dimensions.

To explain: Allah has equipped human beings with various powers and granted them diverse talents to assist them on the path of perfection, which is the goal of creation. In this regard, humans are like plants or various animals, which have been provided with the necessary forces and instincts to reach this goal. The difference, however, is that the human being decides freely and by his own will, whereas animals and plants move toward their goal involuntarily. Furthermore, the ascending arc of human perfection is incomparable to that of other living beings. Thus, from the perspective of creation and nature (the ontological aspect), He has equipped man with intellect, talent, and the necessary powers to traverse this straight path.

On the other hand, from the legislative aspect, God has sent prophets with heavenly revelation, sufficient teachings, and the laws required by humanity to distinguish the right path from the pitfall, encouraging him through various explanations to walk this path and deterring him from deviant routes.

Interestingly, in the above verse, Allah has considered this matter a duty upon Himself, using the phrase عَلَى اللَّهِ (It is incumbent upon Allah). We read similar expressions in other verses of the Quran: إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى—"Indeed, it is upon Us to guide [humanity]" (Al-Layl: 12). If we reflect deeply on the vast scope of the concept عَلَى اللَّهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ and the collection of material and spiritual forces employed in the creation, education, and upbringing of man, we will realize the magnitude of this great blessing, which is superior to all others.

Then, since deviant paths are numerous, He warns humanity, saying: "And of them [the paths] are those that are deviant and stray" (وَ مِنْها جائِرٌ). [^2] Since the blessing of choice, free will, and selection is one of the most important factors in human perfection, He refers to it with a short phrase: "If Allah had willed, He could have guided you all by compulsion" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ), in such a way that you could not take a single step beyond it.

[^1]: Some great commentators, such as Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, have taken qasd to mean "the directer" (the straight one), which is the opposite of ja'ir (the deviant from truth). [^2]: The pronoun in minha refers to sabil (path), and sabil is a metaphorical feminine noun.

However, He did not do this, because compulsory guidance is neither a source of honor nor perfection. Instead, He gave you freedom so that you may walk this path with your own feet and reach the highest pinnacle of perfection.

The above sentence also points to the reality that the fact that a group of humans treads the deviant and stray path should not create the illusion that God has been defeated by them; rather, it is His will and the requirement of His wisdom that humans be free.

In the next verse, the discourse returns to material blessings to stir the sense of gratitude in humans, kindle the fire of love for God in their hearts, and invite them to a deeper recognition of the Giver of these blessings. It says: "He is the One who sent down water from the sky" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً).

A life-giving, pure, transparent water, free from any pollution, "from which you drink" (لَكُمْ مِنْهُ شَرابٌ).

"And from it [grow] the plants and trees in which you pasture your livestock" (وَ مِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ).

Tusimun is derived from the root asamah, meaning to graze animals. We know that animals utilize both the plants of the earth and the leaves of trees; incidentally, the word shajar in the Arabic language has a broad meaning that applies to both trees and plants.

Undoubtedly, the benefits of rainwater are not limited to human consumption and the growth of trees and plants; rather, the washing of the earth, the purification of the air, and the creation of the moisture necessary for the freshness of human skin and ease of breathing are all among the benefits of rain. However, since the two mentioned parts are of greater importance, they have been emphasized.

He continues this same subject: "With this rainwater, He causes crops to grow for you, as well as olives, date palms, and grapes" (يُنْبِتُ لَكُمْ بِهِ الزَّرْعَ وَ الزَّيْتُونَ وَ النَّخِيلَ وَ الْأَعْنابَ).

"And in short, from all kinds of fruits" (وَ مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ).

"Surely in the creation of these colorful and blessed fruits and all these agricultural products, there is a clear sign from God for those who reflect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ).

Zar’ includes any kind of cultivation. Zaytun is both the name of that specific tree and its fruit (though according to some commentators, zaytun is only the name of the tree and zaytunah is the name of its fruit, while in verse 35 of Surah An-Nur, zaytunah is applied to the tree itself).

Nakhil (date palm) is used as both singular and plural, and a’nab is the plural of ’inabah, meaning grapes.

Here, the question arises: why, among all fruits, does the Holy Quran emphasize only these three—olives, dates, and grapes? God willing, you will read the reason for this in the discussion following these verses.

Then, referring to the blessing of the subjugation of various entities of the world for the benefit of man, He says: "Allah has subjected for you the night and the day, and the sun and the moon" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ).

"The stars as well are subjected by His command" (وَ النُّجُومُ مُسَخَّراتٌ بِأَمْرِهِ).

"Surely in these matters are signs of the greatness of God and the majesty of creation for those who exercise their intellect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ).

In the commentary of Surah Ar-Ra'd and Surah Ibrahim, we mentioned that the true meaning of "subjecting" entities for man is that they serve his interests, work for his benefit, and provide him with the possibility of utilization. For this reason, the night, the day, the sun, the moon, and the stars—each of which affects human life in some way and is utilized by him—are "subjected" to him.

This beautiful expression (the subjugation of entities for man by God's command), in addition to clarifying the true personality and greatness of man from the perspective of Islam and the Quran and granting him a dignity worthy of the status of Khalifatullah (the Vicegerent of Allah), reminds him of God's various blessings, stirs a sense of appreciation in him, and draws him closer to God through the marvelous system employed in this subjugation.

For this reason, at the end of the verse, He says: In this subjugation are signs for those who exercise their intellect.

For more information on the secrets of this subjugation, please refer to the detailed explanation we provided under verses 32 and 33 of Surah Ibrahim.

In addition to these, "He also subjected to your command the creatures He created on the earth" (وَ ما ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"Creatures of various and diverse colors" (مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُهُ).

From various types of clothing, foods, pure spouses, and means of comfort to various minerals and useful underground and surface resources and other blessings.

"In these too is a clear sign for a people who take heed" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ).

Key Points

1- Material and Spiritual Blessings

It is interesting that in the above verses, material and spiritual blessings are so intertwined that they cannot be separated from one another. Nevertheless, the tone of the verses regarding spiritual blessings differs from that of material ones.

In no instance does He say it is "upon Allah" to create such-and-such sustenance for you; however, regarding guidance and the straight path, He says: "It is upon Allah to show you the straight path," and to place the necessary powers for traversing this path—both ontologically and legislatively—at your disposal.

Fundamentally, one-dimensional discussions are far from the method of the Quran. Even where it speaks of the creation of trees and fruits and the subjugation of the sun and the moon, it places them in the service of a spiritual goal and says: "These material blessings are also a sign of the greatness of creation and the Creator."

2- Why Only Olives, Date Palms, and Grapes?!

It might seem that if the Quran emphasizes olives, dates, and grapes among all types of fruits in the above verses, it was because of their presence in the environment where the Quran was revealed. However, considering the universal and eternal nature of the Quran and the depth of its expressions, it becomes clear that the matter goes beyond this.

Nutritionists and great scientists who have spent many years of their lives studying the various properties of fruits tell us that there are few fruits as beneficial and effective for the human body in terms of nutrition as these three.

They say that "olive oil" has immense value for producing fuel for the body; its caloric value is very high, and for this reason, it is a source of strength. Those who wish to maintain their health should become fond of this elixir.

Olive oil is a close friend of the human liver and is very effective in relieving kidney ailments, gallstones, renal and hepatic colic, and constipation.

For this reason, it has been highly praised in Islamic narrations. In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام regarding the olive, we read: "It is a good food; it sweetens the breath, removes phlegm, gives clarity and freshness to the face, strengthens the nerves, eliminates illness and pain, and extinguishes the fire of anger." [^1] More importantly, the Quran itself refers to the olive tree as a Shajarah Mubarakah (a blessed tree).

With the advancement of medical science and nutrition, the medicinal importance of "dates" has also been proven. Dates contain calcium, which is the main factor for bone strength, and phosphorus, which is one of the primary elements forming the brain and prevents nervous weakness and fatigue, while also enhancing vision.

Furthermore, potassium is present, the lack of which in the body is considered a true cause of stomach ulcers, and its presence is highly valuable for the muscles and tissues of the body.

It is a well-known saying among nutritionists today that dates prevent cancer. Statistics prepared in this field show that in regions where more dates are consumed, the incidence of cancer is lower. Arabs and desert dwellers who live in nutritional poverty never suffer from cancer because of eating dates; the factor behind this is believed to be the presence of "magnesium."

The abundant sugar found in dates is among the healthiest sugars, which in many cases even those suffering from diabetes can use with ease.

Scientists have discovered thirteen vital substances and five types of vitamins in dates, making them a rich and highly valuable nutritional source. [^2] For this reason, great emphasis on this food item is seen in Islamic narrations. It is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: "Eat dates, for in them is healing from diseases."

It is also narrated that many times the food of Ali عليه السلام consisted of bread and dates.

In another narration, we read: "A house in which there are no dates, its inhabitants are truly hungry!" [^3] In the verses of Surah Maryam, it will also be mentioned that Allah provided Maryam with fresh dates in that desert where there were no means when she gave birth to Jesus. This is an indication that one of the best foods for a mother who has just given birth is fresh dates; even in the narrations under that verse, we read that the best medicine for such women is this very fruit. ^4

As for "grapes," according to nutritionists, they contain so many effective factors that it can be said they are a natural pharmacy. Moreover, in terms of properties, grapes are very close to mother's milk, meaning they are considered a complete food. Grapes produce twice as much heat in the body as meat, and additionally, they are antitoxic. They have a proven therapeutic effect for purifying the blood and treating rheumatism, gout, and excess urea in the blood. Grapes cleanse the stomach and intestines, create cheerfulness, remove sorrow, strengthen the nerves, and the various vitamins present in them grant man power and ability.

In addition to being a highly valuable food, grapes have significant germ-killing power and are even an effective factor in the fight against cancer. [^5] For this reason, in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The best of your food is bread, and the best of your fruit is grapes." [^6]

If we were to bring here all that nutritionists have said regarding these fruits and the numerous narrations cited in Islamic sources in this regard, we would certainly deviate from the method of commentary. The purpose was to clarify that the Quran's emphasis on these three types of fruit is not without reason, and perhaps at that time, a significant part of it was hidden from the people.

3- Reflection, Intellect, Remembrance

In the above verses, after stating three categories of divine blessings, the people are invited to think. However, in one instance it says: "In these are signs for a people who reflect (yatafakkarun)"; in another, it says: "for a people who exercise intellect (ya'qilun)"; and in the third, "for a people who take heed (yadhakkarun)."

This variation in expression is certainly not for the sake of literary variety; rather, based on what we know of the Quran's method, each refers to a specific point. Perhaps the reason for this difference is that regarding the colors of the blessings present on earth, the matter is so clear that mere "remembrance" and "taking heed" are sufficient. But regarding cultivation, olives, date palms, grapes, and fruits in general, a bit more "reflection" is required to become acquainted with their nutritional and medicinal properties; hence the term tafakkur (reflection) is used.

However, regarding the subjugation of the sun, moon, and stars, and the secrets of night and day, even more thought is required; therefore, the term ta'aqqul (exercising intellect) is used, which indicates a higher level of thinking.

In any case, the Quran's address everywhere is to the thinkers, the reflectors, and the possessors of intellect and mind. Given that the Quran emerged from an environment where nothing but ignorance reigned, the greatness of these expressions becomes more apparent. It is also a crushing response to those who, because of some superstitious religions, have drawn a red line through all true religions, claiming that religion is a means of sedation and the disabling of thought, and that belief in God is the product of human ignorance!

Such verses of the Quran are spread across almost all chapters. They clearly state that true religion is born of thought, reflection, and intellect, and Islam everywhere deals with thinkers, intellectuals, and the "people of understanding" (ulul-albab), not with the ignorant, the superstitious, or the illogical pseudo-intellectuals.

[^1]: Islam, the Physician Without Medicine. [^2]: The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 7, p. 65. Interestingly, the seventh volume of this book from beginning to end speaks about the nutritional and medicinal properties of dates and grapes, the study of which acquaints one with the importance of these two foods. [^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 125.

[^5]: The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 7, pp. 101 and 142. [^6]: Islam, the Physician Without Medicine.

Ayahs

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنْهُ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ مَوَاخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٤)
It is He who disposed the sea [for your benefit] that you may eat from it fresh meat, and obtain from it ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships plowing through it, that you may seek His bounty and that you may give thanks. (14)
وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا وَسُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥)
He cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and [made] streams and ways, so that you may be guided (15)
وَعَلَـٰمَـٰتٍ ۚ وَبِٱلنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (١٦)
—and the landmarks [as well]—and by the stars they are guided. (16)
أَفَمَن يَخْلُقُ كَمَن لَّا يَخْلُقُ ۗ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (١٧)
Is He who creates like one who does not create? Will you not then take admonition? (17)
وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٨)
If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful, (18)

Commentary

The Blessing of Mountains, Seas, and Stars

These verses turn toward another important category of Allah’s endless blessings upon mankind, beginning first with the seas, which are a vital source of life, stating:

"And He is the One who subjected the sea for you so that it may serve you" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ).

We know that seas constitute the major portion of the Earth's surface, and we also know that the first spark of life appeared in the seas. Even now, the sea remains a critical source for the continued life of humans and all living creatures on Earth. Placing it at the service of mankind is one of Allah’s great blessings.

The verse then points to three benefits of the seas, stating:

"So that you may eat fresh meat from it" (لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا).

Meat for which you did not labor to raise; rather, the hand of Allah’s power alone nurtured it in the heart of the oceans and placed it freely at your disposal.

The emphasis on the "tenderness and freshness" (طَرِيًّا) of this meat—considering that in that era in one way, and in our era in another, preserved or stale meats were and are abundant—makes the significance of this blessing and the importance of consuming fresh meat even clearer.

Despite all the progress in human material civilization, the sea still constitutes one of the most important sources of human nutrition. Every year, hundreds of thousands of tons of fresh meat, nurtured by the hand of the Lord’s grace for mankind, are harvested from the sea.

Therefore, now that the Earth's population is increasing and some feel, in their preliminary studies, that the threat of food shortage looms over the world's future, the thoughts of scientists have turned toward the seas. They look to them with hope, seeking to alleviate this shortage significantly through the farming and breeding of various fish species.

This is one aspect; on the other hand, regulations have been established to prevent the pollution of seawater and the extinction of fish species. From all this, the significance of the above sentence, revealed in the Quran fourteen centuries ago, becomes even more evident.

Another benefit is the decorative materials extracted from the seas; thus, it adds: "And that you may extract from it ornaments to wear" (وَ تَسْتَخْرِجُوا مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها).

Man is not like the cattle, devoid of taste; rather, one of the four well-known dimensions of the human soul is the "sense of beauty," which is the source of poetry, authentic art, and the like.

Undoubtedly, this dimension of the human soul plays an effective role in human life; therefore, it must be satisfied in a correct and healthy manner—away from any excess, negligence, extravagance, or waste.

Those who are drowned in the worship of luxury and various ornaments are just as misguided as those rigid individuals who oppose any form of adornment. One side is in excess, leading to the destruction of resources, the creation of class divides, and the killing of spirituality, while the other is in deficiency, causing lethargy and stagnation.

For this reason, in Islam, the use of adornment in a reasonable manner free from extravagance—such as using good clothes, various perfumes, certain precious stones, and the like—is recommended, especially for women, whose psychological need for adornment is greater. However, we emphasize again that it must be free from extravagance and waste.

Finally, it mentions the third blessing of the sea: the movement of ships as an important means for transporting humans and their needs, stating: "And you see the ships plowing through the waters" (وَ تَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ).

How spectacular is the scene that appears before the passengers of a ship as it moves across the surface of the oceans! "Allah gave you this blessing so that you may benefit from it and seek His bounty through the use of ships in your trade" (وَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ) [1]. Given all these blessings, it should awaken a sense of gratitude in you: "So that perhaps you may be grateful" (وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ).

"Fulk" (فلك) (ship) is used as both a singular and a plural noun.

"Mawakher" (مواخر) is the plural of "Makherah" (ماخرة), derived from "Makhr" (مخر) (rhyming with Fakhr), meaning to cleave the water to the left and right. It is also used to describe the sound of strong winds blowing.

[1]: The phrase "ولِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ" is mentioned with the conjunction "and" (waw), so it must have an antecedent which is normally implied. The implied meaning is: "So that you may benefit from them and so that you may seek His bounty": You see the ships cleaving the waters with their bows so that you may derive various benefits from them and utilize them for trade.

Since ships cleave the water with their bows as they move, they are called "Makher" or "Makherah."

Fundamentally, who placed this property in the material from which the ship is built so that it stays atop the water? If everything were heavier than water and the specific pressure of water did not exist, could we ever move across the vast surface of the oceans?

Furthermore, who set the steady winds in motion across the oceans? Or who created such power in steam so that we might move motorized ships across the oceans with its force? Is not each of these a great blessing?

Considering that sea routes are much wider, less expensive, and more ready than land roads, and noting that giant ships—sometimes as large as a city—constitute the greatest means of human transport, the magnitude of the blessing of the seas for navigation becomes clearer.

After explaining the blessing of the seas, He turns to the hard and heavy mountains and says: "And He cast into the earth firm, anchored mountains lest it should shake with you, to provide you with tranquility" (وَ أَلْقى‌ فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ) [1]. As we have said previously, mountains are interconnected at their roots and encompass the Earth like a coat of armor. This significantly prevents the severe tremors of the Earth that could occur at any moment due to the pressure of internal gases.

[1]: "أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ" is implied as "لئلا تميد بكم" (lest it shake with you) or "كراهة ان تميد بكم" (to prevent it from shaking with you).

Beyond this, the specific placement of mountains increases the resistance of the Earth's crust against the moon's gravity (tides) and reduces its effect to a minimum.

Thirdly, they reduce the power of severe storms and the constant movement of winds over the Earth's crust; for if there were no mountains, the flat surface of the Earth would be constantly exposed to gales, and no tranquility could be imagined.

And since mountains are one of the primary reservoirs of water (whether in the form of snow and ice or internal waters), He immediately mentions the blessing of the existence of rivers, saying: "And [He placed] rivers for you" (وَ أَنْهاراً).

And since the existence of mountains might create the illusion that they separate parts of the Earth from one another and block the paths, He adds: "And paths for you so that you may be guided" (وَ سُبُلًا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ) [1]. It is noteworthy that within the world's largest mountain ranges, there are often gaps through which man can find his way, and it rarely happens that these mountains completely isolate sections of the Earth from each other.

And since a path without signs, marks, and a guide does not lead a person to their destination, after mentioning the blessing of the "path," He refers to these "signs," saying: "And landmarks" (وَ عَلاماتٍ).

These landmarks are of many types: the shape of mountains, valleys, and their gaps;

[1]: In any case, the above verse is one of the scientific miracles of the Holy Quran which was not discovered by humans at least in that day. Read more details in this regard in the section on the scientific miracles of the Quran in our book "The Quran and the Last Prophet."

the ups and downs of different pieces of land, the color of soils and mountains, and even the way the winds blow—each is a sign for finding paths.

To understand to what extent these signs help travelers and save them from wandering and getting lost, it is enough to consider the condition of some uniform deserts that exist in certain regions, where crossing them is extremely difficult and dangerous. How many people entered them and never returned! Think, if the entire Earth were so uniform—mountains of the same size, plains of one color, valleys looking alike—could humans easily find their way?!

But since at certain times in the deserts, in dark nights, or when man travels at night in the heart of the oceans, there is no trace of these landmarks, Allah has sent celestial signs to help. If there is no sign on Earth, they may use the signs of the sky and not go astray! Thus, He adds: "And by the stars, they are guided" (وَ بِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ).

Of course, this is only one of the benefits of the stars; they have many benefits, but if this were the only one, it would still be significant. Especially before the compass was invented and ships could find their way toward their destination according to maps, movement on the seas was impossible without using the stars. For this reason, on nights when clouds covered the sky, ships would stop moving, and if they continued their path, the danger of death threatened them.

Of course, we know that the stars that appear to us to move in the sky are no more than five (the planets visible to the naked eye), while the rest of the stars maintain their relative positions.

It is as if they are pearls sewn onto a dark-colored fabric; this fabric is pulled from one side of the horizon to the other. In other words, the movement of the "fixed stars" is a collective, group movement, while the movement of the planets is individual, such that their distances from the other stars change.

In addition, the fixed stars form specific shapes known as "constellations," and knowing them is very effective for finding the four cardinal directions (North, South, East, and West).

After stating all those great blessings and the hidden graces of the Lord, He calls upon the human conscience to judge, saying: "Is He who creates like one who does not create? Will you not then take heed?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَخْلُقُ كَمَنْ لا يَخْلُقُ أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ).

This is an effective educational method that the Quran uses in many instances: posing matters in the form of a question and leaving the answer to awakened consciences. It calls upon the spontaneous sense of the people so that the answer wells up from within their own souls, causing them to love it like their own child and accept it.

Fundamentally, it has been psychologically proven that for correct education and training, maximum effort should be made so that the other party considers the information as their own—meaning they feel it has sprung from within themselves, not something imposed from the outside—so that they accept it with their whole being and defend it.

It is also necessary to repeat this point: the polytheists who prostrated before various idols never believed that the idol was the Creator; rather, they considered creation to belong exclusively to "Allah." It is here that the Quran says: Should one prostrate before the Creator of all these blessings, or before beings that are themselves nothing more than insignificant creatures and have never created anything?

Finally, so that no one imagines that Allah’s blessings are limited to these, He says: "And if you were to count the blessings of Allah, you could not enumerate them" (وَ إِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ لا تُحْصُوها).

Your entire existence from head to toe is drowned in His blessings. In every breath that goes in and comes out, there are not just two blessings but thousands, and for every blessing, a thanks is due. Every minute that passes of our lives, our life and health are indebted to the activity of millions of living organisms inside our bodies and millions of animate and inanimate beings outside our bodies, without whose activity the continuation of life for even a moment would be impossible.

Fundamentally, we are not aware of the existence of all blessings, and as the scope of human science and knowledge expands, new horizons of these blessings will open to us—horizons whose boundaries remain invisible. In such a state, do we have the power to enumerate Allah’s blessings?!

Now this question arises: how then can we fulfill the right of gratitude to Him? In this state, are we not among the ungrateful?

The Quran provides the answer to this question in the final phrase of this verse, saying: "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Yes, Allah is more Merciful and Generous than to take you to task or punish you for your inability to thank Him for His blessings. As long as you know that you are drowned in His blessings from head to toe, and that you are unable to fulfill the right of gratitude and bring your excuse of shortcoming to His threshold, you have performed the ultimate gratitude to Him.

Otherwise, the right of His Lordship, No one is capable of fulfilling!

However, all this does not prevent us from attempting to enumerate His blessings to the extent of our ability. This attention increases the level of our knowledge, worldview, and monotheistic understanding of the universe, fans the flame of Allah’s love in the depths of our hearts, and stimulates the sense of gratitude within us. For this reason, the leaders and greats of the religion, in their words and even in their prayers and supplications, would count portions of His endless blessings to serve as a lesson for others.

(We have also discussed the gratitude for blessings and man's inability to enumerate the Lord's blessings under verse 34 of Surah Ibrahim).

Key Points The Path, the Sign, the Leader

Although the above verses speak of land paths and routes as a divine blessing—since paths are the most important means of communication and the progress of human civilization, and for this reason, in development programs, the creation of suitable paths is prioritized, as no developmental activity is possible without them.

Nevertheless, the Quranic expression in this regard can also be a model for the "spiritual life" of humans. To reach any sacred goal, the selection of the correct path is necessary above all else. Furthermore, the existence of signs and landmarks is of vital importance, because similar paths are many, and losing the main path among them is entirely possible. This is where the role of "signs" becomes clear.

Especially the believers, who are described in the verses of the Quran as "Al-Mutawassimin" (the discerning/vigilant), must carefully watch these signs.

They should recognize schools of thought, traditions, calls, and even individuals by their signs, and distinguish truth from falsehood in the light of its signs.

The importance of the issue of the "Leader" also needs no explanation.

It is noteworthy that many narrations have been transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام in which "Najm" (star) is interpreted as the Prophet and "landmarks" as the Imams. In some, both the star and the landmarks are interpreted as the Imams and the leaders of the path of truth. As an example, a few hadiths are mentioned:

  1. In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "The star is the Messenger of Allah and the landmarks are the Imams عليه السلام." [1] The exact same meaning has been narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام.

  2. In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir, we read that in interpreting the above verse he said: "We are the star." [2]

  3. Also, in another hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is narrated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "You are the star of Bani Hashim." [3] And in another narration, he said: "You are one of the landmarks." [4]

All of these refer to the spiritual interpretation of the above verses.

(1, 2, 3, and 4) Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 45.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ (١٩)
and Allah knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose. (19)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (٢٠)
Those whom they invoke besides Allah do not create anything and are themselves created. (20)
أَمْوَٰتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْيَآءٍ ۖ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٢١)
They are dead and lifeless, and are not aware when they will be resurrected. (21)
إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ قُلُوبُهُم مُّنكِرَةٌ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٢٢)
Your God is the One God. Those who do not believe in the Hereafter, their hearts are in denial [of the truth], and they are arrogant. (22)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ (٢٣)
Undoubtedly, Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose. Indeed, He does not like the arrogant. (23)

Commentary

Dead and Senseless Deities!

Since the previous verses pointed to two of the most important attributes of Allah, neither of which exists in idols and fabricated deities (the creation of beings and the granting of blessings), the first of the verses under discussion points to the third attribute of the "True Deity," which is knowledge and wisdom. It says: "And Allah knows what you conceal and what you reveal" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَ ما تُعْلِنُونَ).

So why do you follow idols that have not the slightest share in the creation of the world, have not granted you the smallest blessing, and are unaware of your inner secrets and outward actions? What kind of deities are these that lack all necessary and valuable attributes?!

Once again, He emphasizes the issue of creation, but goes further than the similar verse we had previously and says: "And those they invoke besides Allah create nothing, but are themselves created" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَ هُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ). Until now, the discussion was that they are not creators and for this reason are not worthy of worship; now He says they are themselves created, needy, and dependent. In such a state, how can they be a support for humans and resolve the difficulties of a group? What a foolish judgment this is!

Beyond this, "They are dead, not alive, and they perceive not" (أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ).

Should not a deity at least be a living being and be aware of the needs or the worship of its worshippers? Therefore, the fourth attribute of the True Deity, namely "Life," is also completely absent in them.

Then He adds: "these idols do not even know when their worshippers will be resurrected" (وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ).

If reward and punishment were in their hands, they should at least be aware of the "Resurrection of their servants." With such ignorance, how are they worthy of worship?

And this is the fifth attribute that the True Deity must possess and they lack [1]. Perhaps we have said many times before that idols and idol-worship in the logic of the Quran have a broad meaning, much broader than fabricated gods made of stone, wood, and metals.

Any being or anyone whom we make our support against Allah and consider our destiny to be in their hands is considered our idol. For this reason, all that has appeared in the above verses includes those who today are outwardly monotheists but have lost the independence of a true believer and have chosen a support for themselves from among weak servants

[1]: In the commentary of the verse "أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ," commentators have given other possibilities besides what we mentioned above, including that it means the idols do not know when they themselves will be resurrected. Then they have cited some verses that say: "The polytheists and their idols will both be in Hell" (Surah Al-Anbiya, 98). However, it is clear that if this were the meaning, it would not have a significant connection with the content of the verses before and after. Therefore, the correct interpretation is the one we have provided above (reflect carefully).

and live in a state of dependency.

Those who imagine that the great powers of the world can be a support for their dark days—even though these powers are hellish and estranged from God—they too are practically idol-worshippers and polytheists. It must be said to them: Have these deities of yours created anything? Are they the source of any blessing? Are they aware of your inner and outer secrets? And do they know when you will be resurrected so they may reward or punish you?

Then why do you worship them like idols?

Following these vivid and clear arguments for the negation of the idols' eligibility, the conclusion is drawn: Therefore, "Your God is one God" (إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ). And since faith in the Origin (Allah) and the Return (Resurrection) are everywhere paired and are necessary correlates of one another, He immediately adds: "But those who do not believe in the Hereafter (and naturally do not have a correct faith in the Origin either), their hearts deny the truth and they are arrogant" (فَالَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مُنْكِرَةٌ وَ هُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ) [1]. Otherwise, the proofs of Monotheism are manifest for those who are seekers of truth and humble before reality, as are the proofs of the Resurrection. But the habit of arrogance and lack of submission to the truth causes them to constantly adopt a state of denial and negation, even denying sensory realities, to the point that this becomes a state and a second nature within them. In such a state, no word of truth, proof, or logic can penetrate them.

[1]: The letter "fa" (ف) in "فَالَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ" is, as we know, for derivation (tafri'); meaning the denial of the Day of Judgment and Resurrection, which stems from arrogance, is because of the denial of the Origin.

Are the vivid proofs that passed in the previous verses regarding the unsuitability of idols for worship not sufficient for any sentient being to testify that an idol is not worthy of worship?

Yet we see again that this group, with utter amazement, refused to accept this obvious truth.

The last verse under discussion once again emphasizes Allah's knowledge of the unseen and the seen, the hidden and the manifest, saying: "Assuredly, Allah knows what they conceal and what they reveal" (لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَ ما تُعْلِنُونَ). This sentence is, in fact, a threat against the disbelievers and enemies of truth—that Allah is never heedless of their state; He knows not only their outward but also their inward, and He will hold them to account in due time.

They are arrogant, and "He does not love the arrogant" (إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ).

For arrogance against the truth is the first sign of estrangement from Allah.

The phrase "La Jarama" (لا جرم) is a compound of "La" and "Jaram," which is usually used for emphasis and means "certainly" or "assuredly." Sometimes it is used to mean "inevitably" and even sometimes as an "oath," such as when we say "La jarama la-af'alanna" (I swear I will do this).

As for how these meanings are derived from "La Jarama," it is because "Jaram" originally means picking or cutting fruit from a tree. When "La" is placed before it, the concept becomes that nothing can cut off this matter or prevent it. In this way, the meaning of "certainly," "inevitably," and sometimes "oath" is derived from it.

Key Points Who are the Arrogant?!

In several verses of the Quran, "arrogance" (istikbar) is used as a specific attribute of the disbelievers, and from all of them, it is understood that it refers to arrogance in refusing to accept the truth.

In Surah Nuh, verse 7, we read: وَ إِنِّي كُلَّما دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوا أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَ استَغْشَوْا ثِيابَهُمْ وَ أَصَرُّوا وَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا اسْتِكْباراً:

"And indeed, every time I invited them that You might forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance."

And in Surah Al-Munafiqun, verse 5, we read: وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ وَ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَ هُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ: "And when it is said to them, 'Come, the Messenger of Allah will ask forgiveness for you,' they turn their heads away and you see them evading while they are arrogant."

And in Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 8, we read about this same group: يَسْمَعُ آياتِ اللَّهِ تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها: "He hears the verses of Allah recited to him, then persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them." In reality, the worst form of arrogance is the arrogance of refusing to accept the truth, because it closes all paths of guidance to a person, and they remain in misery, sin, and faithlessness for their entire life.

Ali عليه السلام in the Nahj al-Balagha, in the "Qasi'ah" sermon, explicitly identifies Satan as the "Salaf al-Mustakbirin" (the predecessor and leader of the arrogant), because he took the first step in opposing the truth and refusing to submit to the reality that Adam was more perfect than him.

Thus, all those who refuse to accept the truth, whether they are financially poor or wealthy, are arrogant. However, it cannot be denied that many times great financial means cause a person to refrain from accepting the truth.

In Rawdat al-Kafi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Whoever thinks he has a preference over another is among the arrogant." The narrator of the hadith says: I asked the Imam, "Is there any harm if a person sees someone committing a sin and considers himself, who does not commit that sin, to have a preference over him?"

The Imam replied: "Far from it, far from it! Perhaps he will be forgiven for what he has done, while you are held for accounting. Have you not read the story of the sorcerers of Moses عليه السلام?" [1]

(Who one day, for the sake of Pharaoh's reward and proximity to his court, stood up against the Arch-Prophet of the Lord, but upon seeing the face of truth, they suddenly changed their path to the point that they resisted even Pharaoh's threat of death, and Allah included them in His forgiveness and mercy!).

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 48.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُم مَّاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۙ قَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٤)
When they are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They say, ‘Myths of the ancients,’ (24)
لِيَحْمِلُوٓا۟ أَوْزَارَهُمْ كَامِلَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ وَمِنْ أَوْزَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ (٢٥)
[with the result] that they will bear the full weight of their own burden on the Day of Resurrection, along with part of the burden of those whom they mislead without any knowledge. Behold! Evil is what they bear! (25)
قَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى ٱللَّهُ بُنْيَـٰنَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَوَاعِدِ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّقْفُ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٦)
Those who were before them [had also] schemed. Then Allah came at their edifice from the foundations and the roof fell down upon them from above and the punishment overtook them whence they were not aware. (26)
ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُخْزِيهِمْ وَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تُشَـٰٓقُّونَ فِيهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ إِنَّ ٱلْخِزْىَ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَٱلسُّوٓءَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٧)
Then, on the Day of Resurrection, He will disgrace them, and say, ‘Where are My “partners” for whose sake you used to defy [Allah]?’ Those who were given knowledge will say, ‘Indeed today disgrace and distress pursue the faithless.’ (27)
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ فَأَلْقَوُا۟ ٱلسَّلَمَ مَا كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِن سُوٓءٍۭ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٨)
—Those whom the angels take away while they were wronging themselves. Thereat they submit: ‘We were not doing any evil!’ ‘Yes, [the angels say to them,] indeed Allah knows best what you used to do! (28)
فَٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٢٩)
Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the [final] abode of the arrogant.’ (29)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, we read that according to some narrations, the first verse was revealed concerning the "partitioners" (muqtasimin)—those who practiced discrimination—who were discussed previously. They were sixteen individuals divided into four groups. Each group of four stood at one of the entry roads to Mecca during the Hajj season to prejudice the minds of people against the Quran and Islam before they entered. They would claim that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) had not brought a new religion, but rather the same lying fables of the ancients.

Commentary

Those Who Bear the Burden of Others' Sins

The preceding verses spoke of the arrogant (mustakbirun) who would never submit to the Truth, but rather sought ways to shirk its responsibility.

These verses reflect the perennial logic of this faithless group, stating: "And when it is said to them, 'What has your Lord sent down?' they say, 'These are not divine revelations; they are but the fables of the ancients'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

With this insidious expression, they intended to convey two points: first, that their level of thought is far superior to such matters, dismissing them as mere fables created to entertain the common folk; and second, that this is nothing new, as it is not the first time a man has uttered such words—claiming that even Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) has shown no personal initiative but is merely repeating the myths of those who came before.

"Asatir" (اساطير) is the plural of "usturah" (اسطوره) [1], referring to superstitious and false tales and stories. This word is quoted nine times in the Quran from the tongues of faithless disbelievers against the Prophets, who usually resorted to this excuse to justify their opposition to the call of divine leaders. Remarkably, they always described the word "asatir" with "al-awwalin" (the ancients) to prove that these things were not novel, sometimes even saying, "This is nothing significant; if we wished, we could produce the like of it" (Surah Al-Anfal, Verse 31).

It is noteworthy that the arrogant of today also frequently resort to such insidious tactics to escape the Truth and mislead others. They have even given it a so-called scientific form in sociology books, calling religion the product of human ignorance and religious interpretations "myths"! However, if we penetrate deep into their thoughts, we see the matter is different. They never wage war against superstitious and fabricated religions; in fact, they are often the agents of their creation or propagation. Their opposition is solely toward true religions that awaken human minds, shatter the chains of colonialism, and stand as a barrier in the path of the arrogant and imperialists.

They see that accepting religious teachings—which propose principles of justice and equity and fight against discrimination, tyranny, and all forms of autocracy—is contrary to their schemes.

They see that accepting the moral commands of religion is incompatible with the rebellious whims and the "freedoms" they desire. The sum of these factors causes them to seek the removal of this barrier from their path. They must certainly fabricate an answer for the people, and what answer is better than labeling these truths as myths and false fables?

What unfortunately grants them some success in labeling religion this way are the superstitions that ignorant and uninformed individuals sometimes fabricate and mold in the name of religion. It is necessary for all followers of true religion to vigorously combat such superstitions and disarm the enemies. They must write and declare everywhere that such superstitions have no connection to true religions and that the enemy must not use them as a pretext. Otherwise, the teachings of the Prophets—both in the realm of religious beliefs and practical matters—are so harmonious with reason and logic that there is no room for such accusations.

The next verse describes the consequence of the actions of these blind-hearted individuals: "That they may bear their own burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection, and some of the burdens of those whom they misled without knowledge" (لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَ مِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ). "Behold, evil is that which they bear!" (أَلا ساءَ ما يَزِرُونَ).

This is because their words sometimes cause the deviation of thousands. How difficult it is for a person to bear both the weight of his own sins and the weight of the sins of thousands of others! Whenever their misleading words remain and become a source of deviation for future generations, that burden also weighs heavily upon them. The phrase "Liyahmilu" (they must bear) is in the form of a command, but its meaning is to express the result and consequence of the action—much like telling someone, "Now that you have committed this wrong, you must bear its consequence and taste its bitterness." (Some commentators also suggest that the "Lam" in liyahmilu is the "Lam of consequence").

"Awzar" (اوزار) is the plural of "wizr" (وزر), meaning a heavy burden and also meaning sin. A minister is called a "wazir" because he carries a heavy responsibility on his shoulders.
