Here a question arises: How does the Quran say they bear "part of the burden" of those they misled, rather than the entire burden, while we read in narrations that if someone establishes an evil tradition, he bears all the sins of those who acted upon it?

Some commentators have answered this by saying that "misled followers" have two types of sins: sins committed by following their leaders, and sins they have independently. What weighs on the leaders is the first type.

Others have argued that "min" (from/part of) in the verse is not for partitioning, but rather to state that the sins of the followers are upon the leaders.

However, another interpretation seems more appealing than all of these: misled followers fall into two categories. Sometimes they follow these deviant leaders knowingly, as we have seen many times throughout history. In this case, the factor of sin is both the leaders' command and their own decision; here, a share of the responsibility for their sins is on the leaders (without anything being diminished from the followers' own sins).

But sometimes, followers are deceived against their will and fall under the influence of the temptations of misleading leaders, as seen among the common folk in many societies. They might even follow such programs with the intention of "seeking proximity to Allah." In this case, the entire burden of their sins is on the misleading leaders, and the followers themselves—if they were not negligent in seeking the truth—bear no responsibility. However, the first group, who followed with knowledge and awareness, will certainly not have a grain of their sin reduced, even though their leaders bear a share of the responsibility.

It is also necessary to note that the expression "without knowledge" (bi-ghayri 'ilm) does not imply that the followers of these misleaders had no awareness whatsoever of their leaders' status and were totally deceived so as to have no personal responsibility. Rather, this expression is like saying that ignorant and foolish people fall quickly into the traps of deceivers, while wise people do so very slowly.

Therefore, the Quran in other verses does not exonerate these followers and assigns a share of responsibility to them as well. As we read in Surah Ghafir (Al-Mu'min), verses 47 and 48: "And when they will dispute in the Fire, the weak will say to those who were arrogant, 'Indeed, we were your followers, so can you take from us a portion of the Fire?' Those who were arrogant will say, 'Indeed, all of us are in it. Indeed, Allah has judged between the servants!'"

The next verse points out that this is not the first time the arrogant have accused divine leaders and considered heavenly revelation as the fables of the ancients; rather, "Those before them also plotted, but Allah came at their structure from the foundations and the roof fell upon them from above!" (قَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى اللَّهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ مِنَ الْقَواعِدِ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ السَّقْفُ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ). "And the punishment came to them from where they did not perceive" (وَ أَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

Although some commentators have interpreted the above verse as referring to the story of Nimrod and the building he supposedly built to ascend to heaven and fight the God of heaven!! and others to the story of Nebuchadnezzar, it is certain that the concept of the verse is general and encompasses the plots of all the arrogant and misleading leaders.

Interestingly, the Quran says: Allah does not take superficial action to disrupt the plots of these arrogant ones; rather, He goes to the root and foundation of their work, dismantling their organizations from the base and bringing the roofs down upon them.

Yes, such is the divine punishment for such individuals.

Certainly, the uprooting of the building and the falling of the roof may refer to their physical buildings and roofs that were crushed by earthquakes and lightning and fell upon them. It may also be a metaphor for their organizations and structures that were uprooted by Allah's command and destroyed. Furthermore, there is no obstacle to the verse referring to both meanings.

It is also noteworthy that after mentioning the word "roof" (saqf), the Quran uses the expression "from above them" (min fawqihim), even though a roof is obviously always above. This may be for emphasis, and also to point out that sometimes a roof might fall while the owner of the house is not there; but in the case of these criminals, the roof fell while they were beneath it and they were destroyed. How vividly and clearly today's and past history shows scenes of this divine punishment! There were powerful tyrants who considered their apparatus so firm that they planned and designed it not just for themselves but for the future of their children, and apparently had provided all the preliminaries for maintaining power and the stability of their system. But suddenly, from where they never imagined, divine punishment came to them and collapsed the roof of their palace upon them. They were so destroyed or scattered as if they had never been on the face of the earth!

What has been mentioned is their punishment in this world, but their retribution does not end here; rather, "Then on the Day of Resurrection He will disgrace them" (ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يُخْزِيهِمْ).

And He will question them, saying: "Where are My 'partners' for whom you used to oppose [the truth] and for whose sake you would engage in dispute and even enmity with others?" (وَ يَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تُشاقُّونَ فِيهِمْ) [1]. Here, they certainly have no answer to this question, but "Those who were given knowledge will say: 'Indeed, disgrace this Day and evil are upon the disbelievers'" (قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ إِنَّ الْخِزْيَ الْيَوْمَ وَ السُّوءَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ).

From this, it becomes clear that the speakers on the Day of Resurrection are the scholars and the learned, for in that great presence, words must be spoken that contain no falsehood, and this is not possible for anyone except the faithful scholars.

And if we see in some narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that this is interpreted as the Infallible Imams, it is because they are the most perfect and complete examples of faithful scholars [2].

It is also necessary to mention that the purpose of this question and answer exchanged between the polytheists and the scholars is not to discover a hidden or concealed matter; rather, it is itself a type of psychological punishment and retribution. Especially since the informed believers were always subject to the blame and reproach of these proud polytheists in this world, and there, these proud ones must receive their punishment through this very path and be subjected to blame and taunting in a place where there is no way for denial or salvation for them.

The next verse provides a description of the disbelievers, with whom the previous verse ended, using an expression that is a shaking lesson to awaken the heedless and the unaware. It says: "Those whom the angels take in death while they were wronging themselves" (الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

For whatever oppression or wrong a human does, it primarily returns to himself, and he destroys his own house before the houses of others. This is because the first step of oppression is the first step in destroying one's own inner virtues and noble qualities. Furthermore, if the foundation of oppression is established in any society, given the social bonds, it eventually circles back and returns to the house of the oppressor.

However, when they see themselves on the threshold of death and the veils of pride and heedlessness are removed from before their eyes, "they will immediately offer submission, saying: 'We were not doing any evil!'" (فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ ما كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِنْ سُوءٍ).

Why do they deny doing any evil? Are they lying because lying has become an inherent trait through repetition? Or do they mean to say, "We know we performed these acts, but we were mistaken and had no evil intent"? It could be both.

But immediately it is said to them: You are lying; you performed many ugly deeds. "Yes! Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what you used to do" (بَلى‌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Therefore, there is no place for denial or disavowal!

"Now that it is so, enter the gates of Hell, to abide eternally therein" (فَادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها). "And wretched is the residence of the arrogant" (فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ).

Key Points

1- The Good and Evil Tradition

To perform an action, many preliminaries are certainly required, and among these, the role of leaders, guides, or tempters is a significant one. Likewise, the role of good or ugly and shameful traditions that provide the intellectual and social background for such actions cannot be ignored; in fact, sometimes the role of leaders or tradition-setters surpasses all other factors.

Therefore, there is no reason why they should not be partners in the crime or partners in the good deeds.

Based on this logic, the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations place great emphasis on the issue of "directing toward good and evil" or "establishing a tradition, whether good or bad."

In the above verses, we read that the misleading and seductive arrogant ones bear both the burden of their own sins and a share of the burden of their followers' sins (without the responsibility of the followers being diminished).

This subject is of such importance that, according to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"The one who directs toward good is like the one who performs it" [1]. In a hadith narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding the above verses, we read:

"Whoever calls to guidance and is followed, he shall have a reward like the rewards of those who followed him, without their rewards being diminished in the least. And whoever calls to misguidance and is followed, upon him is a burden like the burdens of those who followed him, without their burdens being diminished in the least!" [2]. And from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) it is narrated:

"Whoever establishes a tradition of justice and it is followed, he shall have the reward of those who act upon it, without their rewards being diminished in the least. And whoever establishes a tradition of tyranny and it is followed, upon him is the like of the burden of those who act upon it, without their burdens being diminished in the least" [3]. Numerous other narrations with the same theme have been narrated from the Infallible leaders, which Sheikh Hurr al-Amili (may he rest in peace) collected in the eleventh volume of Wasa'il al-Shia, "The Book of Enjoining Good and Forbidding Evil," sixteenth chapter.

In Sahih Muslim, there is also a hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) with the following content:

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was sitting among his companions when a group of barefoot and poorly clothed people came to him, while they had swords fastened to their waists (ready for Jihad). Upon seeing their poverty, the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) face changed. He went into his house and then returned, ordering Bilal to call the people to gather and pray. After the prayer, the Prophet delivered a sermon and said: "O people, fear Allah, the same God who created you all from a single soul, and know that Allah is watching over you. People! Practice piety and think of the tomorrow of Resurrection. Let each of you help the needy with a dinar, a dirham, clothing, wheat, dates, or even part of a date."

At this moment, a man from the Ansar brought a bag of money that his hand could barely hold. This encouraged the people, and they provided various aids one after another until two heaps of food and clothing were gathered. Signs of joy appeared on the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) face. At this moment he said:

"Whoever establishes a good tradition in Islam, he shall have its reward and the reward of those who act upon it after him without their rewards being diminished in the least. And whoever establishes an evil tradition in Islam, upon him is its burden and the burden of those who act upon it after him without their burdens being diminished in the least" [1].

The meaning of this part of the hadith is the same as the previous ones.

Here a question arises: How are these hadiths and similar Quranic verses compatible with verse 164 of Surah Al-An'am: "And no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another" (وَ لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى‌)?

The answer to this question is quite clear considering one point: they are not responsible for the sins of others, but rather responsible for their own sins, because they participated in the realization of others' sins, and in one sense, it is considered their own sin.

2- Futile Submission!

Few people are found who see the truth at the stage of witnessing and still deny it. For this reason, sinners and oppressors, when they are on the threshold of death and the veils of heedlessness and pride are removed and they gain purgatorial vision, declare faith, as we read in the above verses: "they offered submission" (fa-alqawu al-salam).

However, at this time they have various utterances. Some disavow their ugly deeds and say: "We did not perform any evil deed" (as stated in the above verses). That is, they have lied so much that lying has become part of the fabric of their being, and even though they know it is no place for lying, they lie again! It is even understood from some Quranic verses that in the Resurrection, a group will lie: "They will say: 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists!'" (Surah Al-An'am, Verse 23).

Others express regret and request to return to the world (Surah As-Sajdah, Verse 12).

Others merely express faith like Pharaoh (Surah Yunus, 90). But in any case, none of these words will be accepted, because the time for it has passed, and this kind of expression of faith is of a desperate nature, and we have repeatedly said that desperate faith is ineffective.

[1]: Some have considered it a plural of a plural, such that "asatir" is the plural of "astar" and "astar" is the plural of "satr," and some believe "asatir" is among the plurals that have no singular of their own kind, but the famous view is what we mentioned in the text of the commentary.

[1]: Tushaqqun is from the root shiqaq, meaning opposition and enmity, and its origin is from the root shaqq, meaning to halve (and create a rift).

[2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

۞ وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ مَاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۗ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ ۚ وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ ۚ وَلَنِعْمَ دَارُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٠)
But those who were Godwary will be asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They will say, ‘Good.’ For those who do good in this world there will be a good [reward], and the abode of the Hereafter is better, and the abode of the Godwary is surely excellent: (30)
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣١)
the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter, with streams running in them. There they will have whatever they wish, and thus does Allah reward the Godwary (31)
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ ۙ يَقُولُونَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٣٢)
—those whom the angels take away while they are pure. They say [to them], ‘Peace be to you! Enter paradise because of what you used to do.’ (32)

Commentary

The Destiny of the Pure and the Righteous

In the preceding verses, we read the statements of the polytheists regarding the Quran and its consequences. However, in the verses under discussion, we read the belief of the believers along with the mention of its results:

First, it says: "When it is said to those who are God-fearing, 'What has your Lord sent down?' they say, 'Goodness'" (وَ قِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا خَيْراً).

In the Tafsir of al-Qurtubi, we read that while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in Mecca during the Hajj season—when people from all over the Arabian Peninsula entered Mecca in groups—they would ask various individuals about the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, as they had heard scattered reports about him. Whenever they asked the polytheists this question, they would say: "It is nothing but the superstitious fables and myths of the ancients." But whenever they encountered the believers and posed this question, the believers would say: "Our Lord has sent down nothing but goodness and virtue."

What an eloquent, beautiful, and comprehensive expression! "Goodness" (Khayr)—and absolute goodness at that—whose broad concept encompasses all virtues, happiness, and material and spiritual victories. Goodness in relation to this world, goodness in relation to the Hereafter, goodness for the individual, and goodness for society. Goodness in the fields of education, politics, economy, security, and freedom; in short, goodness from every perspective (for we know that when the object of a word is omitted, its meaning becomes universal).

It is also necessary to mention that various descriptions of the Quran have appeared in the Quran itself: it is described as Light, Healing, Guidance, the Criterion (Furqan—that which distinguishes truth from falsehood), Truth, a Reminder, and the like. However, this is perhaps the only verse in which it is described as "Goodness" (Khayr), and it can be said that all those specific concepts are gathered within this general concept.

Furthermore, the difference in the expressions used by the polytheists and the believers regarding the Quran is noteworthy: the believers said: Khayran, meaning Anzala Khayran ("He has sent down goodness"), and in this way, they manifested their faith in the Quran being revelation [^1]. But when the polytheists were asked what their Lord had sent down, they said: "These are the myths of the ancients," and thus, they completely denied the Quran's status as revelation [^2]. Then, just as in the previous verses where the result of the polytheists' statements was described in the form of doubled worldly and otherworldly, material and spiritual punishments, in the verse under discussion, the result of the believers' statements is described as follows: "For those who did good in this world, there is goodness" (لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةٌ).

Interestingly, "Hasanah" (goodness/reward), which is their recompense—just like "Khayr" which was their "declaration"—is left absolute and encompasses all types of virtues and blessings of this world. This is their worldly reward. Then it adds: "And the home of the Hereafter is even better, and how excellent is the home of the God-fearing!"

[^1]: Khayran is actually the object (maf'ul) for a deleted verb, and the implied phrase was Anzala Khayran.

[^2]: Asatir al-Awwalin is the predicate (khabar) for a deleted subject, and the implied phrase was Hadhihi Asatir al-Awwalin.

(وَ لَدارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ وَ لَنِعْمَ دارُ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

Again, in this expression, we encounter the word "Khayr" and the phrase "How excellent is the home of the God-fearing," both of which are absolute in their broad meaning. And it must be so, for rewards are reflections of human actions in their quality and quantity!

Moreover, from what we have said, it becomes clear that the sentence "For those who did good..." until the end of the verse is apparently the word of Allah, and the context of comparison between these verses and the previous ones strengthens this meaning, although some commentators have suggested two possibilities: first, that it is the word of Allah, and second, that it is a continuation of the speech of the God-fearing.

The next verse describes the home of the God-fearing, which was previously mentioned in brief, saying: "The home of the God-fearing is the Gardens of Eternity which they shall enter" (جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَها). "Rivers flow beneath their trees" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

It is not just a matter of gardens and trees; rather, "They shall have therein whatever they desire" (لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ‌).

Can a more expansive expression or a more comprehensive concept regarding heavenly blessings be found?

This expression even seems broader than the one in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 71, which says: وَ فِيها ما تَشْتَهِيهِ الْأَنْفُسُ وَ تَلَذُّ الْأَعْيُنُ ("And therein is whatever the souls desire and the eyes delight in"). There, the talk is of the desire of the souls, but here it is of absolute will (Yasha'un - whatever they will).

Some commentators have inferred exclusivity from the placement of "For them therein" (Lahum fiha) before "whatever they desire" (ma yasha'un); meaning it is only there that man finds whatever he wills, otherwise, such a thing is impossible in this world!

We mentioned that the verses under discussion, which explain the nature of the life and death of the God-fearing, are harmonious and parallel to the previous verses that spoke of the arrogant polytheists. There, we read that the angels of death take their souls while they are wronging themselves, and their death is the beginning of a new era of their misery, and then they are commanded to enter the gates of Hell.

But here we read: "The God-fearing are those whose souls the angels take while they are pure and good" (الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ‌).

Pure from the pollution of polytheism, pure from oppression, arrogance, and every kind of sin.

Here, the angels "say to them: Peace be upon you" (يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

A peace that is a sign of security, health, and complete tranquility.

Then they say: "Enter Paradise because of the deeds you used to do" (ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

The expression "Tawaffa" (receiving the soul) is a subtle expression regarding death, indicating that death does not mean annihilation, non-existence, or the end of everything, but rather a transition from one stage to a higher stage.

In Tafsir al-Mizan, we read that in this verse, three subjects are raised:

  1. Being Tayyib (pure and good).
  2. Peace and security from every aspect.
  3. Guidance to Paradise.

These three blessings are the same as what we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 82: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ لَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَ هُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ ("Those who believe and do not obscure their faith with wrongdoing—for them is security, and they are [rightly] guided").

Ayahs

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٣٣)
Do they not consider that the angels may come to them, or your Lord’s edict may come? Those who were before them had acted likewise; Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves. (33)
فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٤)
So the evils of what they had earned visited them, and they were besieged by what they used to deride. (34)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا عَبَدْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ نَّحْنُ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ فَهَلْ عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٣٥)
The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshiped anything besides Him—neither we, nor our fathers—nor we would have forbidden anything without His sanction.’ Those who were before them had acted likewise. Is the apostles’ duty anything but to communicate in clear terms? (35)
وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۚ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٦)
Certainly We raised an apostle in every nation [to preach:] ‘Worship Allah, and shun fake deities.’ Among them were some whom Allah guided, and among them were some who deserved to be in error. So travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers. (36)
إِن تَحْرِصْ عَلَىٰ هُدَىٰهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَن يُضِلُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٣٧)
Even if you are eager for them to be guided, indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead [others], and they will have no helpers. (37)

Commentary

The Duty of Prophets is Clear Proclamation

Once again, the Quran analyzes the mindset and agenda of the polytheists and the arrogant, speaking to them in a tone of warning:

What are they waiting for? "Are they waiting for the angels of death to come to them, for the doors of repentance to be closed, for the record of deeds to be rolled up, and for no way of return to remain?" (هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ).

Or are they waiting for "the command of your Lord regarding their torment and punishment to be issued?" (أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ).

In that state as well, the doors of repentance will be shut, and there will be no way to return or compensate. What kind of thinking is this that they possess, and what foolish stubbornness and persistence?

Here, although "angels" (ملائكه) is mentioned in an absolute sense, considering the preceding verses that spoke of the angels who take souls, it refers to the same matter.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 11; p. 218

The phrase "the command of your Lord comes" (يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ), although it allows for various interpretations, refers here to the descent of punishment, given that this expression is used in various Quranic verses to mean the arrival of torment. In any case, the combination of these two phrases is a threat to these arrogant ones: if the divine exhortations and His prophets do not awaken you, the lashes of punishment and death certainly will—but at that time, awakening will be of no use.

Then it adds that it is not only this group that acts this way; rather, "those before them did the same" (كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

"Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves" (وَ ما ظَلَمَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ).

This is because they are, in reality, only reaping the results of their own actions.

This sentence once again emphasizes the truth that every injustice, oppression, and evil committed by a human eventually catches up with them; in fact, it reaches them before anyone else. This is because an evil deed sprinkles its ominous effects upon the soul and spirit of its doer; their heart becomes dark, their spirit polluted, and their peace will be destroyed.

The text continues to address the consequences of their actions, stating: "So the evil results of what they did struck them" (فَأَصابَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا). "And that which they used to mock (of the promises of divine punishment) encompassed them" (وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

The phrase "حاق بهم" means "it befell them" or "descended upon them," but some, like Al-Qurtubi in his commentary on this verse, and Farid Wajdi in his Tafsir, have taken it to mean "encompassed them." A comprehensive meaning can be stated that includes both "befalling" and "encompassing."

In any case, the expression in the above verse stating "the evil of their deeds reached them" again emphasizes the reality that it is the human's own actions that seize them in this world and the hereafter, manifesting in various forms to cause them distress, pain, and torture, and nothing else. [^1]

The next verse refers to one of the weak and baseless arguments of the polytheists, saying: "The polytheists said: 'If Allah had willed, neither we nor our ancestors would have worshipped anything besides Him'" and we would not have turned to idols (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ نَحْنُ وَ لا آباؤُنا).

[^1]: Therefore, there is no necessity to assume the word "requital" (جزاء) is implied before "evil deeds" (سيئات).

"Nor would we have forbidden anything without His [command]" (وَ لا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

This sentence refers to a portion of livestock that the polytheists in the Age of Ignorance had forbidden on their own authority, for which they were severely criticized by the Prophet of Islam.

In short, they wanted to claim that whatever they do—from worshipping idols to declaring things forbidden or permissible—is all according to the pleasure of Allah and is not without His permission.

This statement might mean they believed in "determinism" (jabr) and considered everything dependent on the decree of fate (as many commentators have understood).

However, there is also the possibility that they did not say this out of a belief in determinism, but rather intended to argue: "If our actions are not pleasing to Allah, then why did He not send prophets earlier to forbid them? Why did He not tell our ancestors from the very first day that He is not pleased with this? His silence is proof of His approval!" This interpretation is more consistent with the end of this verse and the subsequent verses.

Therefore, He immediately says: "Those before them did the same (and resorted to the same excuses), but is there any duty upon the messengers except clear proclamation?" (كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ).

Meaning, firstly, your claim that Allah remained silent is false; He never remained silent, and every prophet who came clearly invited people to monotheism and the rejection of polytheism. Secondly, the duty of Allah and the Prophet is not to compel, but to show the way and present the path, and this task has been accomplished.

Furthermore, the phrase "Those before them did the same" (كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ) is a form of consolation for the Prophet, so he may know that what they are doing is the same as what was done to all previous prophets. Do not be grieved; persevere firmly and stand fast, for Allah is your helper and supporter.

Following the declaration of the reality that the duty of prophets is only clear proclamation, the next verse gives a brief and comprehensive reference to the nature of the prophets' mission, saying: "And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger" (وَ لَقَدْ بَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولًا). "Ummah" (nation/community) comes from the word "Umm" (mother) or refers to anything to which others are joined. Thus, any group that shares a type of unity—whether in terms of time, place, thought, or goal—is called an "Ummah." An examination of its usage in the Quran, which reaches 64 instances, confirms this reality.

Then it adds that the content of the call of all these messengers was: "Worship Allah and avoid the Taghut" (أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ اجْتَنِبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ). [^1] In this way, the call to "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and "Struggle against Taghut" formed the foundation of the mission of all prophets, and it was the first thing to which everyone, without exception, was invited. For if the pillars of Monotheism are not strengthened, and if the Taghuts are not expelled from human societies and the environment of thought, no reform program can be implemented.

[^1]: This sentence is implied as: "To say to them: Worship Allah..."

"Taghut," as we have previously stated, is an intensive form from the root "tughyan," meaning one who exceeds and transgresses boundaries. It is also applied to anything that becomes a means of exceeding limits. For this reason, Satan, idols, tyrannical and oppressive rulers, the arrogant, and ultimately any path that leads to other than the Truth, are called Taghut.

This word is used in both singular and plural senses, although it is sometimes pluralized as "Tawaghit."

Now let us see where the result of the prophets' call to Monotheism led. Here the Quran says:

"Among them were some whom Allah guided" (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ هَدَى اللَّهُ).

"And among them were some upon whom error was deservedly fixed" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ الضَّلالَةُ).

Although the proponents of the school of determinism (jabr) have raised a clamor over this verse, claiming it as another proof for their school, we have repeatedly stated that if the verses of guidance and misguidance are placed side by side, there is no ambiguity in them. Not only do they not suggest determinism, but they clearly express the issue of human choice, will, and freedom. This is because many Quranic verses state that divine guidance and misguidance follow the merits or demerits that arise in humans as a result of their actions.

Allah does not guide the wrongdoers, the extravagant, the liars, and their like, according to the explicit verses of the Quran. Conversely, those who strive and struggle in the way of Allah, and those who accept the call of the prophets, are all included in His favors and are guided along the ups and downs of the path toward perfection and the journey toward Allah. However, He leaves the first group to themselves, so that the consequences of their actions seize them and they wander aimlessly in the wilderness.

And since the property of actions—whether good or evil—is from Allah, their results can be attributed to Allah.

Yes, the Sunnah (way) of Allah is that He first initiates "Legislative Guidance" (Hidayat-e Tashri'i), meaning He sends prophets to invite people to Monotheism and the rejection of Taghut in harmony with human nature (fitrah). Following this legislative guidance, any person or group that shows merit and worthiness becomes subject to His grace and "Existential Guidance" (Hidayat-e Takwini).

Yes, this is the perpetual Sunnah of Allah, not as Fakhr al-Razi and others like him from the proponents of determinism have said—that Allah first invites through prophets, and then He Himself forcibly creates faith or disbelief in individuals (without any reason), and strangely, no question can be asked of Allah in this regard! Truly, what a terrifying depiction they have made of Allah, which is incompatible with any reason, emotion, or logic.

It is noteworthy that the expression in the above verse regarding guidance and misguidance is different:

In the first case, it says "a group whom Allah guided," but in the second case, it does not say "a group whom Allah led astray"; rather, it says "misguidance was fixed upon them" (حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ الضَّلالَةُ) (it became their due and clung to them!).

This difference in expression may point to a reality mentioned in other Quranic verses and some narrations, the summary of which is as follows:

In relation to the issue of guidance, the greatest share belongs to the preliminaries that Allah has created: He gave reason, mobilized the fitrah for Monotheism, sent prophets, and presented legislative and existential signs. Only a free decision from the servants is sufficient to bring them to the intended destination.

But in the issue of misguidance, the misguided servants hold the entire share, because they move against the course of existential and legislative events. They trample upon the divine fitrah, ignore His laws, leave the legislative and existential signs behind them, close the eyes and ears of the heart to the call of the prophets, and in short, by resorting to all this destruction and distortion, they step into the valley of misguidance. Are all these matters not from their own side?

This is exactly what verse 79 of Surah An-Nisa refers to: "Whatever good reaches you is from Allah, and whatever evil reaches you is from yourself" (ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَ ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ).

In Usul al-Kafi, a hadith is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام that clarifies this matter further. He said to one of his companions who asked about the issue of determinism and free will:

"Write: In the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام said: Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: O son of Adam! It is by My will that you are the one who wills, and by My power that you perform My obligations, and by My blessing that you gained strength for My disobedience. I made you hearing and seeing. 'Whatever good reaches you is from Allah, and whatever evil reaches you is from yourself.' And that is because I am more worthy of your good deeds than you, and you are more worthy of your evil deeds than Me." [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 160 (Chapter on Jabr and Qadar, Hadith 12).

At the end of the verse, to awaken the misguided and strengthen the morale of the guided, He issues this general command: "Travel through the earth and observe the remains of the predecessors hidden on the surface of the earth and in the heart of the soil, and see what was the end of those who denied the signs of Allah" (فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ).

This expression is itself living proof of the freedom of human will. For if guidance and misguidance were compulsory, traveling the earth and studying the states of the predecessors would be futile. This command is itself an emphasis that no one's fate has been compulsorily predetermined; rather, it is in their own hands.

The Holy Quran has many interesting and extensive discussions about "traveling the earth" and studying the remains of the predecessors, which we explained in detail in the third volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran (refer to Vol. 3, p. 102).

In the last verse under discussion, while consoling the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it emphasizes that the end of this misguided and stubborn group reaches a point where no matter how much "you are greedy for their guidance and strive, it is of no use, for Allah does not guide those whom He has led astray!" (إِنْ تَحْرِصْ عَلى‌ هُداهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ). "And for them there are no helpers" (وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ).

"Tahris" (تحرص) comes from the root "hirs," meaning to seek something with seriousness and effort.

It is obvious that this sentence is not regarding all the deviant ones, because the duty of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is proclamation and guidance, and we know—and history testifies—that this proclamation and guidance take effect in many of the misguided, who then join the religion of truth and defend it passionately.

Therefore, the above sentence is specific to that group that has reached the peak of obstinacy and stubbornness, and is so drowned in arrogance, pride, negligence, and sin that the doors of guidance will no longer be opened to them. These are the ones for whom, no matter how much the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله strives for their guidance, there is no result, because due to their actions, they have become so lost that they are no longer capable of being guided.

Naturally, such individuals cannot have any helper either, because every helper acts within a suitable context.

Furthermore, this expression is itself a proof for the rejection of determinism, because a "helper" (nasir) relates to a situation where there is an inner boiling from within the person themselves, and the helper's role is one of assistance and aid (reflect on this).

The use of "nasirin" (helpers) in the plural form perhaps indicates that the group of believers, unlike this group, has not just one helper but multiple helpers and supporters: Allah is their helper, the prophets and righteous servants of Allah are their helpers, and the angels of mercy are also considered their supporters.

In Surah Ghafir, verse 51, we read: "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand" (إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ).

And in Surah Fussilat, verse 30, we read: "Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remained on a right course—the angels will descend upon them, [saying], 'Do not fear and do not grieve but receive the good tidings of Paradise, which you were promised'" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخافُوا وَ لا تَحْزَنُوا وَ أَبْشِرُوا بِالْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ).

Key Points

1- What is Clear Proclamation (Balagh Mubin)?

In the above verses, we saw that the primary duty of all prophets is clear proclamation (فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ).

This means that divine leaders cannot continue their call secretly, unless it is for a limited and temporary period. A program of secrecy, especially in the era of the prophetic mission, is not something acceptable or effective. Clarity combined with bravery and decisiveness, alongside wisdom, is a condition of this call.

For this reason, we read in the history of all prophets that although they were often alone, they expressed their call with a clear, open, and manifest statement, and accepted all its consequences.

Such is the way and method of all true leaders (whether prophets or otherwise) who, in the essence of their call, never "hold their tongue," so to speak, nor use ambiguous words. They omit nothing in expressing the truth and accept all the consequences of this clarity and decisiveness with their very lives.

2- Every Nation Has a Messenger

In the above verses, we read that Allah says: "We raised a messenger in every nation." Following this, the question arises: if a messenger was sent to every nation, then prophets must have arisen from all countries of the world, as each of them is at least a nation, whereas history does not show us such a thing?

The answer to this question becomes clear with one point: the goal of sending prophets is for the divine call to reach the ears of the nations. Otherwise, we know, for example, that at the time the Prophet of Islam rose in Mecca or Medina, there was no prophet in other cities of the Hijaz, but the messengers of the Messenger of Allah were dispatched to those regions and brought the voice of the Prophet to everyone's ears.

In addition, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself wrote letters and sent them with messengers to various countries such as Iran, Rome, and Abyssinia, proclaiming the divine message to them.

Even now, we are a nation that has heard the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله across ages and centuries through scholars who are the messengers of that Prophet. The meaning of raising a messenger in every nation is nothing other than this.

Ayahs

وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ لَا يَبْعَثُ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَمُوتُ ۚ بَلَىٰ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٨)
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that Allah will not resurrect those who die. Yes indeed [He will], it is a promise binding upon Him, but most people do not know, (38)
لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٣٩)
so that He may clarify for them what they differ about, and that the faithless may know that they were liars. (39)
إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَىْءٍ إِذَآ أَرَدْنَـٰهُ أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٤٠)
All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is. (40)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse that a man from among the Muslims was owed a debt by one of the polytheists. When he demanded its payment, the polytheist procrastinated in settling his debt. The Muslim man became upset and, during his words, swore an oath: "By that which I await after death..." (referring to the Resurrection and Allah’s reckoning). The polytheist said: "Do you imagine that we will be brought back to life after death?! By Allah, He will never resurrect any dead person." (He said this because they considered the return of the dead to life and a second existence to be impossible or futile). The above verse was revealed to answer him and those like him, explaining the issue of the Hereafter with clear proof. In reality, the conversation between these two individuals served as a catalyst for re-addressing the issue of the Resurrection. [^1]

Commentary

The Resurrection and the End of Disputes

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the issue of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and the mission of the Prophets, these verses complete those discussions by mentioning a portion of the topics related to the "Resurrection."

First, it says: "And they swore by Allah their strongest oaths that Allah will never resurrect those who die," and will not return them to a new life (وَ أَقْسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لا يَبْعَثُ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَمُوتُ).

This baseless denial, especially one accompanied by emphatic oaths, was a sign of their ignorance and lack of awareness. Therefore, the Quran replies to them:

"This is a definitive promise of Allah (that He will return all the dead to a new life so they may see the results of their deeds), but most people do not know," and due to their lack of knowledge, they resort to denial (بَلى‌ وَعْداً عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, and Abu al-Futuh Razi, under the verse in question.

The use of the expression "بَلى" (Yes/Nay), "حَقًّا" (in truth), and then mentioning the "promise"—a promise that is binding upon Allah—all serve as signs of emphasis and the certainty of the issue of Resurrection.

Fundamentally, when facing someone who categorically denies a truth, one must speak with decisiveness, so that the negative psychological effects of that absolute denial are dismantled by an absolute affirmation. Specifically, it must be made clear that this denial stems from a lack of information and ignorance, so that it loses its influence entirely.

Then, it proceeds to explain one of the objectives of the Resurrection, followed by a mention of Allah’s power over this task, to show that if the source of their denial is that they consider the return to life to be beyond Allah’s power, it is a great mistake; and if the source is the perceived aimlessness of the Resurrection, that too is another error.

First, it says: "Allah will resurrect the dead so that He may make clear to them that wherein they differed" (لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ).

"And so that those who disbelieved may know that they were liars" (وَ لِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا كاذِبِينَ).

This is because that world is the world of "uncovering the veil," the removal of curtains, and the manifestation of realities. As we read in Surah Qaf, verse 22: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ—"It will be said to man: You were in heedlessness of such a day, but We have removed from you your veil, and your sight today is sharp!" And in verse 9 of Surah At-Tariq, we read: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ—"The Day when secrets are tested (revealed)." And in verse 48 of Surah Ibrahim, we read: وَ بَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ—"And they will come forth before Allah, the One, the Irresistible."

Ultimately, that day is the day of witnessing, the uncovering of secrets, the manifestation of the hidden, and the day of emergence. In such conditions and environment, differences in belief have no meaning—though it is possible that some stubborn deniers might resort to lies in certain stages of the Resurrection to exonerate themselves, but that is an exceptional and fleeting matter! This is exactly like bringing a criminal before a court; he denies everything, but immediately his recorded voice, his handwriting, and other living and sensory evidence are presented to him, and all the traces of the crime are brought out from within his house and room. Here, no room remains for argument, and he submits. The manifestation of realities in the world of Resurrection is even clearer and more evident than this!

Although life after death and the Resurrection have multiple objectives mentioned scattered throughout the verses of the Quran—such as the perfection of man, the implementation of justice, giving purpose to the life of this world, the continuation of Allah’s grace, and the like—the verse under discussion points to another objective: the resolution of differences and the return to Monotheism.

We know that the most important principle governing the universe is the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid). This all-encompassing principle is true regarding Allah’s Essence, Attributes, and Actions, as well as in the entirety of the created world and the laws governing it; everything must eventually return to this principle.

Therefore, we believe that these differences, wars, and conflicts will all one day come to an end, and all the people of the world will gather under the banner of a single government—the government of Imam Mahdi عليه السلام—because whatever is contrary to the spirit of the universe (i.e., Monotheism) must eventually be dismantled.

However, differences in belief will never completely disappear in this world, because this world is a place of "veils" and coverings. But eventually, a day will arrive when these coverings and veils will be removed; it is the "Day of Emergence" and the "Day of Manifestation."

Therefore, the return to unity and the ending of ideological differences constitute one of the objectives of the Resurrection, as pointed out in the above verse.

In numerous verses of the Holy Quran, this issue is repeated and emphasized: that Allah will judge between people on the Day of Resurrection and differences will be resolved. [^1]

Then, it addresses the second point, which is the explanation of the reality that if they consider the return of man to a new life impossible, they must know that Allah’s power is far superior to this: "When We intend a thing, We only say to it, 'Be!' and it is" (إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْ‌ءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ).

With such perfect power, where the mere command "Be" is sufficient for the existence of anything, what room remains for doubt or hesitation regarding Allah’s power to return the dead to life?

It perhaps requires no explanation that the expression "كُنْ" (Be) is due to the limitation of human expression and the inadequacy of words; otherwise, there is not even a need for the sentence "Be." Allah’s Will is identical to the realization of the thing.

[^1]: In this regard, refer to verses: Al-Imran 55, Al-Ma'idah 48, Al-An'am 164, An-Nahl 92, and Al-Hajj 69.

If we wish to provide a small and imperfect example of this matter in our own lives, we can liken it to the creation of mental images in the brain by mere will. When we decide to have an image of a massive mountain, a vast ocean, or a large garden with many trees in our minds, do we face any difficulty? Do we even need a sentence or a word? Rather, as soon as we will it, the image of these mental entities appears on the horizon of our minds.

In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read that one of his companions asked for an explanation regarding the Will of Allah and the will of the creation. The Imam said: "Will from the creation is the internal decision followed by the actions that appear after it. But the Will of Allah is His act of bringing into existence and nothing else; for in Allah, there is no thought, no decision, and no reflection. These attributes (which are all additional to the essence) have no meaning regarding Allah; these are attributes of the created. Therefore, Allah’s Will is the creation of acts and nothing else. Allah says: 'Be,' and it becomes, without even needing a word, speaking with a tongue, making a decision, or reflecting. And this Will of Allah, like His Essence, is indescribable." [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Chapter on Will (Bab al-Iradah), Hadith 3.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
Those who migrate for the sake of Allah after they have been wronged, We will surely settle them in a good place in the world, and the reward of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known (41)
ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٤٢)
—those who are patient and who put their trust in their Lord. (42)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated regarding the circumstances of the revelation of these verses that a group of Muslims, such as "Bilal," "Ammar Yasir," "Suhaib," and "Khabbab," were under severe pressure in Mecca after embracing Islam. To strengthen Islam and convey their voice to others, they migrated to Medina following the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) migration—a migration that led to victory for them and others. Among them, Suhaib, who was an elderly man, made a proposal to the polytheists of Mecca: "I am an old man; my presence among you is of no benefit to you, and if I were your opponent, I would have no power to harm you. Come, take my wealth and let me go to Medina." They agreed.

Suhaib gave all his wealth to them and migrated toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Some said to Suhaib, "You have made a very profitable bargain!" Then the above verses were revealed, recounting his victory and the victory of those like him in this world and the Hereafter.

It is recorded in history that during the time of the Caliphs, when the funds of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) were being distributed and it was the turn of the Migrants (Muhajirin), they would be told: "Take your share; this is what Allah promised you in this world, and what awaits you in the next world is greater." Then they would recite the above verse. [^1]

Commentary

The Reward of the Migrants

We have repeatedly stated that one of the most effective methods in the educational programs used by the Quran is the method of comparison and contrast, where everything is placed alongside its opposite so that the position of each is clearly defined. Following the previous verses which spoke of the deniers of the Resurrection and the stubborn polytheists, the verses under discussion speak of the sincere and self-sacrificing Migrants, so that the status of the two groups becomes clear in comparison to one another.

First, It says: "And those who emigrated for [the sake of] Allah after they had been wronged—We will surely settle them in this world in a good place" (وَ الَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً).

This is their worldly reward, "but the reward of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew" (وَ لَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

In the next verse, It describes these sincere, steadfast, and faithful Migrants with two attributes:

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

It says: "They are those who have practiced patience and endurance, and their trust and reliance are upon their Lord" (الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ).

Key Points

  1. Although the Muslims had two migrations—the first being on a relatively small scale (the migration of a group of Muslims led by Ja'far ibn Abi Talib to Abyssinia) and the second being the general and collective migration of the Prophet and the Muslims from Mecca to Medina—the apparent meaning of the above verses describes the second migration, and the circumstances of revelation also confirm this.

Regarding the importance and role of migration (Hijrah) in the life and history of Muslims in the past and present, and the continuity of this issue in every age and time, we had a detailed discussion under verse 100 of Surah An-Nisa and also under verse 75 of Surah Al-Anfal. [^1] In any case, the status of the Migrants in Islam is an exceptionally esteemed position. Both the Prophet Himself and the subsequent Muslims all held a special respect for them, because they turned their backs on their entire lives for the sake of spreading the call of Islam. Some risked their lives, and some, like Suhaib, overlooked all their wealth; interestingly, they considered themselves winners in this bargain.

If it were not for the sacrifice of those Migrants in those days, the suffocating environment of Mecca and the satanic elements ruling over it would never have allowed the voice of Islam to reach anyone's ears, and they would have stifled this voice in the throats of the believers forever. However, with this calculated leap, they not only brought Mecca under their influence but also brought the voice of Islam to the ears of the world. This is a Sunnah (tradition) for all Muslims under such conditions throughout all of history.

  1. The expression "migrated in Allah" (hajaru fi-Allah), in which even the word "path" (sabil) is not mentioned, points to the ultimate sincerity of these Migrants, who undertook such a migration solely for Allah, in the path of Allah, for His pleasure, and to support and defend His religion—not to save their own lives or for any other material gain.
  1. The phrase "after they had been wronged" (min ba'di ma zulimu) shows that one should not immediately vacate the field; rather, one must stand and endure as much as possible. However, when enduring the enemy's harassment yields no result other than increasing the enemy's audacity and weakening the believers, then one must resort to migration. This is so that by gaining more power and establishing stronger strongholds, the struggle (Jihad) in all fields can be pursued from a better position, leading to the victory of the supporters of truth in military, cultural, and promotional fields.
  1. The phrase "We will surely settle them in this world in a good place" (lanubawwi'annahum fi al-dunya hasanatan), which is derived from the root bawwa'tu lahu makanan (I prepared a place for him and settled him there), shows that although the true Migrants lose their material resources at the beginning of the task, ultimately, even in terms of worldly life, victory is with them. [^1]

[^1]: Please refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 89 to 94, and Volume 7, pages 261 to 263. [^1]: "Lanubawwi'annahum" is originally from the root bawa', meaning the equality of the parts of a place, as opposed to nabwah (on the weight of mabda'), which means the inequality of the parts of a place. Therefore, the phrase bawwa'tu lahu makanan means "I leveled the place for him," and subsequently came to mean preparing a place for someone.

Why should a person remain under the pressure of the enemy's blows and die humiliatingly? Why not migrate with courage and rise to struggle from a new position to reclaim one's rights? In Surah An-Nisa, verse 100, this same issue is stated with even more clarity: "And whoever emigrates in the way of Allah will find in the earth many [alternative] locations and abundance." (Meaning: they will find many safe and vast points in this wide world of God where they can rub the noses of the opponents in the dust!)

  1. The reason for choosing the two attributes of "patience" and "trust" (tawakkul) for the Migrants is clear. In such difficult and exhausting events that occur in human life, first and foremost, patience, endurance, and steadfastness are required—a steadfastness as great as the event or even greater. Then comes trust and reliance upon Allah. Fundamentally, if a person does not have a firm and reliable spiritual support in such events, patience and steadfastness are impossible for him.

Some have said that the choice of "patience" is because, at the beginning of this path toward Allah, patience and steadfastness against the desires of the self are necessary. The choice of "trust" is because the end of this path leads to detachment and cutting off from everything other than Allah and joining Him. Therefore, the first attribute is the beginning of the path and the second is its end. [^1] In any case, outward migration is impossible without an inward migration. A person must first cut off from base internal material attachments and migrate toward moral virtues so that he can undertake such a migration outwardly and move from the Abode of Disbelief (Dar al-Kufr)—by turning his back on everything—to the Abode of Faith (Dar al-Iman).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, under the verse.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٤٣)
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. (43)
بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ ۗ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٤٤)
[We sent them] with manifest proofs and scriptures. We have sent down the reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people that which has been sent down to them, so that they may reflect. (44)

Commentary

If You Do Not Know, Ask!

Although the previous two verses spoke of the true emigrants in connection with the discussions regarding the polytheists, the verses under discussion once again return to the previous issues related to the principles of religion (Usul al-Din) and respond to one of the well-known objections of the polytheists.

They used to say: Why has Allah not sent down an angel to deliver the message? (Or they would say: Why is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not equipped with an extraordinary power that would force us to abandon our practices?)

In response to them, Allah says: "And We sent not before you [O Muhammad] except men to whom We revealed" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ).

Indeed, these men were of the human race, possessing all human instincts and emotions; they could diagnose human pains better than anyone else and understood their needs perfectly. In contrast, no angel can be fully aware of these matters or clearly perceive what passes within a human being.

Certainly, the men of revelation had no duty other than delivering the message. Their task was to receive the revelation, convey it to humanity, and strive through natural means to realize the goals of that revelation—not to use an extraordinary divine power and disrupt all laws of nature to force people to accept the call and abandon all deviations. Had they done so, believing would have been neither a credit nor a means of perfection.

Then, to emphasize and confirm this reality, He adds: "So ask the people of the message (Ahl al-Dhikr) if you do not know" (فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

"Dhikr" means awareness and information, and "Ahl al-Dhikr" has a broad concept that includes all those who are knowledgeable and informed in various fields. If many commentators have interpreted Ahl al-Dhikr here to mean the scholars of the People of the Book (Ahl al-Kitab), it is not because the term has a limited meaning, but rather it is an application of a general rule to a specific instance. This is because the question regarding previous prophets and messengers—and the fact that they were men of the human race with missionary and executive programs—would naturally have to be directed toward the scholars of the People of the Book and the Jewish and Christian clerics. It is true that they did not share the same beliefs as the polytheists in all aspects, but they were aligned in their opposition to Islam. Therefore, the scholars of the People of the Book were considered a good source for the polytheists to explain the state of previous prophets.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: Dhikr has two meanings; sometimes it means preservation and sometimes it means remembrance. Of course, this remembrance may be through the heart (which is considered internal and inward dhikr) or it may be through the tongue (which is considered verbal dhikr). If we see that the Quran is called Dhikr, it is because it recounts truths.

The next verse says: "If you are not aware of the clear proofs of the prophets and their books, refer to the knowledgeable" (بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ الزُّبُرِ) [1]. Bayyinat is the plural of Bayyinah, meaning clear proofs, and it may refer here to miracles and other evidences proving the truthfulness of the prophets. Zubur is the plural of Zabur, meaning book. In fact, Bayyinat refers to the proofs establishing prophethood, and Zubur refers to the books in which the teachings of the prophets were collected.

Then, addressing the Prophet, He says: "And We revealed to you the message [the Quran] that you may make clear to the people what was sent down to them" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ).

"And that they might give thought [to these verses and the duties they have toward them]" (وَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ).

In reality, your call and the program of your mission are not something novel or unprecedented in principle. Just as We sent down heavenly books to previous messengers so they could acquaint people with their duties toward God, creation, and themselves, We have sent down this Quran to you so that you may clarify its concepts and teachings and awaken human thought, so they may step onto the path of truth and advance toward perfection with a sense of commitment and responsibility (not through compulsion or an unknown divine force).

[1] Regarding which verb بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ الزُّبُرِ is grammatically connected to, commentators have offered several possibilities. Some consider it connected to "لا تعلمون" (as we mentioned above, which is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verses—note that "knowledge" can be transitive both without and with the preposition "ba"). Others have said that the phrase "ارسلنا" is implied before it, meaning "We sent them with clear proofs and books." Others say it is connected to the phrase "وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا" in the previous verse, and some consider it connected to the phrase "نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ". It is clear that according to each of these, the meaning of the verse will have a specific nuance, but overall it does not create a significant difference.

Key Point: Who are the People of the Message (Ahl al-Dhikr)?

In numerous narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), we read that the Ahl al-Dhikr are the Imams (عليه السلام). Among them, in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that in response to a question about the above verse, he said: "We are the People of the Message, and we are the ones who should be asked." [1]

In another narration from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read that in interpreting this same verse, he said: "The Dhikr is the Quran, and the Family of the Messenger are the People of the Message, and they are the ones who should be asked." [2]

In others, we read that the "Dhikr" is the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his Ahl al-Bayt are the Ahl al-Dhikr. [3] Numerous other narrations have reached us with the same theme.

[1], [2], [3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 55-56.

In many commentaries and books of the Ahl al-Sunnah, we also read narrations with this same theme. For instance, in the twelve famous commentaries of the Ahl al-Sunnah, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the above verse that he said: "They are Muhammad, Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn (عليه السلام); they are the People of the Message, intellect, and clarification." [1]

This is not the first time that we see in the narrations interpreting Quranic verses the identification of specific instances which never limit the broad concept of the verse. As we said, Dhikr means any kind of awareness, remembrance, and information, and Ahl al-Dhikr encompasses the knowledgeable and informed in all fields.

However, since the Holy Quran is the most prominent example of remembrance, knowledge, and awareness, it has been called "Dhikr." Similarly, the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is also a clear instance of Dhikr. Accordingly, the Infallible Imams, who are his Ahl al-Bayt and the heirs to his knowledge, are the clearest instances of Ahl al-Dhikr.

Accepting all of this in no way contradicts the generality of the verse's concept, nor its occasion of revelation regarding the scholars of the People of the Book. For this reason, our scholars of Usul (principles of jurisprudence) and jurists have cited this verse in discussions related to Ijtihad and Taqlid (imitation), and the following of the uninformed in religious matters by the knowledgeable and Mujtahids.

[1] Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 3, p. 482. The twelve commentaries refer to the commentaries of Abu Yusuf, Ibn Hajar, Muqatil ibn Sulayman, Waki' ibn al-Jarrah, Yusuf ibn Musa, Qatadah, Harb al-Ta'i, al-Suddi, Mujahid, Muqatil ibn Hayyan, Abu Salih, and Muhammad ibn Musa al-Shirazi.

Another narration from Jabir al-Ju'fi regarding the interpretation of this same verse is cited in the book of al-Tha'labi, stating: "When the above verse was revealed, Ali (عليه السلام) said: 'We are the People of the Message.'" (Refer to the same source above).

Here, a question arises: in a narration cited in Uyun Akhbar al-Rida from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that the Imam criticized those who interpreted the above verse as referring to the scholars of the Jews and Christians, saying: "Glory be to Allah! Is such a thing possible? If we refer to the scholars of the Jews and Christians, they will certainly call us to their own religion." Then he said: "We are the People of the Message." [1]

The answer to this question is clear: the Imam said this to those who interpreted the verse as exclusively meaning referral to the scholars of the People of the Book in every age and time. This is certainly not the case. In an age and time such as that of Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), the people were never obligated to refer to Jewish and Christian scholars to understand truths. In such ages, the point of reference was the scholars of Islam, at the head of whom were the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

In other words, if the polytheists of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time had a duty to refer to the knowledgeable among the People of the Book to learn that God's prophets were always human, it does not mean that all people in every age must refer to them. Rather, every issue in every age must be asked of those knowledgeable in that specific issue, and this is a clear point.

In any case, the above verse expresses a fundamental Islamic principle in all fields of material and spiritual life, emphasizing to all Muslims that they should ask those who know about what they do not know, and not interfere on their own in matters where they lack expertise.

Thus, the "principle of specialization" is not only recognized by the Quran in the field of Islamic and religious matters but is accepted and emphasized in all fields. On this basis, it is necessary for all Muslims to have knowledgeable and expert individuals in all fields in every age, so that if people do not know something, they may refer to them.

However, it is also necessary to note that one must refer to specialists and experts whose honesty, integrity, and impartiality are proven and established. Would we ever refer to a physician who is knowledgeable and specialized in his field but whose honesty and integrity in his work we do not trust?! Therefore, in discussions related to Taqlid and the Marja'iyyah (religious authority), the attribute of 'Adalah (justice/integrity) is placed alongside Ijtihad or being the most learned (A'lamiyyah). That is, the Marja' al-Taqlid must be both knowledgeable in Islamic matters and pious and God-fearing.

[1] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 57.

Ayahs

أَفَأَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن يَخْسِفَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٤٥)
Do those who devise evil schemes feel secure that Allah will not make the earth swallow them, or the punishment will not overtake them whence they are not aware? (45)
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِى تَقَلُّبِهِمْ فَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٤٦)
Or that He will not seize them in the midst of their bustle, whereupon they will not be able to frustrate [Him]? (46)
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلَىٰ تَخَوُّفٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٤٧)
Or that He will not visit them with attrition? Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful. (47)

Commentary

Various Types of Punishments for Various Types of Sins!

In many discussions, the Quran intertwines logical arguments with emotional matters in such a way that it exerts the greatest influence on the souls of the listeners. The above verses are an example of this method. The preceding verses held a logical discussion regarding Prophethood and the Resurrection with the polytheists, but the verses under discussion turn to threatening the tyrants, the arrogant, and the stubborn sinners, warning them of various types of Divine punishments.

First, It says: "Do those who plotted evil deeds feel secure that Allah will not cause the earth to swallow them?" (أَ فَأَمِنَ الَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ اللَّهُ بِهِمُ الْأَرْضَ‌).

Is it far-fetched that a terrifying earthquake should occur, the crust of the earth split and open its mouth, and swallow them and all their livelihood, just as has repeatedly happened in the history of nations?

The phrase "مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئاتِ" means conspiring and plotting for sinister purposes and to achieve improper goals, such as the conspiracies the polytheists devised to extinguish the light of the Quran, destroy the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and harass the believers.

Yakhsif is derived from the root khasf (on the weight of wasf), meaning to disappear and be hidden. Therefore, the disappearance of the moon's light under the shadow of the earth is called khusuf (eclipse), and a "makhsuf" well is a well in which the water has disappeared. Similarly, the disappearance of humans and houses into a fissure in the earth (a fissure created by earthquakes) is called khasf.

Then It adds: "Or that the punishment should come upon them from where they do not perceive?" (أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حيثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

"Or that He should seize them while they are going to and fro [for more wealth and increased income]?" (أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِي تَقَلُّبِهِمْ‌). "For they cannot escape [the grasp of punishment]" (فَما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ‌).

As we have previously stated, "mu’jizin" is derived from the root i’jaz, meaning to render powerless, and in these contexts, it means escaping from the grasp of punishment or resisting it.

Or that the Divine punishment does not come upon them all at once, but rather "seizes them gradually and amidst successive warnings?" (أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلى‌ تَخَوُّفٍ‌). Today their neighbor is afflicted by an accident, tomorrow one of their relatives is caught in an incident, and another day some of their property is destroyed; in short, warnings come to them one after another. If they wake up, so much the better; otherwise, the final punishment will overtake them.

The gradual nature of the punishment in such cases is because there is still a possibility of guidance for this group, and the mercy of Allah does not allow them to be treated like others, "for your Lord is indeed Kind and Merciful" (فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, four types of punishment are mentioned: first, khasf and being swallowed by the earth; second, a surprise punishment from where one does not expect it; third, a punishment that comes upon a person during their efforts and endeavors to accumulate wealth; and fourth, gradual punishment.

It is certain that each of these four types of punishment corresponds to a specific type of sin, even though all of them were mentioned regarding "those who plot evil deeds" (الَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئاتِ), because we know that all of Allah's actions are according to wisdom and the proportion of desert.

Commentators, as far as we have seen, have not spoken on this matter, but it seems that:

The first punishment is specific to that group of conspirators who are in the ranks of dangerous tyrants and the arrogant, like the Qaruns, whom Allah brings down from the height of power and sends into the soil and the depths of the earth to serve as a lesson for all.

The second type is specific to those conspirators who are drowned in their luxury, pleasures, and rebellious desires; suddenly, the Divine punishment seizes them from where they do not expect.

The third type is specific to the wealth-hoarding world-worshippers who strive day and night to increase the volume of their wealth through any means and any crime possible; Allah seizes them with punishment while they are busy accumulating wealth [1]. As for the fourth type of punishment, it is for those whose rebellion, conspiracy, and sin have not reached such a level that there is no way back for them. Here, Allah punishes them through "takhawwuf" (warning and threat); that is, He first gives them a wake-up call through painful events occurring around them. If they wake up and reform their situation, so much the better; otherwise, He swallows them into the jaws of punishment.

Therefore, the mention of Allah's kindness and mercy as a reason pertains to the fourth group, who have not yet severed all their ties with Allah and have not destroyed all the bridges behind them.

[1]: Although "taqallub" in the Arabic language means any kind of coming and going, in these contexts—as many commentators have said and as emphasized in some Islamic narrations—it means coming and going on the path of trade and earning money (take note).

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا۟ ظِلَـٰلُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَٱلشَّمَآئِلِ سُجَّدًا لِّلَّهِ وَهُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ (٤٨)
Have they not regarded that whatever thing Allah has created casts its shadow to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility? (48)
وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٤٩)
To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, including animals and angels, and they are not arrogant. (49)
يَخَافُونَ رَبَّهُم مِّن فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ ۩ (٥٠)
They fear their Lord above them, and do what they are commanded. (50)

Commentary

The Prostration of All Living Creatures, Even Their Shadows, to Allah!

These verses return once again to the discussion of Monotheism (Tawhid). First, it says: "Have they (the plotting polytheists) not considered the things Allah has created, how their shadows incline to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah while they are humble?"

(أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى‌ ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَ الشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ وَ هُمْ داخِرُونَ‌) [^1]

"Yatafayya’u" is derived from the root "fay’," meaning to return or recur.

Some have said that the Arabs call the shadow of objects in the morning "zill" and in the afternoon "fay’." If we see that a portion of spoils and property is called "fay’," it is a subtle allusion to the fact that the best spoils of this world are like the afternoon shadow—quickly fading and vanishing.

However, considering that the above verse refers to the shadows of objects to the right and left and uses the word "fay’" for all of them, it is understood that "fay’" here has a broad meaning and includes every kind of shadow.
