When a person stands facing south at sunrise, he sees the sun's disk emerging from the left, from the eastern horizon, and the shadows of all objects fall to his right, which is the west. This continues, and the shadows constantly shift to the right until the sun's decline at noon. At this time, the shadows change position toward the left until sunset, when the large and long shadows of objects are spread toward the east, and with the setting of the sun, they all disappear.

Here, Allah introduces the movement of the shadows of objects to the right and left as a sign of His greatness, considering them to be in a state of prostration to the Lord, characterized by humility and submission.

[^1]: "Dakher" is originally from the root dukhoor, meaning humility and smallness.

The Role of Shadows in Our Lives

Undoubtedly, the shadows of objects play an effective role in our lives, which many might be heedless of. The Quran pointing its finger at the issue of shadows is to draw attention to this very point.

Although shadows are nothing but the absence of light, they have numerous benefits:

  1. Just as sunlight and its life-giving rays are the source of life and growth for creatures, shadows also play a vital role in moderating the radiation of light rays. Uniform sunlight over a long period would wither and burn everything, but the intermittent caress of shadows keeps it at a balanced and effective level.

  2. For those who travel through deserts or find themselves stranded in one, the effective role of shadows in saving human lives is exceptionally palpable—especially a shadow that is mobile, not concentrated in one spot, moving in every direction in harmony with human needs and desires!

  3. Another important point is that, contrary to public perception, light alone is not the cause of seeing objects; rather, light must always be accompanied by shadows and penumbras for the observation of objects to be realized. In other words, if light shines uniformly around an object such that it has no shadow or penumbra, such objects drowned in light will never be seen.

That is to say, just as nothing can be observed in absolute darkness, nothing can be seen in absolute light either. Rather, seeing objects becomes possible through the mixing of light and darkness (light and shadows). In this way, shadows play a very effective role in observing, distinguishing, and identifying objects from one another (reflect on this).

Another point is that "yamin" (right) in the above verse appears in the singular form, while "shama’il" (plural of shimal, meaning left) appears in the plural.

This difference in expression might be because the shadow at the beginning of the morning (for those facing south) falls to the right side, then constantly moves toward the left until sunset when it vanishes into the eastern horizon. [^1] Commentators have also suggested the possibility that although "yamin" is singular, it is sometimes intended as a plural, and here the plural is meant. [^2]

In the previous verse, only the "prostration" of shadows—with its broad concept—was mentioned. However, in the following verse, this issue is stated as a general program for all material and non-material, celestial and terrestrial beings, saying: "And to Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of moving creatures, and the angels [as well]" (وَ لِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ). "And they are not arrogant" (وَ هُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌); they are in total submission before Allah and His command.

The reality of prostration (sujud) is the ultimate humility, submission, and worship. Our ordinary prostration, performed with seven body parts, is an instance of this general concept and is not limited to it.

Since all beings and creations of Allah, in the realm of "Takwin" (creation), are submissive to the general laws of the universe and do not deviate from the path of these laws—and these

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the mentioned verse. [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 7, p. 110.

laws all originate from Allah—then in reality, all prostrate in His presence. They all express the greatness of His knowledge and power; they are all signs of His majesty and self-sufficiency; and ultimately, they are all evidence of His Holy Essence.

"Dabbah" means moving creatures, and the concept of life is also derived from it. The fact that the above verse says all moving creatures in the heavens and the earth prostrate to Allah allows us to infer that living beings are not exclusive to the planet Earth; rather, there are living and moving creatures in the celestial spheres as well.

Although some have suggested that the phrase "min dabbah" is only a qualifier for "what is on the earth"—meaning it only speaks of the moving creatures of the earth—this seems very unlikely. Especially since we read in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 29: "And of His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and what He has dispersed throughout them of creatures (dabbah)."

It is true that existential (takwini) prostration, humility, and submission are not limited to living and moving beings, but since these exhibit more of the secrets and wonders of creation, they have been specifically highlighted.

And since the concept of the verse includes both rational, believing humans and angels, as well as animals and other living things, the word "prostration" is used in its general sense, which encompasses both voluntary/legislative (tashri'i) prostration and existential/involuntary (takwini) prostration.

As for why the angels are mentioned separately in the above verse, it is because "dabbah" is only applied to moving creatures that are physical. If angels have movement, coming and going, and presence or absence, it is not in a physical and material sense such that they would be included in the concept of "dabbah."

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Allah has angels who have been prostrating to Allah from the beginning of their creation until the Day of Resurrection. On that day, they will raise their heads from prostration and say: 'We did not worship You as You deserve to be worshipped!'"

The phrase "وَ هُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌" (and they are not arrogant) refers to the state of the angels, indicating that they allow no arrogance into themselves while in submission and prostration in the presence of the Truth.

Therefore, immediately after that, it points to two of their attributes which emphasize the negation of arrogance, saying: "They fear their Lord who is above them" (يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ‌). "And they do what they are commanded" (وَ يَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ‌).

Just as we read in Surah At-Tahrim, verse 6, regarding a group of angels: "They do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded" (لا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَ يَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ‌).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that the sign of negating arrogance is two things: fear in the face of responsibilities, and carrying out Allah's commands without question. One refers to the psychological state of non-arrogant individuals, and the other refers to their mode of action and their encounter with laws and instructions; the second is a reflection of the first and its objective realization.

Certainly, the phrase "min fawqihim" (from above them) does not refer to physical or spatial height, but rather to a superiority of rank, for Allah is superior and higher than all.

In Surah Al-An'am, verse 61, we read: "And He is the Irresistible, [High] above His servants" (وَ هُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ). Even Pharaoh, when he wanted to show his power and strength, said: "And indeed, we are irresistible above them" (وَ إِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ). [^1] In all these cases, "above" (fawq) expresses superiority of rank.

[^1]: Surah Al-A'raf, verse 127.

Ayahs

۞ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ (٥١)
Allah has said, ‘Do not worship two gods. Indeed He is the One God, so be in awe of Me [alone].’ (51)
وَلَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلدِّينُ وَاصِبًا ۚ أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَتَّقُونَ (٥٢)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him belongs the enduring religion. Will you, then, be wary of other than Allah? (52)
وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِّعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فَإِلَيْهِ تَجْـَٔرُونَ (٥٣)
Whatever blessing you have is from Allah, and when a distress befalls you, you make entreaties to Him. (53)
ثُمَّ إِذَا كَشَفَ ٱلضُّرَّ عَنكُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنكُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٤)
Then, when He removes the distress from you—behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord, (54)
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ۚ فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٥٥)
being unthankful for what We have given them. So let them enjoy. Soon they shall know! (55)

Commentary

One Religion and One Deity!

Following the discussion on monotheism and the knowledge of God through the system of creation, the verses under discussion proceed to negate polytheism (shirk), so that by juxtaposing these two, the truth becomes more apparent.

At the beginning, it says: "And Allah has said: Do not take for yourselves two deities" (وَ قالَ اللَّهُ لا تَتَّخِذُوا إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ‌).

"There is only one Deity" (إِنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

The unity of the system of creation and the unity of the laws governing it are themselves evidence of the unity of the Creator and the unity of the Deity.

Since this is the case, "Then fear Me alone [and dread opposing My command], and not anyone else" (فَإِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ‌).

The placement of the word "Iyyaya" (Me alone) at the beginning of the phrase indicates exclusivity, similar to "Iyyaka na’budu" (You alone we worship); meaning you must fear only My opposition and My punishment.

Interestingly, this verse only negates "two deities," while we know that the Arab polytheists had many idols and deities, and their idol-temples were filled with various types and forms of different idols. This expression may refer to one or all of the following points:

1- The verse states that even worshipping two deities is wrong, let alone worshipping multiple deities. In other words, it mentions the minimum so that the rest are negated even more emphatically, because any number we choose above one must pass through two.

2- Here, all false deities are considered as one category, saying do not place them against the Truth, and do not worship two deities (the Truth and the Falsehood).

3- The Arabs of the Age of Ignorance had, in reality, chosen two deities for themselves: the Deity who is the Creator of the world, namely Allah, in whom they believed; and the other deity whom they considered an intermediary between themselves and Allah and the source of good, blessing, and bounty, namely the idols.

4- It is possible that the above verse is aimed at negating the belief of the "Dualists" (Zoroastrians or Manichaeans) who believed in a god of good and a god of evil, as they had a logic—albeit weak and incorrect—for this dualism, whereas the Arab idol-worshippers lacked even that weak logic.

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, quotes a subtle statement from some sages under this verse: "The Lord commanded you not to worship two deities, yet you have made so many deities for yourself! Your rebellious self (nafs) is one idol, your whims and desires are another idol, and the world and your material goals are other idols; you even prostrate before humans—how then are you a monotheist?!"

Following this command, within three verses, the reason for the monotheism of worship is clarified through four explanations: First, it says: "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (وَ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Should one prostrate before the One who owns the entire universe, or before idols that possess nothing?

Then it adds that not only do the heavens and the earth belong to Him, but "religion and all laws are also perpetually from Him" (وَ لَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً).

When it is proven that the universe belongs to Him and He created the cosmic laws, it is certain that the legislative laws must also be determined by Him, and naturally, obedience is reserved for Him alone.

"Wasib" is originally derived from the root "wasub," meaning permanence, and some have interpreted it to mean "pure" (naturally, something will not endure unless it is pure). It is possible that the expression in the verse refers to both aspects: that at all times, pure religion belongs to Allah. Those who have interpreted "Din" as obedience have considered "Wasib" to mean "obligatory," meaning one must only obey Allah's command.

In a narration, we read that someone asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام about the interpretation of this phrase; the Imam replied: "Wasib means obligatory." [^1] However, it is clear that these meanings are all interconnected.

At the end of this verse, He says: "Is it, then, other than Allah that you fear?" (أَ فَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ تَتَّقُونَ‌).

Can idols cause you any harm? Or grant you any blessing? That you fear opposing them and consider their worship necessary?!

"And whatever blessing you have is from Allah" (وَ ما بِكُمْ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ‌). This is, in fact, the third explanation for the necessity of worshipping the One Deity, Allah. The point is that if the worship of idols is for the sake of gratitude for blessings, the idols have not given you any blessing that requires gratitude; rather, your entire existence is encompassed by Allah's blessings. Despite this, you abandon His servitude and turn to idols! Furthermore, "when distress, misfortunes, calamities, and suffering touch you, you raise your voices in supplication only to Him to remove them" (ثُمَّ إِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فَإِيْلَيْهِ تَجْئَرُونَ‌).

Therefore, if the worship of idols is for the sake of averting harm and solving problems, that too is from Allah, and you have practically proven that in the most difficult moments of life, you abandon everything and go only to His door.

This is the fourth explanation for the issue of the monotheism of worship.

"Taj’arun" is originally from the root "ju’ar," meaning the cry of cattle and wild animals that they let out involuntarily during pain and suffering. It was later used metaphorically for all cries that rise involuntarily from pain and distress. The choice of this expression here specifically conveys the point that when problems become extraordinary and reach the bone, and you cry out in pain involuntarily, do you call upon anyone other than "Allah" at that time?! So why, in times of calm and minor problems, do you cling to idols?!

Yes, in such cases, Allah hears your call, responds to it, and removes your problems. "Then, when He has removed the distress from you, a group of you associate partners with their Lord" and turn back to idols (ثُمَّ إِذا كَشَفَ الضُّرَّ عَنْكُمْ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْكُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ‌). In fact, the Quran points to this subtle point: that the primordial nature (fitrah) of monotheism exists within all of you, but in normal circumstances, veils of negligence, pride, ignorance, prejudice, and superstition cover it. However, when the gale of events and the storms of calamity blow, these veils are cast aside, and the light of fitrah becomes apparent and shines. It is exactly at this moment that you call upon Allah with your whole being and with complete sincerity. Allah also removes the veils of calamity, suffering, and misfortune from you—this removal of the veils of suffering is the result of the removal of the veils of negligence (note that the verse uses the term "kashf al-durr," which means casting aside the veils of problems).

But when the storm subsides and you return to the shore of tranquility, once again that same negligence and pride, and that same polytheism and idol-worship, manifest themselves.

In the last verse under discussion, after mentioning the above logical reasons and the clarification of the truth, it speaks in a threatening tone: "[Let them] be ungrateful for what We have given them; so enjoy yourselves [for a few days with these worldly goods], but soon you will know" what the result and end of your work is (لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ فَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ‌). This is exactly like when a person advises and guides a defiant and deviant individual with various reasons, and finally, because it is possible these counsels will not affect him, he ends his speech with a threatening sentence, saying: "Given what I have said, do whatever you can, but you will soon see the result of your actions."

Therefore, the "Lam" in "Liyakfuru" is the "Lam of Command" (Lam al-Amr), a command expressed for the purpose of threat, just like "Tamatta’u" (enjoy yourselves), which is also a command as a threat. The difference is that "Liyakfuru" is in the third person (absent) and "Tamatta’u" is in the second person (addressed). It is as if He first considers them absent, saying let them go and be ungrateful for all these blessings, and with this threat, a brief state of attention arises in them such that they become addressed, and He says to them: enjoy these worldly blessings for a few days, but one day you will see what a great mistake you have committed and where your end will lead! In reality, this verse is similar to verse 30 of Surah Ibrahim: "Say: Enjoy yourselves, for indeed your destination is the Fire" [^1]!

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 373.

[^1]: A group of commentators have also suggested the possibility that the phrase "Liyakfuru" is the "Lam of Consequence" (Ghayah), the result of the polytheism and ingratitude attributed to them in the previous verse. Thus, the meaning would be: after being saved from the clutches of events and problems, they abandon the path of monotheism and step into polytheism so that they may deny and be ungrateful for Our blessings.

Ayahs

وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ نَصِيبًا مِّمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ تَٱللَّهِ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَفْتَرُونَ (٥٦)
They assign a share in what We have provided them to what they do not know. By Allah, you will surely be questioned concerning what you used to fabricate. (56)
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ٱلْبَنَـٰتِ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۙ وَلَهُم مَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٥٧)
And they attribute daughters to Allah—immaculate is He—while they will have what they desire! (57)
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (٥٨)
When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face becomes darkened, and he chokes with suppressed agony. (58)
يَتَوَٰرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ مِن سُوٓءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُۥ فِى ٱلتُّرَابِ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٥٩)
He hides from the people out of distress at the news he has been brought: shall he retain it in humiliation, or bury it in the ground! Behold! Evil is the judgement that they make. (59)
لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مَثَلُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦٠)
There is an evil description for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, and the loftiest description belongs to Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (60)

Commentary

Where the Birth of a Daughter Was a Disgrace!

Since the preceding verses presented reasoned arguments regarding the negation of polytheism and idol worship, these verses address a portion of the sinister innovations and ugly customs of the polytheists. This serves as further evidence to condemn polytheism and idol worship. In this regard, it points to three categories of these sinister innovations and customs.

First, it says:

"And they assign to that which they do not know a portion of what We have provided them" (وَ يَجْعَلُونَ لِما لا يَعْلَمُونَ نَصِيباً مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ‌) [^1]

[^1]: Commentators have mentioned two interpretations regarding the meaning of the phrase limā lā ya‘lamūn (that which they do not know) and the pronoun within it. First, that the pronoun in "they do not know" refers to the polytheists; meaning the polytheists assigned a portion for idols from which they knew no good or harm (we have chosen this interpretation above). Second, that the pronoun refers to the idols themselves; meaning they assigned a portion for idols that possessed absolutely no consciousness, knowledge, or intellect. However, in this case, a type of contradiction is felt in the verse's expressions, because mā (that which) is usually used for non-rational beings, while the form ya‘lamūn (they know) is typically used for a rational plural (and these two are inconsistent). But according to the first interpretation, mā refers to the idols and lā ya‘lamūn refers to their worshippers.

This "portion" consisted of a part of their camels and livestock and a portion of their crops, as mentioned in Surah Al-An'am, verse 136. The polytheists in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) considered these specific to the idols and spent them in their path, even though no benefit reached them from these idols, nor were they fearful of harm from them such that they would seek to avert danger through this act. This was the most foolish transaction they performed.

Then it adds: "By Allah, you will surely be questioned about what you used to invent!" (تَاللَّهِ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنْتُمْ تَفْتَرُونَ‌).

Following this questioning and the inevitable confession—for there is no choice but to confess there—you will be punished. Therefore, your ugly and sinister act brings loss both in this world, as it consumes a portion of your capital, and loss in the Hereafter.

Their second sinister innovation was that they attributed daughters to Allah, who is pure from any physical contamination, and "they believed that the angels were the daughters of God" (وَ يَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ الْبَناتِ سُبْحانَهُ‌). But when it came to themselves, "they attribute to themselves what they desire" (وَ لَهُمْ ما يَشْتَهُونَ‌).

That is, they were never willing to accept for themselves the very daughters they attributed to Allah; in fact, a daughter was considered a defect, a disgrace, and a source of humiliation and misery for them!

To complete this subject, the next verse points to their third ugly and sinister habit, saying: "And when one of them is informed of [the birth of] a female, his face becomes dark [with distress]" (وَ إِذا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُمْ بِالْأُنْثى‌ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا).

"And he is filled with inward grief and rage" (وَ هُوَ كَظِيمٌ‌) [^1]

The matter does not end there. To escape this perceived disgrace and shame that he wrongly believes has tainted him, "He hides himself from the people because of the ill of that which he has been informed" (يَتَوارى‌ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ مِنْ سُوءِ ما بُشِّرَ بِهِ‌). Still, the issue does not conclude; rather, he is constantly immersed in the thought: "Should he keep it in humiliation or bury it in the dust?" (أَ يُمْسِكُهُ عَلى‌ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُ فِي التُّرابِ‌).

At the end of the verse, it condemns this oppressive, cruel, and inhumane judgment with the utmost clarity, saying: "Unquestionably, evil is what they decide" (أَلا ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ‌).

Finally, it identifies the root of all these pollutions and miseries as being born from a lack of faith in the Hereafter: "For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is the description of evil" (لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ). "But for Allah is the highest attribute" (وَ لِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى‌).

"And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

To the extent that a human draws near to this Great, Mighty, and Wise God, a powerful ray of His exalted attributes—of His knowledge, power, and wisdom—shines upon his soul, and he distances himself from superstitions, wicked deeds, and sinister innovations. However, the further he moves away from Him, the more he becomes trapped in the darkness of ignorance, weakness, abasement, and sinister habits.

Forgetting Allah and forgetting His court of justice is the motive for all baseness, ugliness, deviations, and superstitions. Remembering these two fundamental principles is the primary source of a sense of responsibility and the struggle against ignorance and superstition, and the factor of strength and knowledge.

[^1]: Kaẓīm refers to someone who is filled with sorrow and rage but restrains himself, or as the saying goes, "clenches his teeth in endurance."

Key Points

1- Why Did They Consider Angels to be the Daughters of Allah?

In several verses of the Quran, we read that the Arab polytheists imagined the angels to be the daughters of Allah, or without mentioning an attribution to God, they considered them to be of the female gender. In Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 19, we read: "And they made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females." And in Surah Al-Isra, verse 40, He says: "Has your Lord then chosen for you sons and taken from among the angels daughters?"

This notion might be the remnants of superstitions that reached the pre-Islamic Arabs from past nations. It might also be because angels are hidden from sight, a quality more associated with women. Thus, as some have said, the reason the Arabs refer to the sun (shams) as feminine and the moon (qamar) as masculine is that the sun's disk is so veiled within its blinding light that it is not easy to look at, whereas the moon's disk is completely visible.

There is also the possibility that the delicacy of the angels' existence led to this delusion, as the female is considered a more delicate gender compared to the male.

In any case, this is an old superstition and false notion whose residues, unfortunately, are still seen in the depths of some people's thoughts and even exist in the literature of various languages. For instance, when they want to describe a good woman, they call her an "angel," and the pictures drawn of angels are often in the form of women, whereas angels fundamentally do not have a material body to be male or female.

2- Why Did the Pre-Islamic Arabs Bury Daughters Alive?

It is truly horrifying that a human could trample upon his own emotions to the point of boasting and taking pride in killing a human being in the most hideous way—a human who is his own flesh and blood, a defenseless and weak being—burying her alive with his own hands.

This is not a simple matter for a human, even a semi-savage one, to commit such a terrifying crime; it certainly had social, psychological, and economic roots.

Historians say: The beginning of this ugly practice in the Jahiliyyah was when a war occurred between two groups. The victorious group took the daughters and women of the defeated group as captives. After some time, when peace was established, they wanted to return the prisoners of war to their tribe. However, some of those captive girls had married men from the victorious group; they preferred to stay among the enemy and never return to their tribe. This was extremely hard for the fathers of those girls and became a source of taunting and reproach against them, to the point that some swore that if they were granted a daughter in the future, they would destroy her with their own hands so she would not fall into the hands of the enemy!

Observe well how the most terrifying crimes were committed under the false cover of defending honor and preserving family dignity and status. Eventually, this ugly and shameful innovation was welcomed by a group, and the issue of wa’d (burying daughters alive) became one of the customs of the Jahiliyyah. This is what the Quran severely condemned, saying: "And when the girl [who was] buried alive is asked, for what sin she was killed?" (At-Takwir: 8-9).

It is also possible that the fact that sons were producers and daughters were consumers in those societies contributed to this crime. A son was considered a great asset for them, utilized in raids and in tending camels and the like, whereas daughters were not.

On the other hand, the existence of constant tribal wars and conflicts led to the rapid loss of men and warrior sons, naturally upsetting the balance between the number of girls and boys. The presence of sons became so cherished that the birth of a son was a source of pride, while the birth of a daughter was a source of distress and suffering for a family!

This reached such a point that, according to some commentators, as soon as a woman went into labor, the husband would flee the house lest she bring him a daughter while he was present! Then, if he was informed the newborn was a boy, he would return home with indescribable joy and excitement. But woe if he was informed the newborn was a girl; the fire of rage and sorrow would consume his soul. [^1] The story of wa’d is full of extremely painful and skin-crawling incidents.

Among them, it is narrated that a man came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and embraced Islam—a sincere Islam. One day he came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked: "If I have committed a great sin, will my repentance be accepted?" He replied: "Allah is the Accepter of Repentance and Merciful." He said: "O Messenger of Allah, my sin is very great." He replied: "Woe to you! However great your sin may be, Allah's forgiveness is greater."

He said: "Now that you say this, know that in the Jahiliyyah, I went on a long journey while my wife was pregnant. I returned after four years. My wife came to welcome me. I looked and saw a little girl in the house. I asked, 'Whose daughter is this?' She said, 'She is the daughter of one of the neighbors!'

I thought she would go to her own home an hour later, but to my surprise, I saw she did not leave—unaware that she was my daughter and her mother was hiding this fact lest she be killed by my hand. Finally, I said, 'Tell me the truth, whose daughter is this?' She said, 'Do you remember when you went on the journey I was pregnant? This is the result of that pregnancy and she is your daughter!'

I slept that night in total distress, sometimes falling asleep and sometimes waking up. Morning was near; I rose from the bed and went to the little girl's bedside. She was asleep next to her mother. I pulled her out, woke her up, and said, 'Come with me to the date grove.'

She followed me until we reached the grove. I began digging a pit, and she helped me remove the soil. When the pit was finished, I took her by the armpits and threw her into the middle of the pit..." At this moment, both eyes of the Prophet filled with tears... "Then I placed my left hand on her shoulder so she could not come out, and with my right hand, I threw soil upon her! She was constantly struggling and crying out piteously, 'Father! What are you doing to me?' At this moment, some soil fell onto my beard; she reached out her hand and wiped the dust from my face, but I continued cruelly throwing soil upon her until her last moans were vanished under a massive layer of earth!" Here, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), while extremely upset and distressed and wiping tears from his eyes, said: "If it were not for the fact that Allah's mercy precedes His wrath, it would be necessary to take vengeance upon you immediately!" [^1]

We also read in the accounts of "Qays ibn Asim" (who was among the nobles and chiefs of the Banu Tamim tribe in the Jahiliyyah and embraced Islam after the appearance of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)): "One day he came to the Prophet to perhaps lighten the heavy burden of sin he carried. He said: 'In the past, a group of fathers, due to ignorance and lack of awareness, buried their innocent daughters alive. I too was granted twelve daughters, all of whom I subjected to this sinister fate! When my wife secretly gave birth to my thirteenth daughter and made it appear as if the newborn was stillborn, but had secretly sent her to her relatives, my mind was temporarily at ease regarding this newborn.

But later, when I became aware of the matter, I took her with me to a certain spot and, paying no heed to her entreaties, pleas, and crying, I buried her alive!' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was deeply saddened by hearing this story and, while shedding tears, said: 'He who does not show mercy will not be shown mercy.' Then he turned to Qays and said: 'You have a difficult day ahead of you.' Qays asked: 'What should I do to lighten my burden of sin?' The Prophet replied: 'Free a slave for each daughter you have killed (perhaps your burden of sin will be lightened).'" [^2]

We also read in the accounts of "Sa'sa'ah ibn Najiyah" (the grandfather of the famous poet Farazdaq), who was a free and noble man, that in the era of Jahiliyyah, he fought against many of their ugly customs to the point that he bought 360 daughters from their fathers and saved them from death. In one instance, to save a newborn girl whose father intended to kill her, he gave his riding mount and two camels to the girl's father.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "You have performed a very great deed, and your reward is preserved with Allah!"

Farazdaq took pride in this deed of his ancestor, saying: And from us is he who prevented the buryers of daughters, So he gave life to the one to be buried, so she was not buried. [^3]

We will soon see how Islam put an end to all these atrocities and terrifying crimes and granted woman a status that was unprecedented in history.

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 20, p. 55. [^2]: Al-Quran Yuwakib al-Dahr, Vol. 2, p. 214. [^3]: Qamus al-Rijal, Vol. 5, p. 125.

3- The Role of Islam in Reviving the Value of Woman's Status

The humiliation and breaking of a woman's personality was not only among the pre-Islamic Arabs; rather, among other nations and perhaps even the most civilized peoples of that time, woman had an insignificant personality and was often treated as a commodity rather than a human being. But certainly, the pre-Islamic Arabs carried out this humiliation in more repulsive and terrifying forms.

To the point that they considered lineage to be related only to the man and regarded the mother only as a vessel for holding and nurturing the fetus! As reflected in the famous Jahiliyyah poem: Our sons are the sons of our sons; as for our daughters, Their sons are the sons of distant men.

We also know that they did not recognize any right for women in inheritance and set no limits or boundaries for polygamy. They would marry as easily as drinking water and divorce them just as easily.

But Islam appeared and fought tenaciously against this superstition in its various dimensions. Specifically, the birth of a daughter, which they considered a disgrace, was introduced in Islamic hadiths as the opening of a conduit of Allah's mercy upon the family.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself showed such respect to his daughter, the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra (عليه السلام), that people were amazed. Despite all his status, he would kiss his daughter's hand. Upon returning from a journey, the first person he would visit was his daughter Fatima, and conversely, when he wanted to go on a journey, the last house he would bid farewell to was again the house of Fatima (عليه السلام).

In a hadith, we read that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was informed that Allah had given him a daughter. Suddenly, he looked at the faces of his companions and saw signs of displeasure appearing in them! (As if the residues of Jahiliyyah thoughts had not yet been cleared from their minds). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) immediately said:

"What is the matter with you? She is a fragrant flower (rayhanah) that I smell, and her provision is upon Allah, the Mighty and Majestic!" [^1]

In another hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that he said:

"What excellent children are daughters! They are affectionate, helpful, companions, and pure and purifying." [^2]

In another hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

"Whoever enters the market and buys a gift and carries it to his family is like one who carries charity to a needy people. And he should start with the females before the males, for whoever makes his daughter happy and joyful, it is as if he has freed a slave from the descendants of Isma'il (عليه السلام)." [^3]

In truth, this respect for the personality of woman was the cause of her freedom in society and the end of the era of women's slavery.

Although there is much to say in this regard and it will be discussed under appropriate verses, one cannot easily overlook the reality that, most unfortunately, traces of those same Jahiliyyah thoughts still exist in Islamic societies. There are still many families who are happy at the birth of a boy and upset by a newborn girl, or at least prefer the birth of a boy over a girl.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 15, p. 102. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 15, p. 100. [^3]: Makarim al-Akhlaq, p. 54.

Of course, specific economic and social conditions regarding the status of women in current societies might be one of the causes of such wrong habits and customs. But whatever it is, all true Muslims must fight against this way of thinking and burn its social and economic roots, for Islam does not approve of its followers returning to Jahiliyyah thoughts after fourteen centuries—and this is a type of "secondary Jahiliyyah."

Even in Western societies, where they imagine they grant woman a high status, we see in practice that they have humiliated her such that she has become a worthless doll, a means to extinguish the fire of lust, or a commodity for advertising their goods. [^1]

[^1]: Interestingly, these discussions were coincidentally written on the 20th of Jumada al-Thani, 1401 AH, which was the birth anniversary of the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra (عليه السلام), and the day approved as "Woman's Day" by the Islamic Republic.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم مَّا تَرَكَ عَلَيْهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٦١)
Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it. But He respites them until a specified time; so when their time comes they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. (61)
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ مَا يَكْرَهُونَ وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَأَنَّهُم مُّفْرَطُونَ (٦٢)
They attribute to Allah what they dislike [for themselves], and their tongues assert the lie that the best of rewards [in the Hereafter] will be theirs. Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot and they will be foremost [in entering it]. (62)
تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦٣)
By Allah, We have certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you. But Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them. So he is their master today and there is a painful punishment for them. (63)
وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۙ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦٤)
We did not send down the Book to you except [for the purpose] that you may clarify for them what they differ about, and as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (64)

Commentary

If the Decree Were to Seize Every Drunkard! ...

After mentioning the previous verses which spoke of the horrific crimes of the Arab polytheists regarding ugly innovations and the burying of daughters alive, a question may arise for some: How is it that Allah does not swiftly punish sinful servants despite all this oppression and heinous crime?! The first verse under discussion serves as an answer to this very question. It says:

"And if Allah were to seize mankind for their wrongdoing, He would not leave upon it [the earth] any creature" (وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ مَا تَرَكَ عَلَيْهَا مِنْ دَابَّةٍ) [1]. Dabbah refers to any living and moving being; here, by the context of "for their wrongdoing," it may be an allusion to human beings. That is, if Allah were to hold humans accountable for their oppression, no human would remain on the surface of the globe.

[1]: The pronoun in alayha (upon it) refers to ard (the earth), even though it was not mentioned previously, due to the clarity of the matter; similar instances are abundant in both Arabic and non-Arabic literature.

There is also the possibility that it refers to all living creatures, for we know that the creatures on earth were generally created for mankind, as the Quran says:

هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً "He it is Who created for you all that is on earth" (Al-Baqarah, 29).

When humans perish, the philosophy behind the existence of other creatures also vanishes, and their lineage would be cut off.

Here, a question arises: if we look at the generality and breadth of the verse's concept, the result would be that no non-oppressive human exists on earth, and everyone has committed some form of wrongdoing in their own capacity. Thus, if the punishment were swift and immediate, it would encompass everyone. This is while we know that not only the Prophets and Imams (peace be upon them), who are infallible, are not instances of such wrongdoing, but in every age and time, there is a group of the virtuous, pure, and true mujahideen whose good deeds certainly outweigh their minor sins and who definitely do not deserve destructive punishment.

The answer to this question can be stated as follows: the verse expresses a general rule regarding the type (mankind), not a universal one encompassing every single individual. Similar expressions are seen in both Arabic and non-Arabic literature. We have often heard this famous Persian poem:

If the decree were to seize every drunkard, They would seize everyone who is in the city!

And another poet says: He said: The sober man must carry out the legal punishment on the drunkard, He replied: Bring a sober man, for no one is sober here!

The evidence for this exception is verse 32 of Surah Fatir, which states: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتَابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا فَمِنْهُمْ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سَابِقٌ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ ذَلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ. According to this verse, people are of three groups: a group who are wrongdoers, a group who are moderate (having light sins), and a group who are foremost in good deeds—the virtuous and pure. Certainly, out of these three groups, only the first group is intended by the verse under discussion, not the second and third. Since the first group usually constitutes the majority of societies, mentioning such a generality is not surprising.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the verse has no indication of denying the infallibility of the Prophets, and those who have thought so have not paid attention to other Quranic verses and the existing context of the speech.

Then the Quran proceeds to mention this point: "But He defers them to a stated term [a fixed time]" (وَلَكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

"And when their term comes, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it]" (فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَ لا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ).

Rather, at the exact promised moment, death will overtake them, and it will not be a moment sooner or later.

What is the Stated Term (Ajal Musamma)?

Regarding what is meant by Ajal Musamma, commentators have various explanations. However, considering other Quranic verses, including verse 2 of Surah Al-An'am and verse 34 of Surah Al-A'raf, it appears that it refers to the arrival of death. That is, Allah grants people respite until the end of their lives to complete the proof (itmam al-hujjah), so that perhaps the wrongdoers might think of reforming themselves, reconsider their life's path, and return toward Allah, truth, and justice.

When this respite ends, the command of their death arrives, and from that very moment of death, the punishments and retributions begin.

For further explanation regarding Ajal Musamma, please refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 5, page 148, and Volume 6, page 157.

Once again, with a new expression, the Quran condemns the ugly innovations and superstitions held by the pre-Islamic Arabs (regarding their hatred for female children and the belief that angels are the daughters of God), saying: "And they assign to Allah what they [themselves] dislike" (وَ يَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ما يَكْرَهُونَ).

This is a strange contradiction, and as stated in Surah An-Najm, verse 22: "That is a most unfair division!" If angels are the daughters of God, then it appears a daughter is a good thing; why then are you upset by female children? And if it is bad, why do you attribute it to Allah?

"And their tongues assert the lie that the best [reward] is for them" (وَ تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ الْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ الْحُسْنى).

With what deed do they expect such a reward? By burying innocent, sinless, and defenseless daughters alive? Or by fabricating lies and slanders against the Holy Presence of the Lord?

With which deed?

The word Husna, which is the feminine of Ahsan and means "better" or "best," here means the best of rewards or the best of ends, which this arrogant and misguided people claimed for themselves despite all their crimes. In this case, a question arises: since the pre-Islamic Arabs did not believe in the Resurrection, how could they say such a thing?!

However, it must be noted that not all of them denied the Resurrection absolutely; rather, they denied the physical Resurrection and were astonished that man could return to material life once more. Additionally, this expression might have been in the form of a conditional statement; that is, they would say: "If there is another world, we will have the best reward there." This is the mindset of many tyrants and stubborn deviants who, while being far from Allah, consider themselves the closest to Him and make hollow and ridiculous claims.

Some commentators have also suggested that Husna refers to the blessing of sons, as they considered daughters to be ominous and bad, but considered sons to be good and a high blessing.

However, the first interpretation seems more correct; therefore, it immediately says: "Assuredly, for them is the Fire" (لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ النَّارَ).

Meaning, not only do they not have a good end, but their conclusion is nothing but the fire of Hell.

"And they will be the foremost [sent] into it" (وَ أَنَّهُمْ مُفْرَطُونَ).

Mufratun is derived from the root fart, meaning those who are sent ahead and precede others.

Since after hearing the story of the pre-Islamic Arabs, a question may arise for some: How is it possible for a human to send their own flesh and blood under the soil with such cruelty? Is such a thing even possible? In the next verse, as if answering this question, it says:

"By Allah, We certainly sent [Messengers] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds fair-seeming to them" (تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا إِلى‌ أُمَمٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

Yes, Satan is so skilled in his whisperings that he sometimes makes the ugliest and worst crimes appear so beautiful in the eyes of man that he considers them a source of pride. Just as the pre-Islamic Arab considered burying his daughters alive a badge of honor and praised it as a means of protecting honor and preserving the dignity and reputation of the tribe! He would say: "I send my daughter under the soil with my own hands today so that tomorrow she does not fall into the hands of the enemy in a war."

When the most shameful acts are made possible under the most deceptive masks due to the beautification and whispering of Satan, then the state of other deeds is clear.

And today, we see many examples of these Satanic "beautifications" in the actions of many individuals who justify their thefts, plunderings, transgressions, and crimes with various expressions and place them under deceptive veils.

Then it adds that the current group of polytheists are also followers of the same deviant programs of previous nations whose deeds Satan had beautified for them.

"And Satan is their patron [and guide] today!" (فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ).

And they take inspiration from his guidance.

And for this reason, "a painful punishment awaits them" (وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

In interpreting the phrase "And Satan is their patron today," commentators have various explanations. Perhaps the clearest is what we mentioned above: that this phrase refers to the state of the Arab polytheists in the Age of Ignorance, who followed the programs of previous deviant nations, and Satan is their patron just as he was the patron of the misguided ones of the past [1]. There is also the possibility that the phrase means that remnants of previous deviant nations still exist and continue their deviant path, and Satan is their patron today just as he was in the past.

[1]: However, the requirement of this interpretation is that there be a difference in meaning between the pronoun in "their deeds" (a'malahum) and the pronoun in "their patron" (waliyyuhum); the first referring to previous nations and the second to the polytheists of the Prophet's time. If we assume a phrase is implied, this problem is solved: "These people follow the past nations" (Observe closely).

The final verse under discussion states the purpose of the mission of the Prophets, so it becomes clear that if tribes and nations set aside their whims, desires, and personal tastes and grasp the handle of the Prophets' guidance, no trace of such superstitions, disputes, and contradictory acts would remain. It says:

"And We have not sent down the Book to you except that you may clarify for them that in which they differed" (وَ ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ).

"And as a guidance and a mercy for a people who believe" (وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

It wipes away the whisperings of Satan from their hearts, removes the deceptive veils of the carnal soul and Satanic entities from the face of realities, reveals the superstitions and crimes hidden under deceptive masks, dismantles the disputes that arose in the shadow of whims and desires, ends cruelties, and scatters the light of guidance and mercy everywhere.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٦٥)
Allah sends down water from the sky with which He revives the earth after its death. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who listen. (65)
وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهِۦ مِنۢ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ لَّبَنًا خَالِصًا سَآئِغًا لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ (٦٦)
There is indeed a lesson for you in the cattle: We give you a drink pleasant to those who drink, pure milk, which is in their bellies, between [intestinal] waste and blood. (66)
وَمِن ثَمَرَٰتِ ٱلنَّخِيلِ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَرًا وَرِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
From the fruits of date palms and vines you draw wine and goodly provision. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (67)

Commentary

Water, Fruits, and Livestock

Once again, the discourse returns to describing the various blessings and favors of the Lord. This serves as an emphasis on the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the recognition of God, while also containing an allusion to the matter of the Resurrection (Ma'ad). Furthermore, by mentioning these blessings, the sense of gratitude in His servants is stimulated, thereby drawing them closer to Allah. These three aspects clarify the connection between these verses and the preceding ones.

On the other hand, the final verse of the previous discussion spoke of the revelation of the verses of the Quran, which are the life of the human soul; the first verse under discussion speaks of the descent of rain from the sky, which is the source of life for the human body.

First, it says: "And Allah sends down water from the sky and gives life thereby to the earth after its death" (وَ اللَّهُ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها).

"Indeed, in that is a clear sign [of God's greatness] for a people who listen" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ‌).

The issue of the life and vitality of the earth through the descent of rainwater from the sky has been mentioned in numerous verses of the Quran. Parched, silent, and soulless lands—which drought has sometimes so incapacitated that one would never believe plants once grew in their embrace or that a living being would ever be born from them—are set into motion by a few successive rains followed by the life-giving rays of the sun. It is as if a sleeper awakens, or more accurately, a corpse returns to life by the Christ-like breath of the rain. Various flowers and plants appear, followed by insects, birds, and all kinds of animals coming from every direction, raising the murmur of life and existence.

In short, in that land which was previously dead and silent, such a clamor arises that it leaves man bewildered, and this is truly one of the masterpieces of creation.

It is both a sign of the power and greatness of the Creator, a proof of the possibility of the Resurrection—showing how the dead will once again don the raiment of life—and a great blessing among the blessings of Allah, especially a blessing like rain for which the servants bear the least amount of trouble to produce!

After the blessing of water, which is certainly the primary foundation of life, the verse points to the blessing of the existence of livestock and specifically a very useful food substance like milk obtained from them, saying: "And indeed, for you in grazing livestock is a great lesson" (وَ إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً).

What lesson could be greater than this: "We give you to drink from what is in their bellies—from between digested food and blood—pure milk, palatable to drinkers" (نُسْقِيكُمْ مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِهِ مِنْ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَ دَمٍ لَبَناً خالِصاً سائِغاً لِلشَّارِبِينَ‌).

"Farth" (فرث) linguistically refers to the digested food inside the stomach. As soon as it reaches the intestines, its vital substances are absorbed by the body, and its waste is sent out. While this digested food is inside the stomach, it is called "farth," and when its waste is expelled, it is called "rawth" (dung).

We know that a small amount of food, such as certain sugars and also water, is absorbed into the body directly through the stomach walls. However, the major portion enters the blood once the digested food is transferred to the intestines. We also know that milk is secreted from special glands inside the udder, and its primary components are taken from the blood and fat-producing glands.

In this way, this clean, pure, white substance—this nourishing and palatable food—is obtained from between digested food mixed with waste and from amidst the blood. This is truly wondrous: a source so contaminated and repulsive, yet a product so pure, beautiful, delightful, and palatable!

After the animals and their milk, the verse addresses a portion of the favors found in plants, saying:

"And from the fruits of the palm trees and grapevines, Allah has granted you a bountiful food, from which you sometimes derive harmful substances and make intoxicants, and sometimes you obtain a good and pure provision" (وَ مِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَ الْأَعْنابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَ رِزْقاً حَسَناً).

"Indeed, in that is another sign of the Lord's power for those who use their intellect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ‌).

"Sakar" (سكر), although it has various meanings linguistically, refers here to intoxicants and alcoholic beverages, which is its most well-known meaning.

It is clear that the Quran in this verse never gave permission to produce wine from dates and grapes; rather, by placing "intoxicants" in contrast to "good provision" (rizqan hasana), it made a brief allusion to the prohibition of wine and its undesirability. Therefore, we do not need to say that this verse was revealed before the prohibition of wine and implies its permissibility; on the contrary, the verse hints at its being forbidden and is perhaps the first warning regarding the prohibition of intoxicants.

In fact, it is as if in a parenthetical statement, the Holy Quran intends to mention the misuse of divine blessings while enumerating them.

Key Points

1- How is Milk Produced?

As we read in the above verses, the Holy Quran states that milk emerges from between "farth" (digested food inside the stomach) and "dam" (blood).

"Modern physiology" has proven that when food is digested in the stomach and prepared for absorption, it is placed over a very vast and extensive surface inside the stomach and intestines in front of millions of capillaries. These capillaries absorb the useful and necessary elements and deliver them to a deep-rooted "tree" that culminates at the nipple.

The mother eats food; its essence enters the blood. The terminal branches of the blood and vascular system are placed adjacent to the terminal branches of the "fetus's" circulatory system, and as long as the baby is in the mother's womb, it is nourished in this way. However, as soon as it is separated from the mother, the needle of this nourishing compass turns toward the mother's nipple. in this state, the mother can no longer deliver her blood to the infant's blood; therefore, a change of state and purification must occur to make the new product palatable and compatible for her child. This is where milk is created: from between "farth" and "dam," meaning from between what she eats (which turns into farth) and what subsequently turns into blood.

Coincidentally, the composition of milk is something intermediate between blood and farth; it is neither purified blood nor merely digested food—it goes beyond the latter but does not reach the former!

To produce the protein substances of milk, the breasts use only the amino acids stored in the body.

Some components of milk do not exist in the blood and are produced in the mammary glands (such as casein).

Other parts present in milk enter the milk through secretion from the blood plasma without change (such as various vitamins, table salt, and phosphates).

Other substances, such as the sugar found in milk (lactose), are taken from the sugar in the blood, upon which the breasts perform activity and play an effective role in this transformation.

As you observe, the production of milk is the result of the absorption of nutrients by the blood and the direct connection of the blood with the mammary glands. But interestingly, neither the specific odor of farth nor the particular color of blood is transferred to the milk; rather, it secretes from the nipple with a fresh color, aroma, and scent.

It is noteworthy that scientists say that to produce one liter of milk in the breast, at least five hundred liters of blood must pass through this organ so it can extract the necessary materials for that one liter of milk from the blood. And to produce one liter of blood in the vessels, a large amount of nutrients must pass through the intestines. This is where the meaning of "from between digested food and blood" (مِنْ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَ دَمٍ‌) becomes fully clear. [^1]

2- Important Nutrients in Milk

Milk is rich in various vital substances which together form a complete nutritional set.

The minerals in milk include sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, and a little iron, as well as phosphorus, chlorine, iodine, and sulfur.

Gases such as oxygen, nitrogen, and carbonic acid also exist in milk.

The sugar content of milk is sufficient and is in the form of "lactose."

The vitamins dissolved in milk are vitamins B, P, A, and D. Today it is proven that if the animal has grazed well, all types of vitamins are present in its milk, the description of all of which does not fit in this brief space. It is also almost universally agreed that fresh milk is considered a complete food.

Perhaps for this reason, we read in a Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Nothing can fully take the place of food and drink except milk."

We also read in narrations that milk increases human intellect, brings clarity to the mind, enhances eyesight, removes forgetfulness, strengthens the heart, and firms the back (and we know each of these effects is closely related to the vital substances present in milk). [^1]

3- Milk: A Pure and Palatable Food

In the above verses, emphasis is placed particularly on milk being "pure" (khalis) and "palatable" (sa'igh). At first glance, it is clear to everyone that milk is a low-volume, high-energy food, pure from redundant materials, and at the same time so palatable that it is useful and compatible for every human at every age, from infancy to old age. For these reasons, many patients use this food; specifically, they attribute an extraordinary effect to it for bone growth, and for this reason, it is recommended in cases of bone fractures.

One of the meanings of "khulus" (purity) is "connection," and perhaps because of this meaning, some consider this Quranic expression regarding milk to be an allusion to its extraordinary effect on bone formation.

Interestingly, this meaning is also clearly seen in Islamic laws regarding the issue of rada' (nursing).

On one hand, we see jurists saying: if a child drinks enough milk from a mother such that its bones become firm and its flesh grows, this child becomes mahram (a close relative) to that mother (and also to the woman's husband and some of their other relatives). On the other hand, they say that 15 consecutive feedings, or even one full day and night of continuous nursing, causes this mahram relationship. If we combine these two statements, does it not imply that even one day and night of nourishment from milk has an effect on strengthening bones and growing flesh?! It is also necessary to mention that in Islamic instructions, great importance is given to "colostrum" milk, which is called "laba" (لباء) in Arabic, to the extent that we read in Islamic legal books that the child's life depends on it. For this reason, giving colostrum to the child is considered among the obligations. [^1] Perhaps it is also for this reason that we read regarding Moses (عليه السلام) in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 7: "And We inspired the mother of Moses, 'Suckle him; but when you fear for him, cast him into the river...'"

[^1]: Adapted from the book Biochemistry and Medicine, and the book The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 6, pp. 71-77. [^1]: In the discussions related to milk, The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 6, has been utilized. [^1]: Sharh al-Lum'ah, Book of Marriage, Laws of Children, including Nursing.

Ayahs

وَأَوْحَىٰ رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلنَّحْلِ أَنِ ٱتَّخِذِى مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا وَمِنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ (٦٨)
And your Lord inspired the bee [saying]: ‘Make your home in the mountains, and on the trees and the trellises that they erect. (68)
ثُمَّ كُلِى مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ فَٱسْلُكِى سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلًا ۚ يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بُطُونِهَا شَرَابٌ مُّخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ فِيهِ شِفَآءٌ لِّلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٦٩)
Then eat from every [kind of] fruit and follow meekly the ways of your Lord.’ There issues from its belly a juice of diverse hues, in which there is a cure for the people. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect. (69)

Commentary

Your Lord Inspired the Bee!

Here, the tone of the Qur'an changes in a wondrous manner. While continuing the previous discussions regarding various Divine blessings and explaining the secrets of creation, it speaks of the "Honeybee" (Nahl) and subsequently honey itself. However, this is presented in the form of a Divine mission and a mysterious inspiration, which has been termed "Revelation" (Wahy). It first says:

"And your Lord inspired the bee: 'Build for yourself houses in the mountains and in the trees and in what they [people] construct.'" (وَ أَوْحى‌ رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ أَنِ اتَّخِذِي مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً وَ مِنَ الشَّجَرِ وَ مِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- The Word Wahy and Its Various Meanings

In this verse, we encounter several expressions that all deserve careful attention:

1- The word Wahy (Revelation) originally, as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, means a "quick sign." Later, it came to mean "secretly conveying a message." However, in the Holy Qur'an, Wahy is used in various senses, all of which trace back to that original meaning:

Among these is the "Revelation of Prophethood," which is mentioned frequently in the Qur'an. In verse 51 of Surah Ash-Shura, we read: وَ ما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً ... "It is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation..." It also means "Inspiration," whether it be conscious inspiration (in the case of humans), such as: وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ أُمِّ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ فَإِذا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِي الْيَمِ‌ (Al-Qasas: 7): "We inspired the mother of Moses: 'Suckle him; but when you fear for him, cast him into the river...'" Or whether it be in the form of unconscious and instinctive inspiration, as we read regarding the honeybee in the verses above; for it is certain that Wahy here refers to the command of instinct and the unconscious motives that Allah has created within various living creatures.

Furthermore, Wahy means a "sign" or "gesture," as we read in the story of Zechariah عليه السلام: فَأَوْحى‌ إِلَيْهِمْ أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَ عَشِيًّا "So he signaled to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening" (Maryam: 11).

It also means conveying a message or news secretly, as we read in verse 112 of Surah Al-An'am: يُوحِي بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ غُرُوراً: "The human and jinn devils inspire one another with flowery speech as a delusion."

2- Is Instinctive Inspiration Exclusive to Honeybees?

The question arises here: instincts or instinctive inspiration are not exclusive to honeybees and exist in all animals; why is this expression used only here?

The answer becomes clear by considering one point: today, as the life of honeybees has been deeply studied by scientists, it has been proven that this amazing insect possesses such a wondrous civilization and social life that it surpasses human civilization and social life in many respects.

Of course, some aspects of its remarkable life were known in the past, but never were its various dimensions—each more astonishing than the last—recognized as they are today. The Qur'an, in a miraculous way, alluded to this subject with the word "Wahy" to clarify the reality that the life of honeybees should never be compared to cattle, livestock, and the like. Perhaps this serves as a motivation for us to step into the mysterious world of this extraordinary insect and become acquainted with the greatness and power of its Creator. This is the secret behind the change in the tone of the discourse in this verse compared to the preceding ones.

3- House-Building Before Everything Else

The first mission of the bees mentioned in this verse is the mission of house-building. This is perhaps because the issue of suitable housing is the first condition of life, following which other activities become possible. Or it may be because the construction of the hexagonal cells of the honeybees—which has continued in this same form for perhaps millions of years in the East and West of the world—is among the most wondrous programs of their lives [1], and perhaps even more wondrous than the production of honey itself. How do they prepare this special waxy substance? And how do they build these hexagonal houses with such extraordinary delicacy and precise engineering? Houses whose plan is highly calculated and whose angles are perfectly precise and uniform. Fundamentally, to make full use of a surface such that no part of it is wasted or creates narrow, dark corners, there is no better way than choosing hexagonal cells with equal angles. Additionally, this type of structure possesses a particular strength.

4- The Manner of Choosing These Houses

The selection of these houses, as the Qur'an states in the above verse, is sometimes in the mountains, amidst impassable crags and specific crevices in rocks that are ready for this purpose; sometimes amidst the branches of trees; and sometimes in hives that humans provide for them upon trellises.

[1]: So far, 4,500 types of wild honeybees have been identified, but the wonder is that all of them share the same type of migration, hive-building, and sucking and feeding from flowers (The First University, vol. 5, p. 55).

Incidentally, it is understood from this expression that the nest and beehive should be in an elevated spot on a mountain, tree, or trellis so that it can be utilized effectively.

Then the second mission of the honeybee begins, as the Qur'an says: "Then eat from all the fruits" (ثُمَّ كُلِي مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ‌).

"And follow the ways of your Lord made easy [for you]" (فَاسْلُكِي سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلًا).

"Dhulul" is the plural of Dhalul, meaning submissive and tamed. Describing the paths as "Dhulul" is because these routes have been so precisely determined that they are completely tamed and submissive to the honeybees, the details of which we will mention later.

Finally, it describes the last stage of their mission (as a result) in this way: "There emerges from their bellies a drink, varying in colors" (يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بُطُونِها شَرابٌ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوانُهُ‌).

"In it is healing for people" (فِيهِ شِفاءٌ لِلنَّاسِ‌).

"Indeed in that—the life program of the honeybees and the gift they bring to the human world, which is food, healing, and a life lesson—is a clear sign of the greatness and power of the Lord for those who give thought" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

Key Points

In this verse as well, there are many meaningful and noteworthy points:

1- What is Honey Made From?

Honeybees usually suck a special sugary substance placed at the base of flowers and collect it. However, apiologists say the work of bees is not limited to using this sugary substance at the base of flowers; rather, they occasionally use the ovaries of flowers, petioles, and fruits, all of which the Qur'an refers to as "from all the fruits" (min kulli al-thamarat).

An extraordinary sentence quoted from Maurice Maeterlinck, the biologist, reveals the importance of the Qur'anic expression when he says: "If today the honeybee (both wild and domestic) were to disappear, one hundred thousand types of our plants, flowers, and fruits would perish. And who is to say that our civilization itself would not fundamentally collapse?" [1] (Because the role of honeybees in transferring the pollen of male flowers and fertilizing female plants, followed by the development of fruits, is so immense that some scientists consider it even more important than the bees' honey production).

In reality, what the honeybees eat are potentially the various fruits that take shape with their help; thus, how meaningful is the phrase "all the fruits."

2- Secure and Tamed Paths!

Through studies conducted on this insect, apiologists have concluded that in the morning, a group of bees acting as flower scouts leave the hive, discover various flower-rich areas, return to the hive, and inform the others. They even specify the direction and the distance from the hive to their fellow citizens.

[1]: The First University, vol. 5, p. 55.

To reach the flower regions, bees sometimes mark their path, and by dispersing various scents along the route and the like, they define the path so clearly that it is rare for a bee to become lost! The phrase "follow the ways of your Lord made easy" seems to refer to this reality.

3- Where is Honey Produced?

Perhaps many people still think that the honeybees' work program is to suck the nectar of flowers, collect it in their mouths, and store it in the hive. However, this is not the case. They send the flower nectars to certain cavities within their bodies that apiologists call the "crop" (honey stomach). There, which acts as a small chemical factory, various transformations occur, and the flower nectar is converted into honey. Then, the bee regurgitates the produced honey from its body.

It is remarkable that Surah An-Nahl is a Meccan Surah, and we know that in the region of Mecca, there were neither flowers and plants nor honeybees. Yet the Qur'an speaks of it with such precision and delicacy, using an interesting expression like: "There emerges from their bellies a drink, varying in colors," even alluding to the intricacies of honey production.

4- The Various Colors of Honey

The color of honey varies depending on which flower or fruit the bee has landed upon and utilized. For this reason, in some cases, it is dark brown, sometimes silvery white, sometimes colorless, sometimes yellow, sometimes transparent, sometimes dark, sometimes golden yellow, sometimes date-colored, and even sometimes leaning toward black!

This diversity of colors, in addition to indicating the variety of sources for obtaining honey, is also considered a variety for tastes and preferences. Today it is proven that the color of food is very effective in stimulating human appetite. The ancients also seemingly recognized this psychological matter, as they colored their foods with saffron, turmeric, and other dyes to encourage their guests to eat through visual appeal as well. In books of nutrition, there is much discussion on this topic, the full citation of which is not suitable for our concise exegetical discussion.

5- Honey: An Extraordinary Healing Substance

We know that within flowers and plants, life-giving medicines are hidden, about which our knowledge—despite our extensive information on herbal medicines—is still meager. Interestingly, scientists have found through experience that bees act so skillfully when making honey that the therapeutic and medicinal properties of the plants are completely transferred to the honey and preserved!

For this reason, honey possesses many of the therapeutic properties of the flowers and plants on earth in a "living" state.

Scientists have stated many properties for honey, which are both therapeutic, preventative, and strengthening.

Honey is quickly absorbed into the blood and is therefore energizing and extraordinarily effective in blood formation.

Honey prevents infection in the stomach and intestines.

Honey is a reliever of constipation.

Honey has a very desirable effect on those who have difficulty falling asleep (provided it is consumed in small amounts, as large amounts reduce sleep).

Honey has a significant effect on relieving fatigue and muscle tension.

If honey is given to pregnant women, the nervous systems of their children will be strengthened.

Honey raises the calcium level in the blood.

Honey is beneficial for those with weak digestive systems; it is especially recommended for those suffering from flatulence.

Because honey enters the body's metabolism stage quickly, it can immediately create energy and restore strength.

Honey is effective in strengthening the heart.

Honey is a good aid for treating lung diseases.

Due to its germicidal properties, honey is useful for those suffering from diarrhea.

Honey is considered an effective factor in treating gastric and duodenal ulcers.

Honey is recognized as a medicine for treating rheumatism, lack of muscle growth, and nervous disorders.

Honey is effective for relieving coughs and clears the voice.

In short, the therapeutic properties of honey are more than can be contained in this brief summary.

Additionally, medicines are made from honey for skin softness and facial beauty, longevity, inflammation of the mouth and tongue, eye inflammation, fatigue, skin cracking, and the like.

The substances and vitamins present in honey are numerous. Among the minerals: iron, phosphorus, potassium, iodine, magnesium, lead, copper, sulfur, nickel, zinc, sodium, and others. Among the organic substances: gum, pollen, lactic acid, formic acid, citric acid, tartaric acid, and aromatic oils. Among the vitamins, it contains the six vitamins: A, B, C, D, K, and E. Some also believe in the presence of vitamin PP in honey.

Honey is at the service of human treatment, hygiene, and beauty.

In Islamic narrations, the therapeutic property of honey has also been emphasized. In several narrations from Imam Ali عليه السلام, Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, and some other Infallibles عليهم السلام, it is narrated that they said: Ma istashfa al-nasu bi-mithli al-'asal: "People do not seek healing with anything like honey." [1] And in another expression: Lam yastashfi maridun bi-mithli sharbati 'asal: "No sick person has been treated with anything like a drink of honey." [2] Also, in hadiths, honey is recommended for treating stomach pain, and it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever drinks honey once every month, seeking the healing mentioned in the Qur'an, Allah will heal him from seventy-seven types of diseases!" [3] But of course, since every general rule has an exception, there are rare cases where drinking honey is discouraged.

6- For People (lin-nas)

Interestingly, apiologists say that for a honeybee to satisfy its hunger, it is enough to suck two or three flowers, whereas it lands on an average of two hundred and fifty flowers per hour and travels kilometers. It is so industrious that during its short lifespan, each produces a significant amount of honey.

[1] & [2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, vol. 17, pp. 73-75. [3]: Safinat al-Bihar, vol. 2, p. 190.

In any case, this remarkable effort and activity is proof that it does not work only for itself, but rather, as the Qur'an says, lin-nas—it strives for all people.

7- Other Interesting Matters Regarding Honey

It is a proven fact today that honey never spoils; that is, it is among the foods that always reach us fresh and "alive," and it never even loses its existing vitamins. Scientists attribute this to the presence of "potassium," which exists abundantly in it and prevents the growth of microbes. Additionally, it contains some disinfectant substances such as "formic acid." Therefore, honey has both the property of preventing microbial growth and is a germicide. For this reason, the ancient Egyptians, who were aware of this property of honey, used it for mummifying their dead!

Honey should not be stored in metal containers; this is what scientists tell us. Interestingly, when the Qur'an speaks of honeybees, it says: "houses in the mountains and in the trees and in what they construct," meaning it limits the houses of honeybees only to stones and wood.

Another important point is that to achieve the therapeutic and hygienic properties of honey, it should never be heated or used in a cooked form within other foods. Some believe the Qur'an's expression "drink" (sharab) regarding honey refers to this very point—that it should be drunk.

It is also noteworthy that the sting of honeybees also has therapeutic properties. Of course, the honeybee, with its delicate nature, does not sting anyone without reason; it is we who provoke them and force them to sting.
