Among these reasons is that honeybees are bothered by bad odors, and whenever a beekeeper approaches the hive with dirty hands or foul-smelling clothes, they will not leave him without a sting. Especially if the beekeeper had previously put his hand in another hive and the scent of a foreign hive is on his hand, they will attack him as soon as he puts his hand in another hive! For this reason, he must wash his hands thoroughly beforehand.

Of course, the bee dies when it stings; therefore, its stinging is a type of suicide! The use of honeybee stings for diseases such as rheumatism, malaria, nerve pain, and some other ailments must be according to a program specified by experts in this field; otherwise, bee stings may occasionally be harmful or even dangerous!

A sting from one or a few bees is usually tolerable, but two hundred to three hundred bees cause poisoning and heart distress, and if their number reaches five hundred, it will cause paralysis of the respiratory system and possibly death.

8- The Extraordinary Life of Honeybees

In the past to a lesser extent, and today much more due to the persistent studies of scientists, it has been proven that honeybees possess a very organized life coupled with planning, division of labor, responsibility, and a very precise system.

The city of bees is among the cleanest, most organized, and most industrious cities. It is a city that, unlike all human cities, has no unemployed or poor members and enjoys a brilliant civilization. Signs of deviant or irresponsible individuals are rarely seen in this city, and if bees rarely use foul-smelling or harmful flowers outside the hive due to negligence, they are inspected at the very gate of the hive and sentenced to death in a field trial!

Maurice Maeterlinck, the Belgian biologist who conducted extensive studies on the life of honeybees, says regarding the extraordinary system governing their city:

"The queen (or more correctly, the mother of the hive) in the city of honeybees is not a ruler as we imagine; rather, she too, like other individuals of this city, is subject to general rules and systems.

We do not know where these laws and systems are enacted, and we are waiting for the day we might be able to discover this and recognize the enactors of these regulations. But for now, we temporarily call it the 'spirit of the hive'! The queen, like others, obeys the spirit of the hive.

We do not know where the spirit of the hive is, or in which inhabitant of the city of honey it resides.

But we know that the spirit of the hive is not similar to the instinct of birds, and we also know that the spirit of the hive is not a habit or a blind will of the honeybee race. The spirit of the hive determines the duty of each inhabitant of the city according to their talent and assigns everyone to a task.

The spirit of the hive commands the engineer, builder, and worker bees to build houses. The spirit of the hive, on a specific day and hour, commands all inhabitants of the city to migrate from the city and entrust themselves to unknown events and various hardships to obtain a new home and shelter!

We cannot understand in which consultative assembly the laws of the city of honey, enacted by the spirit of the hive, are discussed and the decision for their implementation is made, and who it is that issues the command to move on a specific day?

Yes, in the hive, the preparations for migration are made to obey the God who has taken the fate of the honeybee into His hand." [1]

If the aforementioned scientist speaks of this matter with ambiguity due to the residues of the materialist school in his thought, we, with the guidance of the Qur'an, understand very well where all these voices and programs come from and who the organizer and commander is! What a beautiful expression the Qur'an has: "And your Lord inspired the bee..."! Nothing more eloquent, comprehensive, or expressive can be imagined.

Although what remains unsaid about honeybees is far more than what we have said, our exegetical method does not allow us to pursue the above subject further than this [2]. But is not even this much that we have said sufficient for those who are people of thought and reflection to realize the greatness of the Lord? Yes: "Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought."

[1]: Summarized from pages 35 and 36 of The Life of the Bee by Maeterlinck. [2]: In the above discussions regarding honeybees and the properties of honey itself, use was made of the books The First University and the Last Prophet, The Life of the Bee by Maeterlinck, Wonders of the Animal World, and some other books.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَىْ لَا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ (٧٠)
Allah has created you, then He takes you away, and there are some among you who are relegated to the nethermost age so that he knows nothing after [having possessed] some knowledge. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-powerful. (70)
وَٱللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ فَمَا ٱلَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا۟ بِرَآدِّى رِزْقِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ ۚ أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٧١)
Allah has granted some of you an advantage over others in [respect of] provision. Those who have been granted an advantage do not give over their provision to their slaves so that they become equal in its respect. What, do they deny the blessing of Allah? (71)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٢)
Allah made for you mates from your own selves and appointed for you children and grandchildren from your mates, and We provided you with all the good things. What, do they believe in falsehood while they deny the blessing of Allah? (72)

Commentary

The Source of Differences in Provisions

Since the preceding verses explained a portion of the significant divine blessings and the various bounties created in the world of plants and animals—so that through this path, people might become acquainted with the Creator of all these blessings and their precise system—the verses under discussion pursue the issue of proving the One Creator through another path: the transformations and changes in blessings that are beyond human control and demonstrate that they are ordained from another source.

First, It says: "And Allah created you" (وَ اللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ).

"Then He takes your soul," and your death arrives (ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ).

Both life is from His side, and so is death, so that you may know it is not you who are the creators of death and life.

The length of your lifespan is also not in your control; some pass away in youth or near old age, but "some of you are returned to the most abject and worst years of life, which is extreme old age" (وَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلى‌ أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ) [^1]. "And the result of this long life will be that after having possessed knowledge, insight, and awareness,

[^1]: Ardhal is derived from the root radhl, meaning something base and undesirable. What is meant by ardhal al-umur (the most abject part of life) are the very advanced years where weakness and forgetfulness encompass the human being to the extent that he is unable to perform his basic needs; for this reason, the Quran has counted them as the undesirable years of life. Some commentators have estimated it at 75 years of age, some at 90, and some at 95, but the truth is that it has no fixed age and varies relative to different individuals.

he will know nothing" and consigns everything to oblivion (لِكَيْ لا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْئاً) [^1]. Just like the beginning of life and the years of childhood which were entirely characterized by forgetfulness, oblivion, and lack of awareness. Indeed, "Allah is All-Knowing, All-Powerful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ).

All powers are at His disposal; He grants as much as He deems wise and takes away whenever He sees fit.

In the next verse, It continues that even your provisions are not in your hands: "And it is Allah who has favored some of you over others in provision" (وَ اللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ فِي الرِّزْقِ). "But those who have been favored are not willing, due to narrow-mindedness, to give of what they possess to those whom their right hands possess (their slaves) and make them partners in their wealth so that they may be equal with them" (فَمَا الَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا بِرَادِّي رِزْقِهِمْ عَلى‌ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ).

Some commentators have suggested that this sentence might refer to some of the foolish acts of the polytheists, who would designate a share of their livestock and crops for their idols and deities, while these worthless stones and wood played not the slightest role in their lives; yet they were not willing to place that same share at the disposal of the wretched slaves

[^1]: The phrase "so that he will not know anything after having known" might be the ultimate end and result of reaching advanced years of life, meaning that in these years, the human brain and nerves lose the power of concentration and retention, and forgetfulness and ignorance dominate the human being. It is also possible that it carries a causal meaning, i.e., Allah brings them to advanced years for the reason of allowing the state of forgetfulness to overcome them so that other humans may know they possess nothing of their own.

who served them night and day.

Is the Creation of Differences Compatible with Justice?

Here, the question arises: Is the creation of differences and variation in terms of sustenance and provision among people compatible with the principles of the Lord's justice and the equality that should govern the system of human societies?

In response to this question, two points must be carefully noted:

1- Undoubtedly, a significant part of the difference that exists among humans in terms of material benefits and income is related to the difference in their talents. This difference in physical and spiritual talents, which becomes the source of difference in the quantity and quality of economic activities, makes the benefit of some greater and the income of some less.

Of course, there is no doubt that sometimes events which appear accidental to us cause some to attain greater bounties, but these can be considered exceptional cases. What constitutes the main foundation and criterion in most cases is that very difference in the quantity or quality of efforts (of course, our subject of discussion is a healthy society free from exploitation and oppressive profiteering, not deviant societies that are apart from the laws of creation and the human system).

Even those whom we sometimes perceive as clumsy or incompetent and are surprised by their considerable incomes—if we reflect more deeply upon their bodies, souls, and character, and cast away judgments originating from superficial studies—we will see that they often possessed a point of strength that led them to where they reached (again, we repeat, our subject of discussion is a healthy society distant from exploitation).

In any case, this difference in incomes stems from the difference in talents, which are also divine gifts; it may be acquired in some cases, but in others, it is certainly non-acquired. Therefore, even in an economically healthy society, the difference in incomes is undeniable, unless we could create "template" humans—identical in form, color, and talent—who have no difference whatsoever, and that would be the beginning of headaches and problems!

2- Consider the body of a human, or the limbs of a tree, or a rosebush. Is it possible for this harmonious structure to emerge with equality among the members and limbs in every respect? Can the resistance and capacity of tree roots be identical in every way to the delicate petals of blossoms, or the heel bone to the fine membranes of the retina? And if we could make them identical, do you think we would have done something correct?! If we move past "slogans" devoid of "sense," suppose we could one day create those template and imaginary humans in every respect and fill the Earth with five billion people identical in shape, stature, taste, thought, and likeness in every way—just like cigarettes ordered from a factory.

Would a good life be the lot of humans on that day? Certainly not; a hell would emerge where everyone would be in hardship. Everyone would move in one direction, everyone would want one thing, everyone would seek one post, everyone would like one type of food, and everyone would want to perform one single task.

It is obvious that the lifespan of such a life would end very quickly, and assuming it remained, it would be a boring, soulless, and monotonous life that would not differ much from death.

Therefore, the existence of differences in talents and what is necessitated by these differences is of the utmost necessity for maintaining the social order and even for nurturing various talents, and false slogans can never block this reality.

But let this speech not be interpreted as us accepting a class-based society and a system of exploitation and colonialism; no, never. What is meant are natural differences, not artificial ones—differences that are simultaneously complementary and supportive of one another, not creating a barrier in the path of each other's progress or engaging in aggression and transgression.

Class distinction (note that by "classes" we mean the technical term, i.e., the exploiting class and the exploited class) is never in accord with the system of creation. What is in accord and harmony with the system of creation is the difference in talents, efforts, and endeavors, and between these two, there is a distance as vast as that between heaven and earth (reflect carefully).

In other words, the difference in talents must be employed in a constructive path, just like the difference in the structure of the organs of a body or the parts of a flower; despite their differences, they are assistants to one another, not hindrances.

In short: the difference in talents and incomes must not lead to abuse in the way of creating a class society [^1], and for this reason, at the end of the verse under discussion, He says: "Do they then deny the favor of Allah?" (أَ فَبِنِعْمَةِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ).

This points to the fact that these differences in their natural form (not the artificial and oppressive form)

[^1]: Regarding the philosophy of the difference in talents and the benefits resulting from them, we have also discussed in detail in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 365 onwards.

are among the blessings of Allah created for the preservation of the human social order.

The last verse under discussion, which like the previous two verses begins with the word "Allah" and describes the blessings of God, refers to His gifts in terms of human resources, assistants, and helpers, as well as pure provisions. It completes these three links of blessings mentioned in these three verses: starting from the system of life and death, then explaining the difference in provisions and talents which is the system of "diversity of life," and ending with the verse under discussion which regards the system of human reproduction and pure provisions.

He says: "And Allah has made for you spouses from among yourselves" (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً).

Spouses who are both a source of tranquility for the soul and body and a means for the continuation of the lineage.

Therefore, He immediately adds: "and has made for you from your spouses sons and grandsons" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَ حَفَدَةً).

"Hafadah" is the plural of "hafid", which originally means someone who cooperates quickly without expecting a reward. However, in the verse under discussion, according to many commentators, it refers to grandsons, and some have specifically said maternal grandsons.

Others believe that banun refers to small children and hafadah to grown children who can help and cooperate.

And some have considered it to mean any kind of assistant and helper, whether children or otherwise [^1].

[^1]: In this case, hafadah should not be conjoined to banin, but rather conjoined to azwajan; however, this type of conjunction is contrary to the apparent meaning. The apparent meaning is that it is conjoined to banin (reflect carefully).

But the first meaning, i.e., grandsons, seems closest to the context here, even though the original concept of hafadah is broad, as we mentioned.

In any case, undoubtedly the existence of human resources such as children, grandsons, and spouses around anyone is a great blessing for them, supporting them both spiritually and materially.

Then He says: "And He has provided you with good things" (وَ رَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ).

"Tayyibat" (good things) here has a broad meaning that includes every kind of pure provision, whether material or spiritual, individual or social.

And at the end, as a general conclusion from this discussion, He says: Do they, despite seeing all these signs of Allah's greatness and power, and with all these bounties bestowed upon them from His side, still go after idols? "Do they then believe in falsehood and deny the favor of Allah?" (أَ فَبِالْباطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَ بِنِعْمَةِ اللَّهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ).

What kind of judgment is this? And what an incorrect program is this, that a human forgets the source of blessings and goes after a being that is the source of not the slightest effect and is the clear manifestation of "falsehood" in all dimensions?

Key Points

1- The Causes and Sources of Provision

Undoubtedly—as we have said—humans differ from one another in terms of talents and God-given gifts, but nevertheless, what constitutes the main foundation of success

is the effort, endeavor, and striving of the human being. Those who are more hardworking are more successful, and those who are lazier and less striving are more deprived.

For this reason, the Quran considers the human's benefit to be proportionate to his striving and explicitly says: "And that there is not for man except that for which he strives" (An-Najm: 39).

In the meantime, applying the principles of piety, integrity, trustworthiness, and divine systems and laws, and implementing the principles of justice, has an extraordinarily deep effect, as the Quran says: "And if the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (Al-A'raf: 96).

And It also says: "And whoever fears Allah—He will make for him a way out and will provide for him from where he does not expect" (Surah At-Talaq, verses 2 and 3).

And for this same reason, It mentions the role of charity (infaq) in the expansion of provision and says: "If you loan Allah a goodly loan, He will multiply it for you" (At-Taghabun: 17).

Perhaps it does not need reminding that in social life, the loss of an individual or a group results in harm to the entire society, and for this reason, maintaining individuals and helping them has a return for the whole society (aside from the spiritual and human aspects).

In short, if the system governing the economy of a society is a system of piety, integrity, purity, cooperation, and charity, undoubtedly such a society will become powerful and proud. But if, on the contrary, it is a system of exploitation, fraud, plunder, aggression, and forgetting

others, such a society will also be economically backward, and the thread of their material life will also disintegrate.

Therefore, in Islamic narrations, extraordinary importance has been given to the issue of effort and striving coupled with piety for earning a living, to the extent that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Do not be lazy in seeking your livelihoods, for our forefathers used to run for it and seek it." [^1] And we also read from the same great Imam: "The one who toils for his family is like the mujahid in the way of Allah." [^2] It has even been commanded that Muslims should leave their homes as early as possible in the morning and go in pursuit of striving for life [^3], and among those whose prayers are never answered are those who have healthy bodies but sit in a corner of the house and only pray for the expansion of provision.

Here the question arises: then why in many verses and narrations is provision introduced as being in the hand of Allah and striving for it is disparaged?! In response to this question, two points must be noted:

1- Careful study of Islamic sources shows that verses or narrations which seem contradictory at a superficial and initial glance—whether in this discussion or others—

[^1]: Wasa'il, vol. 12, p. 48. [^2]: Wasa'il, vol. 12, p. 43. [^3]: Wasa'il, vol. 12, p. 50.

each looks at one of the dimensions of an issue that possesses various dimensions, and neglect of these dimensions has caused the illusion of contradiction.

Where people pursue the world and the glitz of the material realm with the most intense greed and voracity, and stop at no crime to attain it, emphatic and successive instructions arrive to draw their attention to the instability of the world and the worthlessness of wealth and status.

And where a group, under the pretext of asceticism (zuhd), gives up work, effort, and striving, the importance of work and effort is pointed out to them. In truth, the program of true leaders must be such that they prevent extremes in a fitting manner.

The verses and narrations that emphasize that provision is in the hand of Allah and that He has determined the share and portion of everyone are, in fact, intended to end greed, voracity, world-worship, and unconditional, limitless activity for earning more income; they are not intended to extinguish the warm and hot flames of the vigor of work and effort for an honorable, independent, and self-sufficient life.

With attention to this reality, the interpretation of narrations that say: "There are many provisions that even if you do not go after them, they will come after you," becomes clear.

2- From the perspective of the monotheistic worldview, everything ends with Allah, and a true monotheistic believer does not consider anything to be from himself; rather, every bounty that reaches him, he sees its source as being from Allah and says: "In Your hand is [all] good. Indeed, You are over all things competent" (Al-Imran: 26).

In this way, he must in every case be mindful of the truth that even the products of his striving, effort, thought, and creativity—if he looks correctly—are from

Allah's side, and if His gaze of grace were withdrawn for a moment, all of these would be extinguished.

When he mounts a riding beast, he says: "Exalted is He who has subjected this to us." When he attains a blessing, he chants the song of monotheism and says: "Whatever blessing we have is from You, O Allah." [^1] And even when he takes an important reformative step, like the prophets, in the path of saving humanity, he says: "And my success is not but through Allah. Upon Him I have relied, and to Him I return" (Hud: 88).

But what is certain is that alongside all these discussions, the basis and foundation of earning provision is correct, positive, and constructive effort and activity, far from any extreme. As for the provisions that reach a person without effort, they are secondary, not fundamental. Perhaps for this reason, Ali عليه السلام in his short sayings first mentions the provisions that a person goes after, then those that come after the person:

"O son of Adam! Provision is of two kinds: a provision that you seek, and a provision that seeks you." [^2]

2- Consolation and Equality with Others

In the above verses, there is a reference to the narrow-mindedness and stinginess of many humans who are not willing to grant from the greater bounties they possess to those under their hand

[^1]: This sentence is mentioned in the supplications following the Afternoon (Asr) prayer in books of supplication. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 379.

(except those who have been nurtured in the school of training of the prophets and divine leaders).

Under these same verses, narrations emphasize and recommend the issue of equality (musawat) and consolation (muwasat). Among them: in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim under the above verses, we read: "It is not permissible for a man to reserve for himself any food to the exclusion of his family." [^1] And it is also narrated from Abu Dharr that he said: I heard the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saying:

"They are only your brothers, so clothe them from what you wear, and feed them from what you eat." After this recommendation of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Abu Dharr would act such that the cloak and clothing of those under his hand had not the slightest difference from his own! [^2]

From the above narrations and also from the verse itself which says: "so they are equal therein," it is understood that Islam recommends that all Muslims, as an Islamic ethical program, observe equality as much as possible with all members of their family and those who are under their hand, and that they should not claim any superiority or privilege for themselves in the family environment or before their subordinates.

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 68.

Ayahs

وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ رِزْقًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٧٣)
They worship besides Allah what has no power to provide them with anything from the heavens and the earth, nor are they capable [of anything]. (73)
فَلَا تَضْرِبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٧٤)
So do not draw comparisons for Allah: indeed Allah knows and you do not know. (74)

Commentary

Do Not Ascribe Similitudes to Allah

Following the previous discussions on Monotheism (Tawhid), the verses under discussion address the issue of polytheism (Shirk). With a tone full of reproach, blame, and rebuke, it says: "And they worship besides Allah that which possesses no provision for them from the heavens and the earth, and has no role in this regard" (وَ يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ رِزْقاً مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ شَيْئاً).

Not only do they possess nothing in this regard, but "they are not capable of creating, producing, or accessing them either" (وَ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ‌).

This is an allusion to the fact that the polytheists pursued the worship of idols under the assumption that they were influential in their destiny, profit, and loss. However, we know that one of the most important issues in human life is his sustenance and provision, whether it be the provisions that descend from the heavens (such as the life-giving drops of rain, the vitalizing rays of the sun, and the like) or those that grow from the earth and are extracted from it. None of these are within the control of the idols; they are worthless beings that possess no will of their own, and it is only superstitions and ignorance-filled prejudices that have assigned them a role.

In reality, the phrase "they are not capable" (لا يستطيعون) serves as a reason for the phrase "they do not possess" (لا يملكون); meaning they do not possess any of the provisions because they lack the slightest power to create or preserve them.

The next verse, as a conclusion, says: "Now that this is the case, do not strike similitudes for Allah" (فَلا تَضْرِبُوا لِلَّهِ الْأَمْثالَ‌). "For indeed, Allah knows and you do not know" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Some commentators have stated that the phrase "do not strike similitudes for Allah" refers to the logic held by the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance (and similar logic is seen among some polytheists of our own time). They would say: "If we turn to idols, it is because we are not worthy of worshipping Allah; we must go to the idols because they are those brought near to His threshold! Allah is like a great king whose ministers and elites approach him, but the masses of people who have no access to him must go to his associates, elites, and those close to him."

This kind of ugly and erroneous logic, which is sometimes manifested in the form of a deviant parable, is one of the most dangerous types of logic.

In response to them, the Quran says: "Do not strike parables for Allah," meaning parables that are consistent with limited thoughts and "contingent" beings full of deficiencies.

If you were to pay attention to the existential encompassment of Allah over all beings, and His infinite grace and mercy, and His closeness to yourselves—to the extent that He is closer to you than you are to yourselves—you would never turn to intermediaries to turn toward Allah.

A God who has invited you to supplication and to speak directly with Him, and has kept the doors of His house open to you night and day, must not be likened to a tyrannical, arrogant king who has retreated inside his palace and whom no one can reach except a limited few. "Therefore, do not strike similitudes for Allah."

In discussions regarding the Attributes of Allah, we have specifically noted that one of the most dangerous precipices on the path to knowing His Attributes is the precipice of "anthropomorphism" (Tashbih)—that is, comparing His Attributes to those of His servants and considering them similar. This is because Allah is an Infinite Being in every respect, while others are finite beings in every respect, and any kind of similitude or parable here becomes a cause for distance and alienation from knowing Him.

Even in instances where we are compelled to liken His Holy Essence to "Light" and the like, we must be mindful that such similitudes are, in any case, deficient and inadequate, and are acceptable only from one aspect, not from all aspects! (Reflect carefully).

While many people do not pay attention to this reality, they often fall into the valley of similitude and analogy, and drift away from the truth of Monotheism. Therefore, the Quran repeatedly warns: sometimes with the phrase "And there is none co-equal unto Him" (وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ) (Surah al-Tawhid, 4); sometimes with the phrase "There is nothing like unto Him" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْ‌ءٌ) (Surah ash-Shura, 11); and sometimes with the phrase "Do not strike similitudes for Allah" as mentioned in the verses above, drawing people's attention to this reality.

And perhaps the phrase "Indeed, Allah knows and you do not know" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌) at the end of the above verses also expresses this same subject: that people are often unaware of the secrets of Allah's Attributes.

Ayahs

۞ ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا عَبْدًا مَّمْلُوكًا لَّا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَمَن رَّزَقْنَـٰهُ مِنَّا رِزْقًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ يُنفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَجَهْرًا ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوُۥنَ ۚ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
Allah draws a parable: a slave owned [by his master] who has no power over anything, and another one [a free man] whom We have provided a goodly provision and who spends out of it secretly and openly. Are they equal? All praise belongs to Allah. But most of them do not know. (75)
وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَبْكَمُ لَا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ كَلٌّ عَلَىٰ مَوْلَىٰهُ أَيْنَمَا يُوَجِّههُّ لَا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى هُوَ وَمَن يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۙ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٧٦)
Allah draws [another] parable: Two men, one of whom is dumb, having no power over anything and who is a liability to his master: wherever he directs him he does not bring any good. Is he equal to someone who enjoins justice and is [steady] on a straight path? (76)
وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَآ أَمْرُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ ٱلْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٧٧)
To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. The matter of the Hour is just like the twinkling of an eye, or [even] swifter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (77)

Commentary

Two Living Examples for the Believer and the Disbeliever!

Following the previous verses which spoke of faith and disbelief, and of believers and polytheists, the verses under discussion clarify the state of these two groups by mentioning two living and clear examples:

In the first example, He likens the "polytheists" to a possessed slave who has no power over anything, and the "believers" to a wealthy and self-sufficient person who provides for everyone from his resources. He says: "Allah presents an example: a slave owned [by another] who has no power over anything" (ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا عَبْداً مَمْلُوكاً لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

He possesses neither much creative power—as he is always a captive in the clutches of his master and limited in every respect—nor legislative power, for he has no right to dispose of his property (if he has any) or enter into other contracts concerning himself.

Indeed, being a servant to the servants of Allah results in nothing but captivity and limitation in every sense.

"And in contrast, He presents the example of a person of faith to whom We have granted a good provision and various types of sustenance and pure blessings" (وَ مَنْ رَزَقْناهُ مِنَّا رِزْقاً حَسَناً).

This free human being, possessing abundant means, "spends from what he possesses, secretly and openly" (فَهُوَ يُنْفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَ جَهْراً).

"Are these two equal?!" (هَلْ يَسْتَوُونَ‌).

Certainly not. Therefore, "All praise and thanks belong to Allah" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ‌).

The God whose servant is free, powerful, and generous—not like those idols whose servants are so weak, limited, powerless, and captive! "But most of them (the polytheists) do not know" (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌) [1].

Once again, He sets another eloquent example for the worshippers of idols and the true believers, likening them respectively to a person born mute who is also a slave and powerless, and a free, articulate person who constantly invites to justice and is on a straight path. He says: "And Allah presents an example of two men, one of whom is mute from birth and has no power over anything" (وَ ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَجُلَيْنِ أَحَدُهُما أَبْكَمُ لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ) [2]. "While being a slave, he is a burden upon his master and guardian"

[^1]: According to the commentary mentioned above, the example is a comparison between the believer and the disbeliever. However, a group of commentators have suggested another possibility: that the "possessed slave" refers to "idols" and the "free believer" who bestows all kinds of charity and blessings refers to "Allah" (but this view seems unlikely).

[^2]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: Abkam refers to one born mute (whereas Akhras refers to any kind of mute person). Therefore, "Every Abkam is Akhras, but not every Akhras is Abkam." He then adds that sometimes this term is applied to one who is unable to speak due to weakness of intellect.

(وَ هُوَ كَلٌّ عَلى‌ مَوْلاهُ‌).

For this reason, "wherever he directs him, he brings no good" (أَيْنَما يُوَجِّهْهُ لا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ).

Thus, he possesses four completely negative traits: being born mute, absolute helplessness, being a burden on his master, and when sent for a task, taking no positive step.

These four traits, which are simultaneously causes and effects of one another, depict a person who is one hundred percent negative, whose existence is the source of no good or blessing, and who is a burden on society or his family.

"Is such a person equal to one who has an articulate and eloquent tongue, and constantly invites to justice, and is on a smooth road and a straight path?!" (هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَ مَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

Although only two traits are mentioned here—constant invitation to justice and having a straight method and correct program free from any deviation—these two traits themselves represent other qualities. Is it possible for someone who constantly invites to justice to be mute, cowardly, or lacking personality? Never; such a person has an articulate tongue, firm logic, a powerful will, and sufficient courage and bravery.

Is someone who always walks on the straight path a clumsy, helpless, unconscious, or unintelligent person? Never; he is certainly an inquisitive, intelligent, resourceful, and steadfast individual.

The comparison of these two individuals illustrates the vast distance between the mindset of idolatry and the worship of God, and clarifies the difference between these two programs and those nurtured by these two schools of thought.

Since we have often seen that the Quran blends discussions related to Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism with matters concerning the Hereafter and the great court of Resurrection, here too, after the discourse in the previous verses regarding polytheism and Monotheism, it turns to the Resurrection and addresses some of the objections of the polytheists. First, it says: "To Allah belongs the unseen of the heavens and the earth" (وَ لِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

This seems to be a response to an objection raised by deniers of the physical Resurrection, who said: "When we have died and the particles of our dust are scattered in every corner, who has knowledge of them to gather them?"

Furthermore, assuming their particles are gathered and they are returned to life, who is it that knows their deeds—which have been forgotten—and can examine their records?! The above verse answers this question in all its dimensions in a single sentence: that Allah knows the unseen of all the heavens and the earth. He is present everywhere and always; therefore, fundamentally, "unseen" and "hidden" have no meaning for Him. Everything is witnessed by Him, and these different expressions are in comparison to our existence and in harmony with our logic.

Then He adds: "The matter of the Hour is as easy as the twinkling of an eye, or even closer!" (وَ ما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ‌) [1]. This is actually an allusion to another objection from the deniers of the Resurrection who said:

"Who has the power for such a task? This is an extraordinarily difficult matter." The Quran

[^1]: Lamh (rhyming with Mas-h) originally means the flash of lightning, then it came to mean a quick glance. Additionally, it should be noted that "or" (aw) here means "rather" (bal).

responds to them: "For you, who have negligible power, this program seems difficult, but for Allah, whose power is infinite, it is as simple as the twinkling of your eye, which is both fast and very easy!" Interestingly, after describing the occurrence of the Resurrection as a quick glance or the twinkling of an eye, He states the phrase "أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ‌" (or even closer). That is, even the comparison to a quick glance is due to the limitations of language; it is so rapid that time does not even factor into it. If the expression "كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ" was used, it is because this is the shortest duration in your logic.

In any case, these two phrases are a living and eloquent indication of Allah's infinite power, especially regarding the issue of the Resurrection and the rising of humanity. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "For Allah is powerful over all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Key Points

1- Free Humans and Captives!

Contrary to what some imagine, the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk) is not merely a matter of belief and intellect; rather, it affects the entirety of human life and colors everything. When Monotheism sprouts in any heart, it brings life to that heart and is the factor for its growth and flourishing, because Monotheism expands a person's horizon so much that it links them to the Infinite.

Conversely, polytheism plunges a person into an extremely limited world—the world of stone and wooden idols or weak, idol-like humans—and shapes a person's vision, understanding, ambition, ability, and effort in that same color and dimension.

In the above verses, this reality is expressed so beautifully in the form of an example that nothing could be more eloquent.

The polytheist is "Abkam"—mute, born mute—whose actions reflect weakness of thought and a lack of rational logic. Due to being captive in the clutches of polytheism, he has no power over any positive action (لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

He is not a free human being but a captive in the clutches of superstitions and delusions.

Because of these traits, he is considered a burden on society, for he leaves his destiny in the hands of idols or exploitative humans.

He is always dependent, and until he tastes the flavor of Monotheism—which is the creed of freedom and independence—he will not emerge from this dependency: "وَ هُوَ كَلٌّ عَلى‌ مَوْلاهُ‌".

With this mindset, in whatever path he treads, he will be unsuccessful, and whichever way he turns, no good will come to him (أَيْنَما يُوَجِّهْهُ لا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ).

How great is the distance between such a narrow-minded, superstitious, captive, helpless, and planless person and the free, brave human who not only practices the principles of justice himself but is also a constant herald of justice in his society?! Furthermore, because of his logical thinking and harmony with the monotheistic system of creation, he always walks on the straight path—which is the shortest of paths—reaches his goal quickly, and does not waste his existential capital on crooked and deviant ways.

In short, Monotheism and polytheism are not just a belief; they are a complete model for all of life, a vast and extensive program that encompasses thought, ethics, emotions, and individual, social, political, economic, and cultural life. A comparison between the polytheistic Arabs of the Age of Ignorance and the monotheistic Muslims of early Islam can provide a clear depiction of these two programs and paths.

Those who until yesterday were so entangled in ignorance, division, decadence, and misery that they knew nothing beyond their limited environment filled with poverty and corruption, once they stepped into the valley of Monotheism, they enjoyed such unity, awareness, and power that they took the entire civilized world of that day under their wings.

2- The Role of "Justice" and "Uprightness" in Human Life

It is interesting that in the above verses, from among all the programs of the monotheists, the finger is pointed at the invitation to justice and walking on the straight path. This shows that the primary root of the happiness of an individual or a human society lies in these two: having a correct program that is neither Eastern nor Western, neither deviating to the left nor to the right, and then inviting to the implementation of the principles of justice—not as a temporary program, but as the phrase ya'muru bil-'adl suggests (considering that the present tense verb implies continuity), as a continuous and perpetual program.

3- [The meaning of "The one who commands justice" refers to the Imams]

3- In a narration transmitted through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read in the interpretation of the above verses:

"The one who commands justice is the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams (صلوات اللَّه عليهم)."

Some commentators have interpreted the phrase "مَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ‌" as referring to Hamza, Uthman ibn Maz'un, or Ammar, and the "أَبْكَمُ" (mute) as referring to Ubayy ibn Khalaf, Abu Jahl, and their like.

It is clear that all of these are by way of mentioning clear and important examples and are by no means a reason for exclusivity. These interpretations also clarify that the above verses are intended to present a comparison for the polytheists and the believers, not for the idols and Allah.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 70.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُم مِّنۢ بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۙ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٨)
Allah has brought you forth from the bellies of your mothers while you did not know anything. He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts so that you may give thanks. (78)
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍ فِى جَوِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٧٩)
Have they not regarded the birds disposed in the air of the sky: no one sustains them except Allah. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (79)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّن جُلُودِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بُيُوتًا تَسْتَخِفُّونَهَا يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ وَيَوْمَ إِقَامَتِكُمْ ۙ وَمِنْ أَصْوَافِهَا وَأَوْبَارِهَا وَأَشْعَارِهَآ أَثَـٰثًا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٨٠)
It is Allah who has made your homes as a place of rest for you and He made for you homes out of the skins of the cattle which you find portable on the day of your shifting and on the day of your halt, and out of their wool, fur and hair [He has appointed] furniture and wares [enduring] for a while. (80)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلَـٰلًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ أَكْنَـٰنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ ٱلْحَرَّ وَسَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُم بَأْسَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ (٨١)
It is Allah who made for you the shade from what He has created, and made for you retreats in the mountains, and made for you garments that protect you from heat, and garments that protect you from your [mutual] violence. That is how He completes His blessing upon you so that you may submit [to Him]. (81)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٨٢)
But if they turn their backs [on you], your duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (82)
يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنكِرُونَهَا وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٣)
They recognize the blessing of Allah and then deny it, and most of them are faithless. (83)

Commentary

Types of Material and Spiritual Blessings

Once again, the Quran returns to the subject of the various blessings of the Lord as a lesson in Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God. In this section of blessings, it first points to the blessing of science, knowledge, and the tools of cognition, saying: "And Allah brought you forth from the wombs of your mothers while you knew nothing" (وَ اللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُمْ مِنْ بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً).

Certainly, in that limited, dark, and dependent environment, this ignorance and lack of awareness was bearable. However, when you stepped into the vast expanse of this wide world, the continuation of that ignorance was no longer possible. Therefore, He placed the tools for perceiving realities and recognizing existents—namely, "hearing, sight, and intellect—at your disposal" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَ الْأَبْصارَ وَ الْأَفْئِدَةَ).

This was so that you might perceive these great blessings, and your sense of gratitude toward the Giver of all these bounties might be stimulated, "that perhaps you may give thanks" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

1- Man possessed no knowledge at the beginning

This verse clearly states that at the moment of birth, man possessed absolutely no knowledge; therefore, whatever he has acquired was after birth and through the tools that Allah placed at his disposal.

Here, a question arises: How does the Quran say that at the time of exiting the environment of the fetus you knew nothing, while we possess a series of innate (fitri) sciences? Examples include the awareness of Monotheism and the knowledge of God, as well as self-evident matters (such as the impossibility of the conjunction of opposites, or that the whole is greater than the part, or in social and ethical matters like the beauty of justice and the ugliness of oppression, and the like). All of these are sciences that have been placed in our nature from the beginning and were born with us from the mother. So how can the Quran say that at the beginning of birth you knew nothing?

Did even our knowledge of our own existence, which is considered "knowledge by presence" (al-ilm al-huduri), not exist and was it acquired through "hearing, sight, and intellect" (as-sam‘ wa al-absar wa al-af’idah)?

In response to this question, we say: Undoubtedly, even these self-evident, necessary, and innate sciences were not present in man in a state of "actuality" (fi‘liyyah) at that moment; only the talent and potential (quwwah) for them existed in the human essence. In other words, at the moment of birth, we were heedless of everything, even of our own selves, but the perception of many realities lay hidden within us potentially. Gradually, our eyes gained the power of vision, our ears the power of hearing, and the intellect the power of perception and analysis, and we benefited from these three Divine bounties. First, we perceived the conceptions of many objects through the senses and entrusted them to the intellect; then, from them, we constructed general and even more universal concepts, and through "generalization" and "abstraction," we attained intellectual realities.

Our power of thought reaches a point where we become aware of ourselves (as a knowledge by presence); then, the sciences and knowledge that were potentially in our nature take life and become actualized. We then use these self-evident and necessary sciences as a foundation for recognizing theoretical and non-evident sciences.

Therefore, the generality and universality of the verse which says: "at the beginning of birth you knew nothing" has no specification or exception.

2- The blessing of the tools of cognition

Undoubtedly, the external world never finds its way into our inner being; rather, an image, a depiction, and forms of it are imprinted upon our soul through certain means. In this way, our recognition of the external world is always carried out through tools, the most important of which are hearing and sight (as-sam‘ wa al-basar).

These tools transfer what they have perceived from the outside to our mind and thought, and we perceive it through the power of intellect and reflection and engage in its analysis:

For this reason, in the aforementioned verse, after stating the absolute lack of awareness of man when stepping into this world, it says: "Allah placed hearing, sight, and hearts at your disposal" (so that you may perceive the realities of existence).

And if we see that it mentions "hearing" first and then "sight," even though sight apparently has a wider circle of activity, it is perhaps because the activity of hearing begins first in the newborn, and after a while, the eyes gain the power of vision. This is because the eye, in the world of the mother's womb where absolute darkness prevails, is not ready to receive light rays at the beginning of birth. For this reason, it is often closed after birth, gradually becoming accustomed to light, and the power of vision is awakened in it. Meanwhile, the human ear, according to some, has more or less the power of hearing even in the fetal world and hears the rhythm of the mother's heart and becomes accustomed to it! Furthermore, with the eye, man only sees sensory matters, whereas the ear is a means for education and training in all fields; for through hearing words, one becomes acquainted with all realities, whether they are within the circle of the senses or outside of it, while the eye does not have this breadth of action. It is true that man can become acquainted with these issues by reading words through the eye, but we know that reading and literacy are not universal for all humans, whereas hearing words is universal.

As for why "hearing" (sam‘) is mentioned in the singular form and "sights" (absar) in the plural? We have explained the reason for this in the first volume under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

It is also noteworthy that although "fu’ad" has been translated as heart (intellect), it differs from "qalb" in that the meaning of fu’ad involves a sense of boiling, burning, or in other words, analysis and initiative.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "The fu’ad is like the qalb (heart), but it is called fu’ad when the meaning of tafa’ud—that is, burning or intensity—is considered within it." (And certainly, this matter comes to man after sufficient experience; in any case, although the tools of cognition are not limited to these two or three, these are certainly the most important tools).

This is because human knowledge is obtained either through "experience" or "intellectual reasoning." Experience is impossible without the use of the eye and ear, and intellectual reasoning is carried out through the fu’ad, meaning the intellect.

3- That you may give thanks (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌)

Since the blessing of the tools of cognition is the highest blessing given to man—for not only does he observe the signs of God in the expanse of existence with his eyes and ears, hear the words of Divine leaders, and perceive and analyze with his heart, but also in his material life, every kind of progress, advancement, and perfection is indebted to these three means—therefore, immediately after that, with the phrase "perhaps you may give thanks," He reminds us of the importance of these three subjects. That is, He gave you these means so that you might become knowledgeable and aware, and then give thanks for all this awareness, science, and knowledge, which is your greatest distinction from animals. Undoubtedly, no human is capable of fulfilling the gratitude for these three great blessings except by offering an apology for his shortcomings at the threshold of Allah!

In the next verse, He continues the secrets of Allah's greatness in the expanse of existence and says: "Have they not looked at the birds held poised in the midst of the sky?" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّراتٍ فِي جَوِّ السَّماءِ). "Jaww" linguistically means "air" (as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat) or that part of the air which is far from the earth (as stated in the commentaries of Majma‘ al-Bayan, Al-Mizan, and Al-Alusi).

And since the nature of objects is to be attracted toward the earth, He has described the movement of birds above the earth with the term musakhkharat (subjected/poised), meaning that Allah has placed a power in their wings and feathers and a property in the air that allows them to fly in the air contrary to the law of gravity.

Then He adds: "None holds them up except Allah" (ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا اللَّهُ‌).

It is true that the natural property of the wings and the muscles created therein, the specific shape of the birds, and the characteristics present in the air have joined hands to allow the birds to fly, but who created this shape and these properties?

And who established this precise and calculated system? Was it blind and deaf nature? Or One who is aware of all the physical properties of objects and whose infinite knowledge encompasses all of this?

And if we see these matters attributed to Allah, it is because their source is from Him, and this expression—attributing the properties of causes and means to Allah—is frequent in the Quran.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed in that are signs (of Allah's greatness and power) for a people who believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

That is, they look at these matters with an inquisitive and truth-seeking eye and engage in their analysis, and in the light of that, their faith becomes stronger and more firm.

Key Points

1- The secrets of the flight of birds in the expanse of the sky

It is easy to understand why many of the wonders of the universe do not appear great in our eyes: because we have always seen them and have become accustomed to them. This habit and familiarity have, in truth, become a veil draped over these amazing scenes. For this reason, if we can empty our minds of what we are accustomed to, we will observe many wonders around us.

The issue of the flight of birds is of this kind: a heavy body moving easily contrary to the law of gravity, rising so quickly that it disappears from sight in a few moments—this is not a simple matter.

If we reflect on the structure of birds from every aspect, we will see that their entire being is coordinated for flight: their spindle-shaped structure which reduces air resistance on their bodies to a minimum; their light and hollow feathers, plus their broad chests which allow them to ride the airwaves; the specific structure of the wings which creates the "lift force" in them [^1].

[^1]: "Lift force" is a term in modern physics used regarding airplanes. In short, if an object has two different surfaces (like an airplane wing where the bottom surface is flat and the top surface has a curve and protrusion) and moves horizontally, a specific force is created in it that lifts it to the upper surface. This force is because the air pressure on the bottom surface is greater than on the top surface, as the bottom surface is shorter and the top surface is longer. This is an important issue upon which the movement of airplanes is based, and if we look closely at the wings of birds, this subject can be clearly seen in them (observe carefully). In principle, it must be said that the structure of the airplane in various aspects is an imitation of the structure of birds!

And the specific structure of the tails which helps them in maneuvering and moving quickly to the left, right, up, and down (like the tail of an airplane), and the coordination of the power of sight and other senses that assist them in this program—all are matters that provide the possibility of rapid flight for birds.

Beyond all this, the development of the fetus separately from the mother (through egg-laying) causes the weight of the pregnancy period, which would certainly be an obstacle to flight, to be removed from them, along with many other matters, each of which is precisely an effective physical factor for flight.

This collection shows that extraordinary knowledge and power have been employed in its creation, and as the Quran says: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe."

The wonders of the world of birds are more than can be contained in one or several books. We currently know many birds as migratory birds that travel to different parts of the world to continue their lives, sometimes even traversing the very long distance between the North and South Poles. In this long journey, they use mysterious guidance tools, and in the light of those, they can find their way through mountains, plains, valleys, and seas, even on cloudy days and occasionally dark nights when no human is capable of finding his way. Sometimes they are asleep in the sky while flying! Sometimes they continue flying for weeks without a moment's stop, day and night, without needing food! For before starting the movement, by an inner inspiration, they eat excessively, and this food is stored as fats around their bodies. There are also other secrets regarding birds in building nests, training offspring, fighting enemies, finding necessary food, cooperating with one another, and even cooperation and coexistence with species other than their own, each of which has a long story.

Yes, as we read in the aforementioned verse: "Each of these is a sign of the greatness of the Lord and His infinite knowledge and power."

2- The connection between the verses

Undoubtedly, there is a connection between the above verse, which speaks of the flight of birds, and the verses before and after it, as all of them speak of Allah's blessings in the world of creation and the dimensions of His power and greatness. However, it is also not far-fetched that the mention of the flight of birds following the mention of the tools of cognition is a subtle allusion to comparing the flight of these birds in the sensible world with the flight of thoughts in the non-sensible world, each of which flies in its own specific atmosphere with the tools they possess.

Ali عليه السلام says in the Sermon of Shiqshiqiyyah: "The flood of science and knowledge flows down from me, and far-reaching thoughts cannot reach my peak!" And in his short sayings, in describing the virtue of Malik al-Ashtar, that devoted officer, we read: "No mount can climb the mountain of his being, and no bird of thought can reach his height." [^1]

As we said at the beginning of this Surah, one of the names of this Surah is the "Surah of Blessings" because various material and spiritual blessings of the Lord, numbering over fifty, are mentioned in this Surah, both as a proof for recognizing His Pure Essence and as a motivation for gratitude for His blessings.

In the third verse under discussion, the matter continues, saying: "And Allah has made for you from your houses a place of rest" (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَناً).

And truly, the blessing of housing is among the most important blessings; until it exists, the rest will not be pleasant.

The word "buyut" is the plural of "bayt", meaning room or house, and the root "baytutah" from which this word is derived originally means staying overnight. Since man uses his room and house mostly for rest at night, the word "bayt" has been applied to it.

It is also necessary to note that the Quran does not say: "He made your house your place of rest," but rather, by mentioning the word "min" (from) which is for partitivity, it says: "He made a part of your house and your rooms a place of rest." This is a very precise expression because a complete house is one that has various facilities, from the living area to the place for the mount, storage for necessary supplies, and other needs.

Following the mention of fixed houses, it speaks of mobile houses and says: "And He made for you houses from the skins of cattle" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ جُلُودِ الْأَنْعامِ بُيُوتاً) [^1].

[^1]: Although in our age and time, tents made of skin are few, the above verse shows that in that age and time, leather tents were considered among the best tents, which the Quran pointed out first. Perhaps this is because, against the scorching winds of the Hijaz deserts, these types of tents were the most secure.

"Houses that are very light and low in weight, which you find light (to carry) on the day of your travel and the day of your encampment" (تَسْتَخِفُّونَها يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ وَ يَوْمَ إِقامَتِكُمْ‌).

"Furthermore, from their wool, fur, and hair, He provided for you furnishings and goods and various means of life for a time" (وَ مِنْ أَصْوافِها وَ أَوْبارِها وَ أَشْعارِها أَثاثاً وَ مَتاعاً إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌).

We know that the hair that grows on the bodies of cattle is sometimes quite coarse, like goat hair, which the Arabs call "sha‘r" (plural ash‘ar); sometimes it is a bit softer, which we call wool and the Arabs call "suf" (plural aswaf); and sometimes it is even softer than that, which we call "fur" or "down," and the Arabs call it "wabar" (plural awbar). It is obvious that this difference in the structure of the hair causes each to be used for a specific purpose: one for making carpets, another for clothing, another for tents, and the like.

Regarding what is meant by "athath" (furnishings) and "mata‘" (goods) in this verse, commentators have offered several possibilities:

On the whole, "athath" means household items and is originally derived from the root "athth" meaning abundance and entanglement; since household items are usually many, they are called athath.

"Mata‘" refers to any kind of thing from which man derives enjoyment and benefit. (Therefore, both expressions show one subject from two different angles).

Given what has been said, mentioning these two expressions one after the other may be an indication that you can provide many items for your home from the wool, fur, and hair of cattle and benefit from them.

Others, such as Fakhr al-Razi, have suggested the possibility that athath refers to coverings and clothing, and mata‘ refers to carpets, but they have mentioned no evidence to prove this.

Alusi in Ruh al-Ma‘ani also narrates the possibility that athath refers to household items and mata‘ to items used as trade goods.

However, what we said first seems closer to the truth.

For the phrase "until a time" (إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌), several interpretations have also been mentioned, but apparently, it means that you benefit from these means in this world and until the end of life, an allusion to the fact that the life of this world and its means and tools are not eternal, and whatever they may be, they are limited.

Shadows, Shelters, and Coverings

Then it moves to another of the Divine blessings and says: "And Allah has made for you, from what He has created, shadows" (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلالًا).

"And He made for you, from the mountains, shelters" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْجِبالِ أَكْناناً).

"Aknan" is the plural of "kinn", meaning a means of covering, protection, and preservation. For this reason, hiding places, caves, and shelters that exist in mountains are called aknan.

Here we clearly see that shadows—whether the shadows of trees and other objects or the shadows of caves and mountain shelters—are described as a significant Divine blessing. And this is indeed the truth, for just as man needs the radiance of light in his life, he often needs shadow; for if light shone uniformly, life would be impossible. We know that the greatest shadow for us inhabitants of the earth is the shadow of the earth itself, which is called night and covers half of the earth's surface. The role of this great shadow in the lives of humans is hidden from no one, as is the role of smaller and smaller shadows during the day in various regions.

The mention of the blessing of "shadows" and "mountain shelters" after the mention of the blessing of "houses" and "tents" in the previous verse is as if to indicate that humans are not outside of three groups: a group living in cities and settlements who benefit from houses; another group who are traveling and have tents with them and use tents; but Allah has not left the third group—travelers who do not even have tents with them—deprived, and has prepared shelters for them along the way.

The importance of caves and mountain shelters may never be clear to comfortable city dwellers, but desert-dwellers, defenseless travelers, shepherds, and in short, all those who are deprived of the blessing of fixed and mobile houses and are caught in a hot summer sun or the sting of winter cold, know to what extent the existence of a mountain shelter is of vital importance and sometimes saves many humans and animals from certain death, especially since these types of shelters are usually warm in winter and cool in summer.

Then, following these natural and artificial canopies, it goes to the coverings of the human body and says: "And He made for you garments that protect you from the heat" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ).

"And also specific, highly resistant garments that protect you in your battle" (وَ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمْ بَأْسَكُمْ‌).

"Sarabīl" is the plural of "sirbāl", which according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat means a shirt, of whatever material it may be. Other commentators have also confirmed this meaning, though some have considered it to mean any kind of clothing and covering, but the first meaning is the well-known one.

Of course, the benefit of covering and clothing is not only to protect man from heat and cold; rather, it is both a dignity for man and a defensive means against many dangers directed at his body, for if man were naked, perhaps a part of his body would be injured every day. However, in the above verse, the first property is emphasized due to its importance.

It is noteworthy that only "protection against heat" is mentioned in the verse. This expression is perhaps because the Arabs, in many cases, mention one of two opposites as an abbreviation, and the second becomes clear by the context of the first; or it is because in those regions where the Quran was revealed, protection against heat was of greater importance.

There is also the possibility that the dangers of heatstroke and sunburn are more immediate and dangerous; in other words, human endurance against heat and intense sunlight is less than his resistance against cold, because the internal heat of the human body can protect him to a great extent in the cold, but the body's defense against heat is less.

At the end of the verse, as a reminder and warning, it says: "Thus does He complete His blessing upon you that perhaps you may submit (to His command)" (كَذلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ‌).

It is natural that when man examines the various blessings that surround his existence, he involuntarily remembers the Creator of the blessing, and if there is the slightest sense of appreciation and gratitude within him, he awakens and goes toward the Giver of the blessing and engages in His recognition.

Although some commentators have limited the word "blessing" mentioned in the above verse to certain blessings like the blessing of creation and the perfection of intellect, or Monotheism, or the blessing of the existence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is clear that "blessing" in this verse has a broad meaning encompassing all these and others, and these limited interpretations are of the type of interpreting by a clear and obvious instance.

After mentioning these manifest and hidden blessings, He says: "But if they turn away and do not submit to the call of truth, do not be distressed, for your duty is only the clear delivery (of the message)" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ‌). No matter how reasoned, captivating, and attractive the logic and speech of the speaker may be, as long as there is no readiness in the listener, it will not be effective. In other words, the receptivity of the vessel is also a condition, and every word has its people. Therefore, if the stubborn blind-hearted do not submit to your call, it is nothing new; the important thing is that you do not fall short in the clear delivery and that you express your call openly for everyone.

This sentence is, in truth, for the encouragement of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the consolation of his heart.

To complete this discourse, He adds: "They recognize the blessing of Allah and are acquainted with its dimensions and have realized its depth, yet they still deny it" (يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنْكِرُونَها).

Therefore, the reason for their disbelief should not be sought in a lack of awareness, for they have become sufficiently aware; the factor of this disbelief must be found in their other ugly traits that have become a barrier to their faith, which are blind prejudice, stubbornness, enmity with the truth, prioritizing short-term material interests over everything, being polluted with various lusts, and finally, arrogance and self-superiority.

Perhaps for this reason, He adds at the end of the verse: "And most of them are disbelievers" (وَ أَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ‌).

The word "aktharuhum" (most of them) has attracted the attention of many commentators, and they have asked themselves why He uses the expression "most"?

Each has mentioned an interpretation for it, but what seems closest to the truth is what was pointed out above: that the majority of these disbelievers are stubborn, antagonistic, and prejudiced disbelievers, and those who are in error constitute a minority.

The application of "disbelief" (kufr) to this type of disbelief which stems from arrogance is also seen in other verses of the Quran; for example, regarding Satan we read: "He refused and was arrogant and was of the disbelievers" (أَبى‌ وَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَ كانَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ‌) (Baqarah-34).

The possibility has also been suggested that "most" refers to those for whom the proof has been completed, and in contrast to them was a minority for whom the proof had not yet been completed. This meaning can also be returned to the first meaning.

Key Points

1- What is meant by "the blessing of Allah" (نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ‌) in this verse?

In what is meant by "the blessing of Allah" in this verse, we encounter several interpretations in the words of the commentators, which are mostly of the type of interpreting by an instance, while the concept of "the blessing of Allah" is so broad and expansive that it includes all material and spiritual blessings, and even the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself.

In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we read that the meaning of the blessing of Allah is the blessing of the existence of the Imams and the infallible leaders.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "By Allah, we are the blessing of Allah with which He has blessed His servants, and through us, those who succeed have succeeded." [^1] It is clear that success and victory are not possible without using the leadership of the true leaders, and this is among the clearest of Divine blessings, mentioned here as a manifest instance.

2- In the struggle between the forces of Truth and Falsehood

Some commentators have turned their attention to why in the above verses, in the sentence "They recognize the blessing of Allah, then they deny it" (يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنْكِرُونَها), the word "thumma" (then), which is usually used for conjunction with a delay, is mentioned, showing that there was a gap between their awareness of the Divine blessings and then denying them. They have said that the goal of this expression is to state that it was deserving for them to sincerely confess upon recognizing God's blessing and come toward Him, but they pursued the path of denial; the Quran has considered their action far-fetched and expressed it with "thumma."

However, we suggest that "thumma" here may point to a subtler point: that when the call of truth with its logical principles shines within the human soul, it clashes with the factors of negation and falsehood that may exist therein. This storm and struggle last for a while, the duration of which is proportionate to the strength or weakness of the factors of negation. If the factors of negation and denial were stronger, after a while they would prevail, and thus the expression with "thumma" would be perfectly appropriate.

In the verses of Surah Al-Anbiya, while recounting the story of Ibrahim, we read that when he revealed his powerful logic to the idolaters after breaking the idols, they reflected within themselves for a few moments and blamed themselves, and awakening and inclination toward the truth were close to illuminating their entire being. However, the negative factors—namely prejudice, arrogance, and stubbornness—prevailed over the call of truth; these moments ended and they rose again in denial (here too, the word "thumma" is seen): "So they returned to themselves and said, 'Indeed, you are the wrongdoers.' Then they were turned on their heads [reverting to their old ways]: 'You already knew that these do not speak!'" (Al-Anbiya-64-65).

Incidentally, from this explanation, the harmony of what we said regarding the meaning of "disbelievers" here with "thumma" becomes clearer.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 72. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 443.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا ثُمَّ لَا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٨٤)
The day We shall raise a witness from every nation, the faithless will not be permitted [to speak], nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (84)
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٨٥)
And when the wrongdoers sight the punishment, it shall not be lightened for them, nor will they be granted any respite. (85)
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَهُمْ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُرَكَآؤُنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِكَ ۖ فَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٨٦)
When the polytheists sight their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort to them, ‘You are indeed liars!’ (86)
وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلسَّلَمَ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٨٧)
They will submit to Allah on that day, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (87)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ عَذَابًا فَوْقَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يُفْسِدُونَ (٨٨)
Those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah—We shall add punishment to their punishment because of the corruption they used to cause. (88)
وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ شَهِيدًا عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تِبْيَـٰنًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٨٩)
The day We raise in every nation a witness against them from among themselves, We shall bring you as a witness against these. We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things and as guidance, mercy and good news for the Muslims. (89)

Commentary

Where All Doors Are Closed to the Evildoers

Following the previous verses which described the improper reaction of the deniers of truth toward the various blessings of the Lord, these verses point to a portion of their painful punishments in the other world, so they may realize their grim fate and rise to reconsider before it is too late.

First, It says: "And [remind them of] the day when We shall raise up from every nation a witness against them" (وَ يَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً) [1]. Does there exist a need for a witness and another observer despite the infinite knowledge of Allah? This is a question that immediately comes to mind here, but the answer becomes clear by noting one point: these matters often have a psychological aspect. That is, the more observers and witnesses a person sees before him, the more he accounts for his own actions; at the very least, he becomes concerned about shame and disgrace among a greater number of individuals.

Then It adds that in that court, "the disbelievers will not be permitted [to speak]" (ثُمَّ لا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

Is it possible that Allah would not grant a criminal the permission to defend himself? Yes, for there, there is no need to speak with the tongue; the hands, feet, ears, eyes, skin, and even the earth upon which the human committed a sin or a good deed shall bear witness. Therefore, it does not reach the turn of the tongue. This is a reality that is also derived from other verses of the Quran (Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 65, and Surah Al-Mursalat, Verse 36).

Not only are they not permitted to speak, "nor will they be allowed to make amends [or seek favor]" (وَ لا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ) 2.

[1]: Yawm (Day) here is an adverbial noun of time and relates to an omitted verb, which is estimated as "And let them remember" or "And remember [O Prophet]."

2: Yusta‘tabun is from the root isti‘tab, originally derived from ‘atab, which means expressing harshness in speech toward another. Therefore, isti‘tab means that the guilty person seeks "reproach" from the right-holder—meaning he places himself before the other's rebukes so that the right-holder's anger subsides and he grants satisfaction. For this reason, some have taken isti‘tab to mean istirdha’ (seeking pleasure/satisfaction), while its literal meaning is not seeking satisfaction itself, but rather its necessary consequence.
