This is because that place is the realm of encountering the results and reflections of actions, not a place for performing actions or making amends and corrections—just like a fruit that is separated from the branch, whose period of growth and development has ended.

The next verse adds that when these habitual wrongdoers have passed the stage of accounting and stand before the Divine punishment, they sometimes request a reduction and sometimes a respite, but "when the wrongdoers see the punishment, it will not be lightened for them, nor will they be given respite" (وَ إِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الْعَذابَ فَلا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ).

An interesting point to note is that in these two verses, four stages of the criminals' states are mentioned, the likes of which we see with our own eyes in this world:

The first stage is that the criminal tries to exonerate himself through procrastination and excuses.

When he does not achieve this goal, in the second stage, he tries to win the other party's affection, accepting their rebukes and seeking their satisfaction.

If this stage also proves ineffective, in the third stage, he requests a reduction in punishment, saying: "Punish me, but less."

If that too fails due to the magnitude of his sin, he requests a respite (delay), and this is the final attempt to escape punishment.

But the Quran says: the deeds of these wrongdoers are so ugly and the burden of their sins so heavy that they find no permission for defense, nor do they succeed in gaining satisfaction, nor is there any reduction in their case, nor any respite!

In the following verse, the discourse continues regarding the grim future of the polytheists due to their worship of idols. It says that on the scene of Resurrection, when the fabricated deities and the humans who were worshipped like idols are placed alongside their worshippers, "when those who associated partners [with Allah] see their 'partners,' they will say: 'Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You'" (وَ إِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ).

They will claim: "These deities also tempted us into this act and were in fact partners in our crime; therefore, assign a portion of our punishment to them."

At this moment, by the command of Allah, "the idols will throw back the word at them, [saying]: 'Indeed, you are liars!'" (فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

"We were neither partners to Allah, nor did we tempt you, nor are we condemned to accept a portion of your punishment."

Here, several points should be noted

1- The expression "their partners" (شُرَكاءَهُمْ)

The use of "their partners" instead of "partners of Allah" (شرکاء الله) is because the idols were never partners of the Lord; rather, these were imaginary, illusory, and false partners that the polytheists had fabricated for themselves. Thus, it is better that they be attributed to the polytheists themselves rather than to Allah.

Furthermore, as we have seen previously, the polytheists used to dedicate a portion of their livestock and crops to the idols, making them their "partners."

2- [The idols also appear in the arena of Resurrection]

It is understood from the above verse that the idols also appear in the arena of Resurrection. Not only the humans who were worshipped, such as Pharaoh and Nimrod, but even the inanimate idols are present there.

In Verse 98 of Surah Al-Anbiya, addressing the polytheists, We read: "Indeed, you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of Hell" (إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ).

3- [The polytheists confess that they worshipped these deities]

In the verse above, we read that the polytheists say on that day: "We used to worship these deities." Of course, this statement is not a lie that the idols would want to deny.

However, it is possible that this denial is because the fabricated deities deny their own worthiness for worship, or that the worshippers added another sentence: "O Allah, these deities were also partners in tempting us," and in response, they hear: "You are lying; we had no power to tempt you."

4- [The expression "they will throw back the word at them" (فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ)]

The expression "they will throw back the word at them" instead of "they said to them" is perhaps because the idols have no power of speech of their own. If they speak, it is due to the inspiration of the Lord; that is, Allah casts a word into them, and they cast that same word back at their worshippers.

The next verse adds that after this speech and hearing the response, "they will all offer submission to Allah on that day." The pride, arrogance, and blind prejudices of these ignorant worshippers will vanish upon seeing the face of Truth, and they will bow their heads in submission before Him (وَ أَلْقَوْا إِلَى اللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ السَّلَمَ) [1]. At this moment, when everything becomes as clear as the sun, "all the lies and fabrications of this foolish group will be lost and vanished from them" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ).

Both the false attribution of being Allah's partner will fade away, as will the notion of the idols' intercession before Allah, for they will clearly see that not only can the idols do nothing, but they themselves become the fuel of Hell and turn against their worshippers!

Up to this point, the description was of the state of the misguided polytheists who were immersed in their shirk and deviation without calling others to this path. Then, It describes the state of those who, while being misguided themselves, insist on misguiding others as well, saying: "Those who disbelieved and barred [others] from the way of Allah, We shall add punishment over punishment for them because of the corruption they used to spread" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ زِدْناهُمْ عَذاباً فَوْقَ الْعَذابِ بِما كانُوا يُفْسِدُونَ).

They carry both the burden of their own responsibility and are partners in the crimes of others, for they were the agents of corruption on earth and the misguidance of Allah's creation, and they prevented people from traversing the path of Truth.

We have repeatedly reminded that from the perspective of Islamic social logic, whoever establishes a good or evil tradition is a partner in all the actions of those who follow that tradition. In a famous Hadith, we read: "Whoever establishes a good tradition, for him is the reward of those who act upon it without their rewards being diminished, and whoever establishes an evil tradition, the sin of all those who act upon it shall be written for him without anything being diminished from their sins."

In any case, these soul-shaking expressions of the Quran and Hadiths reveal the responsibility of leaders before Allah and Allah's creation.

Then, following the mention of the existence of a witness in every nation a few verses prior, It returns to that discussion with more explanation, saying: "And [remind them of] the day when We shall raise up in every nation a witness against them from among themselves" (وَ يَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

Although Allah's knowledge encompasses everything, the existence of these witnesses—especially those who have risen from among the nations themselves—is a further emphasis on the permanent supervision over human actions and a warning regarding this certain and perpetual monitoring.

Although this general rule includes the Muslims and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله as well, It specifically mentions it for further emphasis, saying: "And We shall bring you [O Muhammad] as a witness over these [people]" (وَ جِئْنا بِكَ شَهِيداً عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ).

Based on what has been said, "these" (هؤلاء) refers to the Muslims who lived in the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the observer and witness of their deeds. Naturally, after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, there must be someone among the Ummah who serves as a witness to their actions—a witness who is pure from any sin or error so that he can properly fulfill the right of testimony.

For this reason, some commentators (both from the Shi'a and Sunni scholars) have sought to take this verse as evidence for the existence of a witness and a just Proof (Hujjah) in every age and time. Of course, its interpretation according to the Shi'a belief regarding the existence of an Infallible Imam in every age is perfectly clear, but for the scholars of the Sunni school, its justification is not so easy.

Perhaps because of this difficulty, Fakhr al-Razi in his Tafsir fell into a justification that is not without flaws. He says: "From this verse, we conclude that no age among the ages is devoid of a witness over the people. The witness must not be prone to error; otherwise, he too would need another witness, and this matter would continue in an infinite regress. The conclusion is that in every age there must be a group whose word is a Proof, and there is no way for this except to say that the consensus (Ijma') of the Ummah is a Proof" (meaning the collective body of people in any time, as a whole, never follows the path of error) [1]. Had Fakhr al-Razi stepped slightly outside the boundaries of his own beliefs, he certainly would not have fallen into such a biased statement, for the Quran says: "For every nation We placed a witness of their own kind," not that the entire Ummah is a Proof and witness for every individual of the Ummah.

Of course, as we explained in detail under Verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa, two other possibilities have been given in the interpretation of "these" (هؤلاء): first, that "these" refers to the witnesses of previous nations, i.e., the Prophets and their successors, and thus the Prophet of Islam is a witness both over this Ummah and over the past Prophets. Second, that the witness means a "practical witness"—that is, someone whose existence is a model and a criterion for measuring truth and falsehood (for more explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 391 onwards).

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 20, p. 98.

And since appointing a witness is secondary to the fact that a complete and comprehensive program has already been placed at the disposal of individuals—such that the proof is completed for all, so that subsequently the issue of supervision and monitoring finds a correct meaning—It immediately says: "And We have sent down to you the Book (the Quran) as an exposition of everything" (وَ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"It is both a guidance and a mercy, and a source of glad tidings for all the Muslims of the world" (وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ).

Key Points

1- The Quran is the Explainer of Everything

The most important topic raised in the above verses is the Quran being "an exposition of everything" (tibyanan li-kulli shay’).

"Tibyan" (with a kasra or fatha on the 't') has an infinitive meaning, i.e., "to explain" [1]. From this expression, considering the breadth of the concept of "everything," one can well argue that in the Quran, there is an explanation for everything. However, noting that the Quran is a book of education and human-building, revealed for the perfection of the individual and society in all spiritual and material aspects, it becomes clear that "everything" refers to all matters necessary for traversing this path. It does not mean that the Quran is a vast encyclopedia containing all the details of mathematical, geographical, chemical, physical, botanical sciences, and the like—even though the Quran has made a general call to acquire all sciences and knowledge, and all the aforementioned sciences and others are included in this general call. Additionally, occasionally, in accordance with monotheistic and educational discussions, it has pulled back the curtain from sensitive parts of sciences and knowledge. Nevertheless, that for which the Quran was revealed and which constitutes its primary and ultimate goal is the issue of human-building, and in this field, it has neglected nothing.

Sometimes it places its finger on the details of these issues and explains all the intricacies (such as the rules for writing commercial contracts and debt documents, which are explained in the longest verse of the Quran, i.e., Verse 282 of Surah Al-Baqarah, through 18 rulings!) [1]. And sometimes it presents the vital issues of man in general and more general forms, such as the verse whose commentary follows this discussion: "Indeed, Allah commands justice, and good conduct, and giving to relatives, and He forbids immorality, and bad conduct, and oppression."

The breadth of these concepts is like the breadth of the concept of "fulfilling the covenant" in the verse "Indeed, the covenant will be questioned" (Al-Isra, 34), and "fulfilling contracts" in the verse "Fulfill the contracts" (Al-Ma'idah, 1), and "the necessity of fulfilling the right of Jihad" in the verse "And strive for Allah with the striving due to Him" (Al-Hajj, 78), and "establishing equity and justice" in the verse "That the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice" (Al-Hadid, 25), and "attention to order in all fields" in the verse "And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance, that you not transgress within the balance. And establish weight in justice and do not make deficient the balance" (Surah Ar-Rahman, 7-9), and "refraining from any corruption on earth" in the verse "And do not cause corruption upon the earth after its reformation" (Al-A'raf, 85), and the "call to reflection, thought, and reason" which appears in many verses of the Quran. Such human-encompassing programs that can be path-breaking in all fields are clear evidence that in the Quran there is an explanation for everything.

It has not even left the branches of these general commands undecided; it has explained the channel through which these programs must be expounded, saying: "And whatever the Messenger has given you—take; and what he has forbidden you—refrain from" (Al-Hashr, 7).

The more a person swims in the boundless ocean of the Quran and dives into its depths to extract the pearls of the programs for salvation, the more the greatness of this Heavenly Book and its inclusiveness and comprehensiveness become apparent.

Precisely for this reason, those who, in the field of these programs, stretch their hands of need here and there have certainly not recognized the Quran, and they "beg from the stranger for what they themselves possess."

This verse, in addition to specifying the authenticity and independence of Islamic teachings in all fields, makes the burden of responsibility for Muslims heavier, telling them: through continuous and persistent study of the Quran, extract whatever you need from it.

In Islamic narrations, the comprehensiveness of the Quran has been greatly emphasized, especially in relation to this verse and its likes.

Among them, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, revealed in the Quran the exposition of everything, until, by Allah, He left out nothing that the servants need, so that no servant can say, 'If only this were revealed in the Quran,' except that Allah has already revealed it therein." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, did not leave anything that this Ummah needs except that He revealed it in His Book and explained it to His Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله, and He set a limit for everything, and placed a proof upon it to point to it, and set a limit and punishment for whoever exceeds that limit." 2

Even in Islamic narrations, there is a clear indication of the fact that in addition to the outward aspect of the Quran and what the masses and scholars understand from it, the inward aspect of the Quran is also an ocean in which many issues that our minds do not reach are hidden. This part of the Quran has a specific and complex knowledge that is at the disposal of the Prophet and his true successors, as we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "There is no matter over which two people differ except that it has a root in the Book of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, but the minds of men do not reach it." [3]

The lack of general access to this part—which can be likened to "man's subconscious"—will never prevent the conscious and outward part from being utilized by everyone.

2- Four Stages of Guidance

Interestingly, in the above verse, four expressions are mentioned for the purpose of the Quran's revelation:

1- Tibyanan li-kulli shay’: "The Quran is the explainer of everything." 2- It is a source of guidance (Huda). 3- It is a means of mercy (Rahmah). 4- In the final stage, it says it is a source of glad tidings for all Muslims (Bushra lil-Muslimin).

If we think correctly, there is a clear logical link between these four stages. For in the path of guiding and directing humans, the first stage is explanation and awareness. It is certain that after awareness comes guidance and finding the way, and following that are the actions which will be the source of mercy. Finally, when a person performs positive and righteous deeds, he finds himself before the infinite reward of Allah, which is the source of glad tidings and joy for all travelers of this path.

[1]: Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani quotes from some Arabic linguists that all infinitives on the weight of tif‘al are with a fatha on the 't' except for two words: tibyan and tilqa’. Additionally, some have considered this word an infinitive (masdar) and others a quasi-infinitive (ism masdar). [1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 283 onwards. [1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 74.

[3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 75.

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ وَإِيتَآئِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَيَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْبَغْىِ ۚ يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٩٠)
Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness, and generosity towards relatives, and He forbids indecency, wrongdoing, and aggression. He advises you, so that you may take admonition. (90)

Commentary

The Most Comprehensive Social Program

Following the previous verse, which described the Quran as an exposition of all things, these verses present an example of the most comprehensive Islamic teachings regarding social, human, and ethical matters.

In the first verse, six important principles are observed: three are positive aspects and commanded (Ma'mur bihi), and three are negative aspects and forbidden (Manhi 'anhu).

At the beginning, it says: "Indeed, Allah orders justice and good conduct and giving to relatives" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَ الْإِحْسانِ وَ إِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌).

What law can be imagined that is broader, more engaging, and more comprehensive than "Justice" ('Adl)? Justice is the very law upon which the entire system of existence revolves; the heavens, the earth, and all creatures are sustained by justice (بالعدل قامت السماوات و الارض - "By justice, the heavens and the earth were established").

Human society, which is a small corner of this vast universe, cannot remain apart from this universal law and continue its healthy life without justice.

We know that justice, in the true sense of the word, means that everything should be in its proper place. Therefore, any form of deviation, excess, deficiency, transgression of limits, or violation of the rights of others is contrary to the principle of justice.

A healthy human being is one whose bodily systems each perform their own function without increase or decrease. However, as soon as one or more systems fall short in performing their duty or step into the path of transgression, signs of disorder immediately appear throughout the body, and illness is inevitable.

The whole of human society is also like a human body, which will be ill without observing the principle of justice.

However, since justice—despite all its power, majesty, and deep impact—is not sufficient on its own in critical and exceptional situations, the command for "Good Conduct" (Ihsan) immediately follows it.

To put it more clearly, sensitive moments arise in human life where solving problems through the principle of justice alone is not possible; rather, there is a need for altruism, forgiveness, and sacrifice, which must be realized using the principle of "Ihsan."

For example, a treacherous enemy has attacked a society, or a storm, flood, or earthquake has devastated part of a country. If people in such conditions were to wait for, say, just taxes and other ordinary laws to solve the problem, it would be impossible. This is where all those who possess greater resources in terms of intellectual, physical, and financial power must engage in sacrifice and give as much as they can; otherwise, a tyrannical enemy might destroy their entire society, or painful natural disasters might completely paralyze a large group.

Coincidentally, these two principles also naturally govern the organization of a human body. In a normal state, all bodily systems have mutual service toward one another; every organ works for the whole body and benefits from the services of other organs (this is the principle of justice).

But sometimes an organ is injured and loses its reciprocal capacity. Is it possible in this state for the rest of the organs to consign it to oblivion because it is incapacitated? Is it possible for them to stop supporting and nourishing the injured organ? Certainly not (and this is Ihsan).

In the whole of human society, these two states must also prevail; otherwise, it is not a healthy society.

In Islamic reports and the words of commentators, various statements are seen regarding the difference between these two—justice and good conduct—which mostly return to what we mentioned above.

In a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Justice is fairness (Insaf), and good conduct (Ihsan) is grace (Tafaddul)." "Justice is that you deliver the rights of people to them, and Ihsan is that you bestow grace upon them." [^1] This is the same point mentioned above.

Some have said justice is performing obligatory acts (Wajibat) and Ihsan is performing recommended acts (Mustahabbat).

Others have said justice is Monotheism (Tawhid), and Ihsan is performing obligatory acts (according to this interpretation, justice refers to belief and Ihsan to action).

Some have said justice is the harmony of the outward and the inward, and Ihsan is that a person's inward state be better than their outward.

Others have considered justice to relate to practical aspects and Ihsan to relate to speech.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 231.

However, as we said, some of these interpretations are consistent with the interpretation we provided above, and others are not in conflict with it and can be integrated.

As for the issue of kindness to relatives (إِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌), it is actually a part of the issue of Ihsan, with the difference that Ihsan is for the whole society, while "giving to relatives" is specifically for kinsfolk and family, which is considered a small society. Given that the large society is composed of smaller societies—namely family units—whenever these smaller units enjoy greater cohesion, its effect appears in the whole society. In fact, duties and responsibilities are correctly divided among the people, because each group primarily attends to the weak among their own relatives, and in this way, all such individuals come under the protective cover of their kin.

In some Islamic traditions, we read that by "relatives" (Dhi al-Qurba), the relatives of the Prophet—namely the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام—are meant, and by "giving to relatives," the payment of Khums is intended.

This interpretation never intends to limit the concept of the verse; rather, there is no obstacle to the verse remaining in its broad concept, and this part being considered one of the clear instances of that general concept.

In fact, if we take "Dhi al-Qurba" to mean relatives in an absolute sense—whether relatives in family and lineage or other close ones—the verse takes on a broader meaning that even includes neighbors, friends, and the like (although the well-known meaning of Dhi al-Qurba is kinsfolk). The issue of helping small societies (i.e., relatives and kinsfolk), because it enjoys a strong emotional backing, will benefit from a greater guarantee of implementation.

After completing these three positive principles, He addresses three negative and forbidden principles and says: "And He forbids immorality and bad conduct and oppression" (وَ يَنْهى‌ عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَ الْمُنْكَرِ وَ الْبَغْيِ‌).

Commentators have also said much regarding these three expressions: "Fahsha" (immorality), "Munkar" (bad conduct/evil), and "Baghy" (oppression). However, what seems most appropriate to the literal meaning and the context of comparing these qualities with one another is that "Fahsha" refers to hidden sins, "Munkar" refers to manifest sins, and "Baghy" is any form of transgressing one's right, injustice, and self-superiority over others.

Some commentators [^1] have said: The source of moral deviations is three powers: The carnal power (Shahwani), the irascible power (Ghadhabi), and the satanic imaginative power (Wahmi).

The carnal power calls man to seek as much pleasure as possible and drowns him in "Fahsha" and ugliness. The irascible power compels man to commit "Munkarat" (evil deeds) and harass people. The satanic imaginative power awakens in man the sense of seeking superiority, leadership, exclusivity, and "transgressing the rights of others" (Baghy) and compels him to these acts.

Allah, with the three aforementioned expressions, has warned against the rebellion of these instincts and, with a comprehensive statement that encompasses all moral deviations, has guided toward the path of truth.

At the end of the verse, as a renewed emphasis on all these six principles, He says: "He admonishes you that perhaps you will be reminded" (يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Vol. 20, p. 104.

The Most Comprehensive Verses of Good and Evil

The attraction of the content of this verse and its manner of expression is such that we read in a narration that "Uthman ibn Maz'un," who is a well-known companion of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, says:

"In the beginning, I had accepted Islam only outwardly and not with my heart and soul. The reason was that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeatedly proposed Islam to me, and I accepted out of shyness. This situation continued until one day I was in his presence and saw him deeply immersed in thought. Suddenly, he fixed his eyes toward the sky as if receiving a message. When he returned to his normal state, I asked about the matter. He said: 'Yes, while I was speaking with you, I suddenly observed Gabriel, who brought this verse to me: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَ الْإِحْسانِ وَ إِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌...' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله read the verse to the end for me. The content of the verse affected my heart so much that from that very hour, Islam settled in my soul. I went to Abu Talib, the Prophet's uncle, and informed him of the event. He said: 'O tribe of Quraysh, follow Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and you will be guided, for he invites you to nothing but noble morals.' Then I went to 'Walid ibn Mughirah' (the famous Arab scholar and one of the leaders of polytheism) and read this same verse to him. He said: 'If this speech is from Muhammad himself, he has spoken very well, and if it is from his Lord, it is also very well.'" [^1]

In another Hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله read this verse to Walid ibn Mughirah. Walid said: "O son of my brother, [^2] read it again." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeated it.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: This expression is because Walid ibn Mughirah was the uncle of Abu Jahl, and both were from Quraysh.

Walid said: "Indeed, it has a sweetness, and indeed it has a radiance; its top is fruitful, and its base is overflowing, and it is not the word of a human" (ان له لحلاوة، و ان عليه لطلاوة، و ان اعلاه لمثمر، و ان اسفله لمغدق، و ما هو قول البشر). [^1]

In another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The sum of piety (Taqwa) is in the words of Allah the Almighty: 'Indeed, Allah orders justice and good conduct...'" [^2]

From the above Hadiths and numerous other Hadiths, it is well understood that the above verse, as a general Islamic instruction and one of the articles of the Islamic constitution and its universal charter, has always been of interest to Muslims. To the extent that according to a Hadith, whenever Imam Baqir عليه السلام performed the Friday prayer, his last words in the prayer sermon were this very verse, followed by this supplication: "O Allah, make us among those who are reminded and for whom the reminder is beneficial" (اللهم اجعلنا ممن‌يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنْفَعَهُ الذِّكْرى‌). Then he would descend from the pulpit. [^3]

Reviving the three principles of justice, good conduct, and giving to relatives, and fighting against the three deviations of immorality, evil, and oppression on a global level, is sufficient to build a prosperous, peaceful world free from any misery and corruption. And if it is narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, the famous companion, that "this verse is the most comprehensive verse of good and evil in the Quran," it is for this very reason.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 78. [^3]: Al-Kafi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 77.

The content of the above verse reminds us of a striking Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "There are two groups from my Ummah who, if they are righteous, my Ummah will be righteous, and if they are corrupt, my Ummah will be corrupt." They asked, "O Messenger of Allah, who are these two groups?" He said: "The jurists (scholars) and the rulers!"

The late "Muhaddith Qummi" in "Safinat al-Bihar," after quoting this Hadith, quotes another appropriate Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "He said: On the Day of Resurrection, the Fire will speak to three: a ruler, a reciter (scholar), and a possessor of wealth. It will say to the ruler: 'O you to whom Allah gave authority but you did not act with justice,' and it will swallow him as a bird swallows a sesame seed. It will say to the reciter: 'O you who beautified yourself for the people but confronted Allah with sins,' and it will swallow him. And it will say to the wealthy: 'O you to whom Allah gave a vast and flowing world and the lowly asked you for a small loan but you refused out of stinginess,' and it will swallow him." [^1]

Regarding justice, which is a very important Islamic pillar, we provided a detailed discussion under verse 8 of Surah Al-Ma'idah (refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 299).

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 30.

Ayahs

وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا عَـٰهَدتُّمْ وَلَا تَنقُضُوا۟ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِهَا وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٩١)
Fulfill Allah’s covenant when you pledge, and do not break [your] oaths, after pledging them solemnly and having made Allah a witness over yourselves. Indeed Allah knows what you do. (91)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّتِى نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَهَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنكَـٰثًا تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ هِىَ أَرْبَىٰ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَبْلُوكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٩٢)
Do not be like her who would undo her yarn, breaking it up after [spinning it to] strength, by making your oaths a means of [mutual] deceit among yourselves, so that one community may become more affluent than another community. Indeed Allah tests you thereby, and He will surely clarify for you on the Day of Resurrection what you used to differ about. (92)
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
Had Allah wished, He would have made you one community, but He leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes, and you will surely be questioned concerning what you used to do. (93)
وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌۢ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِهَا وَتَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِمَا صَدَدتُّمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٩٤)
Do not make your oaths a means of [mutual] deceit among yourselves, lest feet should stumble after being steady and [lest] you suffer ill for barring from the way of Allah and face a great punishment. (94)

Occasion of Revelation

The great commentator, al-Tabarsi, in Majma' al-Bayan, states regarding the occasion of revelation for the first of the above verses:

At a time when the Muslim population was small and their enemies were numerous, and it was possible that some believers—due to this disparity and a feeling of loneliness—might break their allegiance (bay'ah) to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and withdraw their support, the above verse was revealed to warn them in this regard.

Commentary

The Firmness of Your Covenants is Proof of Your Faith!

Following the principles mentioned in the previous verse as a series of fundamental Islamic tenets regarding justice, benevolence, and the like, these verses address another part of the most important Islamic teachings: the issue of fulfilling covenants, remaining committed to treaties, and standing firm by oaths.

First, He says: "And fulfill the covenant of Allah when you have taken a covenant with Him" (وَ أَوْفُوا بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ إِذا عاهَدْتُمْ‌).

Then He adds: "And do not break oaths after their confirmation" (وَ لا تَنْقُضُوا الْأَيْمانَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِها).

"While you have sworn by the name of Allah and have made Allah a surety (kafil) and guarantor over your oath" (وَ قَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللَّهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلًا).

"For indeed, Allah knows all that you do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ‌).

Although commentators have mentioned several interpretations for "the covenant of Allah" ('ahd Allah), it appears that it refers to the covenants people make with God (and obviously, a covenant with His Prophet is also a covenant with Him). Therefore, it includes every divine covenant and allegiance in the path of faith, Jihad, and otherwise.

In fact, considering that legislative duties announced by the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) are always accompanied by a type of implicit divine covenant, and intellectual duties—granted by virtue of being given reason, intelligence, and talent—are also coupled with such an implicit treaty, all of these fall within this broad "Covenant of Allah."

As for the issue of "oaths" (ayman, plural of yamin meaning oath) mentioned in the verse, which has also been subject to various interpretations, given the context of the sentence, it has a broad meaning. It includes both the commitments a person makes to Allah through an oath and the commitments made to God's creation while relying on an oath.

In other words, any commitment made under the name of God and by swearing by His name is included in this sentence, especially since the phrase "while you have made Allah a surety over you" (وَ قَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللَّهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلًا) follows it as an explanation and emphasis.

The conclusion is that the phrase "fulfill the covenant of Allah" is specific, while the phrase "do not break oaths" is general.

Since the issue of fulfilling covenants is one of the most important supports for the stability of any society, the next verse follows up with a tone of reproach and blame, saying: "And do not be like the woman who broke her yarn into pieces after it was strongly spun" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّتِي نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَها مِنْ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنْكاثاً [1]). This refers to the story of a woman from the Quraysh named "Raytah" during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). From morning until noon, she and her handmaidens would spin the wool and hair they had, and then she would order them all to unravel it. For this reason, she became known among the Arabs as "the foolish woman" (al-Hamqa').

This act is particularly foolish because wool gains new strength and perfection through spinning, and by unraveling it, a regressive move is made that is not only fruitless but harmful. Similarly, those who accept a commitment by covenanting with God and in the name of Truth, any move they make toward breaking this covenant is not only futile but also evidence of the degradation and fall of their character.

Then He adds: "You should not break your oaths and use them as a means of deception, fraud, and corruption among yourselves just because one group is more numerous than another" (تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلًا بَيْنَكُمْ أَنْ تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ هِيَ أَرْبى‌ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ) [1]. This is evidence of a person's weak character and spiritual frailty, or evidence of their deceit and betrayal—that upon seeing the numerical superiority of the opponents, they abandon their true religion and join a degraded and baseless creed simply because its followers are in the majority.

"Be aware, Allah only tests you by this" (إِنَّما يَبْلُوكُمُ اللَّهُ بِهِ‌).

The day you are in the majority and the enemy is in the minority is not the day of testing; the days of testing are those when a multitude of enemies stands against you and you appear fewer and weaker than them.

In any case, "Allah will certainly make clear to you on the Day of Resurrection that which you used to differ about, and He will lift the veil from the secrets of the hearts," and He will recompense everyone for their deeds (وَ لَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ ما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

Since the discussion of divine testing and the emphasis on faith and fulfilling duties often creates the misconception of whether it is difficult for Allah to forcibly compel all humans to accept the truth, the next verse addresses this misconception, saying:

[1]: Ankath is the plural of nakth, meaning to unravel wool or hair after it has been spun. It is also used for unraveling clothes woven from wool or hair. Regarding the grammatical position of ankath in the verse, some consider it an emphatic hal (circumstantial adverb), and others consider it the second object for naqadat, meaning "she made her yarn into unraveled pieces."

"If Allah had willed, He would have made you one nation" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً).

"One nation" in terms of faith and accepting the truth, but in a compulsory form. However, it is obvious that such acceptance of truth is neither a step toward perfection nor a reason for pride for the one who accepts it. Therefore, the Sunnah (way) of Allah is to leave everyone free so that they may seek the path of truth by their own choice.

But this freedom does not mean that no help is provided by Allah to those who seek this path. Rather, those who step onto the path of truth and strive (mujahadah), Allah's success (tawfiq) encompasses them, and in the light of His guidance, they reach their destination. And those who step onto the path of falsehood are deprived of this blessing and their wandering increases.

Therefore, He immediately says: "But He leaves to stray whom He wills and guides whom He wills" (وَ لكِنْ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

However, this divine guidance and misguidance never absolves you of responsibility, because the first steps are taken by you. For this reason, He adds: "And you will certainly be questioned about what you used to do" (وَ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This expression, which on one hand attributes the performance of deeds to the humans themselves and on the other hand emphasizes their responsibility for their actions, is among the clear indicators that explain the concept of divine guidance and misguidance in the previous sentence—that this guidance and misguidance never have a compulsory nature.

We have discussed this topic previously (refer to the commentary on Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 26, in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh).

Then, to emphasize the issue of fulfilling covenants and standing firm by oaths—which are among the important factors of social stability—He says: "And do not take your oaths as a means of deception and hypocrisy among yourselves" (وَ لا تَتَّخِذُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلًا بَيْنَكُمْ‌). [1]

Because this act has two great harms: First, "it causes feet to slip after they were firmly planted in faith" (فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِها).

For when you make a covenant and take an oath but do not intend to fulfill it and repeat this act, the capital of people's trust in you vanishes, and some of the believers become weak and shaky in their faith. It is as if they placed their foot on a seemingly firm spot, but it was slippery underneath and caused them to slip.

The other harm is that "you will taste the evil consequences of this act—that is, hindering from the way of Allah—in this world, and a great punishment from Allah awaits you in the next world!"

In reality, breaking covenants and violating oaths leads, on one hand, to people's pessimism and hatred toward the true religion, the scattering of ranks, and mistrust, to the point that people show no desire to accept Islam. If they make a treaty with you, they do not consider themselves bound to fulfill it, and this itself is a source of much distress, defeat, and bitterness in this world.

On the other hand, it brings you divine punishment in the other abode.

Key Points

1. The Philosophy of Respecting Covenants and Treaties

We know that the most important capital of a society is the mutual trust of its members toward one another. Fundamentally, what transforms a society from scattered individuals and links them together like the links of a chain is this principle of mutual trust, which is the backbone of coordinated social activities and large-scale cooperation.

Covenants, treaties, and oaths are an emphasis on preserving this solidarity and mutual trust. But on the day when covenants and treaties are broken one after another, no trace of this great capital of public trust will remain, and the seemingly organized society will turn into scattered individuals lacking power.

For this reason, the issue of fulfilling covenants and standing firm by oaths has been repeatedly emphasized in the verses of the Quran and extensively in Islamic hadiths, and breaking them is considered among the major sins (kaba'ir).

Commander of the Faithful Ali (peace be upon him), in his "Decree to Malik al-Ashtar," pointed to the importance of this issue in both Islam and the Age of Ignorance, calling it the most important and universal issue. He emphasized that even the polytheists adhered to this matter because they had realized the painful consequences of breaking covenants. [1] In Islamic military instructions, we read that even if a single ordinary soldier grants protection (aman) to an individual or individuals from the enemy army, it must be respected by all Muslims!

Historians and commentators say that among the things that caused large groups to accept Islam—the great divine religion—in the early days of Islam was the Muslims' commitment to their covenants and their observance of their oaths.

This matter is so important that we read in a narration from Salman al-Farsi: "This nation will perish because of its breaking of covenants!" [1] That is, just as fulfilling covenants and treaties leads to power, glory, and progress, breaking covenants causes weakness, inability, and destruction.

In the history of Islam, we read that when the Muslims defeated the Sassanid army during the era of the second Caliph and Hormuzan, the great general of the Persian army, was captured, he was brought before Umar. The Caliph said to him, "You made covenants with us many times and broke them; what is the reason?"

Hormuzan said, "I fear that you will kill me before I can state the reason!" The Caliph said, "Do not fear."

At this moment, Hormuzan asked for water, and soon a simple and worthless vessel filled with water was brought to him.

Hormuzan said, "Even if I die of thirst, I will never drink water from such a vessel!" The Caliph said, "Bring a vessel of water that is acceptable to him." The vessel was given to him, and he looked around and did not drink the water, saying, "I fear that I will be killed while I am busy drinking the water."

The Caliph said, "Do not fear! I give you assurance; we will have nothing to do with you until you drink."

Hormuzan suddenly overturned the vessel and spilled the water on the ground! The Caliph, thinking the water had spilled involuntarily, said, "Bring him more water and do not kill him thirsty."

Hormuzan said, "I do not want water; my intention was to obtain protection (aman) from you."

The Caliph said, "I will kill you in any case."

But Hormuzan replied, "You gave me protection and assurance."

The Caliph said, "You are lying; I did not give you protection."

Anas was present and said, "Hormuzan is telling the truth; you gave him protection. Did you not tell him 'I have nothing to do with you until you drink the water'?" The Caliph was stumped and said to Hormuzan, "You deceived me, but I was deceived so that you might accept Islam."

Upon witnessing this scene (and the Muslims' commitment to their covenant), the light of faith began to shine in Hormuzan's heart, and he became a Muslim. [1]

2. Unjustified Excuses for Breaking Covenants

The ugliness of breaking a covenant is such that few people are willing to explicitly take responsibility for it. Therefore, covenant-breakers usually resort to excuses, however hollow and baseless they may be, an example of which we read in the above verses: sometimes some Muslims, on the excuse that the enemy had a numerical majority and the believers were in the minority, would hesitate in continuing their covenant with God and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). However, neither is numerical majority a proof of power and strength—as it has often been seen that a determined and faithful minority has triumphed over a vast majority lacking faith—nor can the power and strength of the enemy (assuming it is achieved through large numbers) be a justification for breaking a covenant with friends. For if we analyze this, it is actually a type of polytheism (shirk) and alienation from God.

This same subject has manifested itself in another form in our era, where some seemingly small Islamic governments, out of fear of the great colonial powers, refuse to fulfill their commitments to the believers. They prioritize the insignificant power of weak humans over the infinite power of God; they rely on other than God, fear other than God, and even sacrifice their covenants for this purpose—all of which are phenomena of polytheism and idolatry.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letters of Ali (peace be upon him), Letter 53. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [1]: Adapted from Tarikh al-Kamil, Vol. 2, p. 549. [1]: Dakhal (rhyming with daghal) means corruption, hidden enmity, and fraud, and is derived from the same root as dakhal meaning "inside." It is also necessary to note that according to what we interpreted above, the phrase "you take your oaths..." is a hal (circumstantial) clause, but some commentators have considered it an interrogative clause; the first interpretation is more consistent with the appearance of the verse.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٩٥)
Do not sell Allah’s covenants for a paltry gain. Indeed, what is with Allah is better for you, should you know. (95)
مَا عِندَكُمْ يَنفَدُ ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ بَاقٍ ۗ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
That which is with you will be spent [and gone], but what is with Allah shall last, and We will surely pay the patient their reward by the best of what they used to do. (96)
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُۥ حَيَوٰةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٧)
Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do. (97)

Occasion of Revelation

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, narrates from Ibn Abbas that a man from the inhabitants of Hadramaut came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah! I have a neighbor named Imru' al-Qays who has usurped a portion of my land. People bear witness to my truthfulness, but because they hold him in higher regard, they are unwilling to support me."

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله summoned Imru' al-Qays and questioned him regarding this matter. In response, he denied everything. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله proposed that he take an oath, but the complainant said: "O Messenger of Allah! This man is unscrupulous and has no qualms about swearing a false oath." The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله replied: "In any case, there is no other way; you must either bring witnesses or submit to his oath." When Imru' al-Qays stood up to take the oath, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله granted him a respite (and said: "Reflect upon this and take the oath later").

The two departed, and at that moment, the first and second verses under discussion were revealed (warning them against false oaths and their consequences).

When the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله recited these two verses to them, Imru' al-Qays said: "It is the truth; whatever is with me will eventually perish, and this man speaks the truth—I have usurped a portion of his land, though I do not know exactly how much. Now that it is so, let him take as much as he wishes (and knows is his right), and I shall add an equivalent amount to it because of the benefit I have derived from his land during this time." At this point, the third verse under discussion was revealed (giving glad tidings of a "Pure Life" to those who perform righteous deeds coupled with faith).

Commentary

What Does a Pure Life Depend On?

Following the previous verses which spoke of the ugliness of breaking covenants and false oaths, the first verse under discussion also emphasizes the same point, with the difference that the motivation for breaking covenants and false oaths in the preceding verses was the issue of being intimidated by numerical superiority, whereas here the motivation is the acquisition of worthless material gains.

Therefore, It says: "And do not exchange the covenant of Allah for a small price" (وَ لا تَشْتَرُوا بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا).

This means that whatever price you receive in exchange for it is small and insignificant; even if the entire world were given to you, it would not equal the value of a single moment of fulfilling a divine covenant.

Then, as a reason, He adds: "Indeed, what is with Allah is better for you, if you only knew" (إِنَّما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In the next verse, He explains the reason for this superiority as follows: "Whatever is with you will eventually perish and vanish, but what is with Allah remains and is everlasting" (ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ باقٍ‌). Material benefits, however great they may appear, are nothing more than a bubble on the surface of the water, whereas the divine reward—which, like His Holy Essence, is eternal—is superior and higher than all of these.

Then He adds: We will surely reward those who practice patience and steadfastness in the path of obeying Our command (especially in standing firm regarding oaths and covenants) according to the best of their deeds (وَ لَنَجْزِيَنَّ الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

The expression "the best" (احسن) indicates that their good deeds are not all equal; some are good and some are better, but Allah counts them all according to the "better" ones and grants them the reward of the "better" deeds. This is the height of generosity—for example, if a person offers several types of goods for sale: excellent, good, and average, and the buyer purchases them all at the excellent price! Furthermore, the phrase "And We will surely reward those who were patient..." is not devoid of an allusion to the fact that patience, steadfastness, and perseverance in the path of obedience—and especially being committed to covenants and promises—are among the best of human deeds.

In a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha, we read: "Patience and steadfastness in relation to faith are like the head to the body; there is no good in a body that has no head, nor in faith that has no patience." [1]

Following that, He states as a general law the result of righteous deeds coupled with faith, regardless of who performs them or in what form they are realized, in this world and the next. He says: "Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer - We will surely cause him to live a pure life, and We will surely give them their reward [in the Hereafter] according to the best of what they used to do" (مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَ لَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Thus, the sole criteria are "faith" and the "righteous deeds born from it," and there are no other conditions or restrictions regarding age, race, gender, or social status.

The result of this righteous deed born of faith in this world is a "Pure Life" (Hayat Tayyibah), meaning the realization of a society accompanied by tranquility, security, prosperity, peace, affection, friendship, cooperation, and constructive human concepts. It is safe from the disorders, pains, and sufferings that arise from arrogance, oppression, rebellion, egoism, and monopolization, which darken the sky of life.

This is on one hand, and on the other hand, God will reward them "according to the best of their deeds," the commentary of which was mentioned in the previous verses.

Key Points

1. Eternal Capitals

The nature of life in this material world is annihilation and destruction. The strongest buildings, the most enduring governments, the most powerful human beings, and anything even more solid than them will eventually become old, worn out, and then destroyed; all, without exception, are subject to decay.

However, if these beings can somehow be linked to the Pure Essence of God and be employed for Him and in His path, they take on the color of eternity. For His Pure Essence is eternal, and anything that has a relationship with Him acquires the hue of eternity:

Righteous deeds are eternal; martyrs have eternal life; the Prophets, Imams, true scholars, and mujahideen in the path of truth have a history coupled with eternity, because they all bear the Divine color.

For this reason, the above verses warn us: Come and save the capitals of your existence from perishability, and store them in a savings box that the hand of time cannot reach, and the passing of night and day cannot drive toward annihilation.

Come and employ your capitals for God and in the path of God's creation and to seek His pleasure, so that they become instances of "with Allah" (عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌), and according to "what is with Allah remains" (ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ باقٍ‌), they become lasting and established.

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, we read: "When the son of Adam dies, his hope is cut off from everything except three things: ongoing charity (charitable works that continue to serve people and are in the path of God), knowledge from which people benefit, and a righteous child who prays for him." [1] This is because all of these are for God and in His path and bear the color of eternity.

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read: "How different are two types of deeds: a deed whose pleasure vanishes but its consequence remains, and a deed whose hardship vanishes but its reward remains." [2]

2. Equality of Men and Women

Undoubtedly, men and women have differences in terms of body and soul, and for this reason, they differ in holding social positions, and each task is suited to someone. However, none of these are reasons for a difference in their human personality or a difference in their status before God. In this regard, both are completely equal. For this reason, the criterion that governs their personality and spiritual status is only one criterion: faith, righteous deeds, and piety, to which the access of both is equal.

The above verses explicitly state this truth and silence the idle talkers who, in the past or present, doubted the human personality of women or considered her status lower than the human status of men. Simultaneously, it reveals the logic of Islam regarding this important social issue and proves that, contrary to the thoughts of the narrow-minded, Islam is not a "masculine" religion; it belongs to women as much as it belongs to men.

Both genders, if they walk the path of righteous deeds—a positive and constructive step aided by faithful motivations—will both equally possess a "Pure Life," and both will enjoy equal reward and compensation before God, and their social standing will also be similar, unless one surpasses the other in terms of faith and righteous deeds.

3. The Roots of Righteous Deeds are Nourished by Faith

"Righteous deed" (Amal Salih) has such a broad and expansive meaning that it includes all positive, useful, and constructive activities in all scientific, cultural, economic, political, and military fields, and the like.

It encompasses everything from the invention of a scientist who has labored for years to help humanity, to the struggles of a martyr who sacrifices his life and offers the last drops of his blood on the battlefield of truth and falsehood; and from the pain and suffering a faithful mother endures during childbirth and then for the upbringing of her child, to the efforts scholars make to write their valuable books.

From the greatest tasks, such as the mission of the Prophets, to the smallest programs, such as moving a small stone from the middle of the road—all are gathered within this broad concept.

Here, the Question arises: why is this righteous deed restricted by the condition of faith, while it is possible to perform it without faith, as has been seen in some cases?

The Answer to this question lies in one point: if the motivation of faith is not present, the deed often becomes tainted. If it does not become tainted, it is very exceptional. But if the roots of the tree of righteous deeds are nourished by the water of monotheism and faith in Allah, it is less likely that pests such as vanity, hypocrisy, ostentation, self-centeredness, deception, and reminding others of favors will find their way into it. For this reason, the Holy Quran often links these two together, because their connection is an objective and inseparable reality.

It is necessary to clarify this issue with an example: suppose two people have each started building a hospital. One has a divine motivation and does this to serve God's creation, but the other's goal is ostentation and reaching a social position.

In a superficial view, we might think that ultimately both are building a hospital and people will benefit from their work equally. It is true that one has a divine reward and the other does not, but their deeds do not apparently differ from each other.

However, as we said, this is the result of a superficial study. If we look a bit more closely, we will see that these two deeds themselves differ in various aspects. For instance, the first person goes to a neighborhood of the city where the underprivileged and needy are more numerous, and sometimes acts in an obscure neighborhood that is not on a main thoroughfare. But the second person goes to a spot that is more in the public eye, even if the need there is much less.

The first person, in choosing materials and the quality of construction, considers the distant future and builds the foundation such that it lasts for hundreds of years. But the second person often seeks to have the building erected and inaugurated sooner to create a clamor and achieve his desired result.

The first person seeks to strengthen the inner quality of the work, while the second person clings to the outward glitter and glamour. Likewise, in choosing medical departments, doctors, nurses, and other requirements of this hospital, there is a great difference between the two, because different motivations always manifest themselves throughout the entire course of the action, or in other words, they color the action with their own hue.

4. What is the Pure Life?

Commentators have provided several interpretations for the meaning of "Hayat Tayyibah" (Pure Life):

Some have interpreted it as "lawful sustenance" (Rizq Halal). Some as "contentment" (Qana'ah) and being pleased with one's lot. Some as "daily bread." Some as "worship coupled with lawful sustenance." And some as "success in obeying God's command" and the like.

But perhaps it does not need mentioning that the concept of "Pure Life" is so broad and expansive that it includes all of these and more. A life that is pure from every perspective: pure from pollutions, oppressions, betrayals, enmities, captivities, humiliations, and all kinds of anxieties and anything that makes the clear water of life unpleasant to the human palate.

However, considering that it is followed by mention of divine reward in the "best" manner, it is understood that the "Pure Life" pertains to this world and the "best reward" pertains to the Hereafter.

Interestingly, in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in Nahj al-Balagha, we read: "He was asked about the word of Allah Almighty: 'We will surely cause him to live a pure life.' He said: 'It is contentment (Qana'ah).'" [1] Undoubtedly, the concept of this interpretation is not to limit the Pure Life to contentment, but rather it is the statement of an instance—but a very clear instance. For if the whole world were given to a person but the spirit of contentment were taken from him, he would always live in suffering, pain, and anxiety. Conversely, if a person possesses the spirit of contentment and stays away from greed and avarice, he is always at ease and happy.

Similarly, in some other narrations, "Pure Life" has been interpreted as "satisfaction and pleasure with what God has given," which is close in horizon to contentment.

But these concepts should never be given a sedative aspect; rather, the main goal of expressing satisfaction and contentment is to end greed, avarice, and egoism, which are the causes of transgressions, exploitations, wars, and bloodshed, and sometimes the cause of humiliation and captivity.

[1] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 82. [1] Irshad al-Daylami. [2] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 121. [1] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 229.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ (٩٨)
When you recite the Quran, seek the protection of Allah against the outcast Satan. (98)
إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ سُلْطَـٰنٌ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٩٩)
Indeed he does not have any authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord. (99)
إِنَّمَا سُلْطَـٰنُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِهِۦ مُشْرِكُونَ (١٠٠)
His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]. (100)

Commentary

Read the Quran Like This

We have not forgotten that several verses prior, it was mentioned that the Quran contains the explanation of all things (تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ), followed by a mention of some of the most important divine commands found within the Quran.

The verses under discussion explain the method of utilizing the Holy Quran and how to recite it; for the richness of the Quran's content alone is not enough—obstacles must also be removed from our being and from the environment of our thoughts and souls so that we may attain that rich content.

Therefore, it first says: "When you recite the Quran, seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the rejected" (فَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ الرَّجِيمِ‌).

Of course, the intention is not merely the verbal utterance of the phrase "I seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the rejected" (اعوذ باللَّه من الشيطان الرجيم).

Rather, adopting its character is the condition. That is, the mention of this phrase should be a prelude to the realization of a state within the human soul and spirit: a state of attentiveness toward God, a state of detachment from rebellious whims and desires that hinder correct understanding and perception, and a state of alienation from prejudices, vanities, selfishness, and self-centeredness that compel a person to use everything—even the words of God—to benefit their deviant desires.

Until such a state is created in the human spirit and soul, understanding the truths of the Quran is impossible for him; on the contrary, he might use the Quran, through "interpretation based on personal opinion" (Tafsir bi-ra'y), as a means to justify his polytheistic desires.

The next verse is, in fact, a reason for what was stated in the previous verse; it says: "Indeed, he has no authority over those who believe and rely upon their Lord" (إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ‌).

"His authority is only over those who take him as a friend and have chosen him for leadership and guardianship" (إِنَّما سُلْطانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ‌). "And those who associate him as a partner with Allah in obedience and servitude" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ‌).

They consider the command of Satan, instead of the command of Allah, as obligatory to follow!

Key Points

1- Obstacles to Knowledge

No matter how clear and brilliant the face of truth may be, it cannot be perceived until it is placed before a seeing eye. In other words, two things are necessary for the recognition of truths: the manifestation of the face of truth and possessing the means of sight and perception.

Can a blind person ever see the disc of the sun? Can the deaf hear the soul-stirring melodies of the world? Similarly, those who do not have a truth-seeking eye are deprived of seeing the face of reality, and those who do not have a truth-hearing ear are deprived of hearing the verses of truth.

What prevents a person from losing the power of recognition?

Undoubtedly, in the first degree, false preconceptions, carnal desires, blind and extreme prejudices, and being caught in the clutches of selfishness and vanity—in short, anything that disrupts the clarity of the heart and the purity of the human soul and gives it dark and murky colors—all of these are obstacles to understanding the truth.

The beauty of the Beloved has no veil or curtain, But settle the dust of the road so you may behold!

Until the soul is purified from prohibitions, The heart will not become a mirror for Divine Light!

In a Hadith, we read: "If it were not for the devils hovering around the hearts of the children of Adam, they would have looked upon the kingdom and the inner reality of the heavens." (لو لا ان الشياطين يحومون حول قلوب بنى آدم لنظروا الى ملكوت السماوات).

For this reason, the first condition for travelers on the path of truth is self-purification and piety (Taqwa), without which a person becomes trapped in the darkness of illusion and wanders in the wilderness.

And when we see the Quran saying: "A guidance for the pious" (هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌), it is also an allusion to this reality.

We have often seen people who approach the verses of the Quran with prejudice, stubbornness, and individual or group preconceptions; instead of understanding the truth from the Quran, they impose what they themselves want upon the Quran. In other words, they seek what they desire in the Quran, not what Allah has expressed. Instead of the Quran being a source of guidance for them, it increases their deviation (of course, not the Quran itself, but their rebellious desires). "As for those who believe, it has increased them in faith and they rejoice. But as for those in whose hearts is a disease, it has added filth to their filth, and they died while they were disbelievers!" (Surah At-Tawbah, 124-125).

Therefore, it must be stated clearly that the purpose of the above verse is not that we should suffice only with saying "I seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the rejected." Rather, we must transform this "remembrance" (dhikr) into "thought" (fikr), and thought into an "inner state," and at the time of reading every verse, seek refuge in Allah so that the whispers of Satan do not become a veil between us and His life-giving Word.

2- Why do we [seek refuge in Allah] from the rejected Satan?

"Rajim" (رجيم) is from the root "rajm" (رجم), meaning to expel or drive away. Originally, it meant to pelt with stones, and subsequently, it was used to mean expulsion.

Here, among all the attributes of Satan, his being "rejected" is mentioned. This reminds us of his arrogance in the face of Allah's call to prostrate and show humility before Adam. This arrogance caused a veil to be established between Satan and the understanding of truths, to the extent that he imagined himself superior to Adam and said: "I am better than him; You created me from fire and created him from clay." This rebellion and pride even led him to object to Allah's command—an objection that became the cause of his disbelief and expulsion.

It seems that by using the term "Rajim," the Quran wants to convey this reality: that at the time of reciting the Quran, distance yourselves from arrogance, pride, and prejudice so that you do not meet a fate like that of the rejected Satan, and instead of perceiving the truth, you do not fall into the abyss of disbelief and lack of faith.

3- Those under the banner of Truth and those under the flag of Satan?

In the above verses, people are divided into two groups: those under the authority of Satan and those outside this authority. For each of these two groups, two attributes are mentioned.

Those outside the authority of Satan possess faith and reliance upon their Lord. That is, in terms of belief, they are monotheists only, and in terms of action, they are independent of everything and reliant upon Allah—not reliant upon weak and dependent humans, or upon whims, prejudices, and stubbornness.

But those under the authority of Satan: firstly, they have accepted his leadership in terms of belief (يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ‌), and secondly, in terms of action, they have considered Satan a partner to Allah in obedience; that is, they practically follow his command.

Of course, it cannot be denied that there are humans who strive and place themselves in the first group, but later, due to being distanced from divine educators, or being placed in polluted environments, or for other reasons, they fall into the second group.

In any case, the above verses once again emphasize the truth that Satan's authority over humans is not forced, mandatory, or unconscious; rather, it is humans themselves who provide the conditions for Satan's entry into the environment of their souls and, so to speak, give him the permit to pass through the gate of the heart.

4- Etiquettes of Quranic Recitation

Everything requires a program, especially benefiting from a great book like the Quran. For this reason, the Quran itself mentions etiquettes and conditions for the recitation and utilization of these verses:

1- First, it says: "None touch it except the purified" (لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ‌). This expression may refer both to physical cleanliness—that touching the script of the Quran must be accompanied by ritual purity and ablution—and to the fact that understanding the concepts and content of these verses is only possible for those who are pure from moral vices, so that the ugly traits that cast a veil over the human eye of truth do not deprive them of observing the beautiful face of truth.

2- At the beginning of the recitation of the Quran, one must seek refuge in Allah from the rejected Satan who is driven from the Divine presence, as we read in the above verses: "When you recite the Quran, seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the rejected" (فَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ الرَّجِيمِ‌).

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in response to the question of how to act upon this instruction and what to say, he said: "Say: I seek refuge in the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing, from Satan, the rejected" (استعيذ بالسميع العليم من الشيطان الرجيم).

In another narration, we read that the Imam, at the time of reciting Surah al-Hamd, said: "I seek refuge in Allah, the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing, from Satan, the rejected, and I seek refuge in Allah lest they attend me" (اعوذ باللَّه السميع العليم من الشيطان الرجيم، و اعوذ باللَّه ان يحضرون). As we said above, this seeking of refuge must not be limited to words and speech; rather, it must penetrate the depths of the soul and spirit such that the person, while reciting the Quran, detaches from Satanic traits and draws near to Divine attributes, so that the obstacles to understanding the Word of Truth are removed from the environment of his thought and he may correctly see the heart-ravishing beauty of truth.

Therefore, seeking refuge in Allah from Satan is necessary both at the beginning of the recitation of the Quran and throughout the entire duration of the recitation, even if it is not voiced.

3- The Quran must be recited in the form of "Tartil," meaning measuredly and accompanied by reflection: "And recite the Quran with measured recitation" (وَ رَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا) (Muzzammil, verse 4).

In a Hadith interpreting this verse, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "The Quran is not to be read rapidly and brokenly, but it should be recited with Tartil. When you pass by a verse in which there is a mention of the Fire, you stop there (and reflect) and seek refuge in Allah from the Fire!"

4- In addition to Tartil, the command for "contemplation and reflection" (Tadabbur and Tafakkur) on the verses of the Quran has been given where it says: "Do they not then reflect upon the Quran?" (أَ فَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ‌) (An-Nisa, 82). In a Hadith, we read that the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to learn the Quran from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ten verses at a time, and they would not learn the next ten verses until they knew what was in the first verses in terms of knowledge and action. [^1] In another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Recite the Quran eloquently and seek its wonders." [^2] Also, in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated: "Allah has manifested Himself to His creation in His speech, but they do not see." [^3] (Only the enlightened and the faithful thinkers observe His beauty in His word).

5- Those who hear the verses of the Quran also have a duty; their duty is to remain silent—a silence accompanied by thought and reflection: "And when the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent that you may receive mercy" (وَ إِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَ أَنْصِتُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌). [^1] Additionally, in Islamic narrations, instructions have been given regarding the recitation of the Quran with a beautiful voice, which certainly has a psychological effect on its concepts, the explanation of which is not appropriate here. [^2]

[^1]: Bihar, Vol. 92, p. 106. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 92, p. 107. [^3]: Al-A'raf, verse 204. [^4]: For more information, refer to Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 9, p. 190 onwards.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَآ ءَايَةً مَّكَانَ ءَايَةٍ ۙ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍۭ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠١)
When We change a sign for another in its stead—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, ‘You are indeed a fabricator.’ Indeed, most of them do not know. (101)
قُلْ نَزَّلَهُۥ رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ مِن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيُثَبِّتَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٠٢)
Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down duly from your Lord to fortify those who have faith and as guidance and good news for the Muslims. (102)
وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُۥ بَشَرٌ ۗ لِّسَانُ ٱلَّذِى يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِىٌّ وَهَـٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٠٣)
We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that instructs him.’ The language of him to whom they refer is non-Arabic, while this is a clear Arabic language. (103)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَهْدِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٠٤)
Indeed those who do not believe in the signs of Allah—Allah shall not guide them and there is a painful punishment for them. (104)
إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِى ٱلْكَذِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٠٥)
Only those fabricate lies who do not believe in the signs of Allah, and it is they who are the liars. (105)

Occasion of Revelation

Ibn Abbas says: When a verse was revealed containing a difficult command, and was subsequently followed by another verse providing a lighter instruction, the excuse-seeking polytheists would say: "Muhammad mocks and ridicules his companions; today he commands something and tomorrow he forbids the very same thing. This indicates that Muhammad says all of this from himself and not from Allah." At this point, the first verse was revealed to answer them.

Commentary

A Scandalous Lie!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the Quran and how to benefit from it. The verses under discussion address other aspects of issues related to the Quran, specifically the objections the polytheists raised against these divine verses. First, it says:

"And when We substitute a verse in place of a verse—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, 'You are but a fabricator!' But most of them do not know the truth of the matter" (وَ إِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

The truth is that the polytheists do not perceive what the function of the Quran is and what mission it carries. They do not know that the Quran seeks to build a human society—a progressive, prosperous, and free society with high spirituality. Indeed, "most of them do not know" (أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Nevertheless, it is obvious that this divine prescription, written for the salvation of these patients' lives, sometimes requires substitution and change. Today a prescription must be given, tomorrow it must evolve, and finally, the ultimate prescription must be issued.

Yes, they are unaware of these truths and uninformed about the conditions of the Quran's revelation; otherwise, they would know that the "abrogation" (naskh) of certain instructions and verses of the Quran is a precise and calculated educational program. Without it, the ultimate goal and the attainment of perfection would not be secured. For this reason, they imagined that this was evidence of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) contradictory speech or of fabricating lies against Allah.
