In reality, the issue of abrogation is an inevitable matter for a society transitioning from a highly decadent stage to sublime stages, because often an instantaneous transition is impossible and must take place step by step.

Can a chronic illness be cured in a single day? Or can a drug addict who has been contaminated for years be treated in one day? Is it anything other than the fact that there must be transitional stages in between? And is abrogation anything other than temporary programs during transitional periods?! (We discussed the issue of abrogation in Volume 1 under verse 106 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

The next verse follows up and emphasizes this same issue, commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Say: The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord in truth" (قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِ).

The "Holy Spirit" (Ruh al-Qudus) is the same messenger of divine revelation, "Gabriel the Trustworthy" (Jibra'il al-Amin). It is he who, by Allah's command, brings down the verses—both the abrogating and the abrogated—to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). These are verses that are all truth, all pursuing a reality, and that reality is nothing but the cultivation of human beings—a cultivation that sometimes necessitates abrogating and abrogated rulings to be achieved.

For this reason, it follows by saying: "The goal is to make those who believe more firm in their path, and it is a guidance and glad tidings for all Muslims" (لِيُثَبِّتَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ هُدىً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ).

According to the eminent commentator, the author of Tafsir al-Mizan, the above verse says regarding the believers: the goal is for them to be "fortified" in their path; but regarding the Muslims, it says: the goal is "guidance and glad tidings." This is due to the difference between a "Mu'min" (believer) and a "Muslim," as faith (iman) resides in the heart while Islam is in outward action.

In any case, to strengthen the power of faith and to traverse the path of guidance and glad tidings, there is sometimes no choice but to have short-term and temporary programs that later give way to the final and permanent program. This is the secret behind the existence of the abrogating and the abrogated in the divine verses.

This was the answer to one of the polytheists' excuses regarding the Quranic verses. Then, it points to another excuse—or more accurately, a slander by the opponents against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله)—stating:

"And We certainly know that they say, 'It is only a human being who teaches him'" (وَ لَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ).

Regarding whom the polytheists were referring to in this statement, commentators have various discussions: It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that it was a man named "Bal'am" in Mecca, whose profession was sword-making and who was originally a Christian from Rome. Some considered him a Roman slave belonging to the "Bani Hadram" tribe named "Ya'ish" or "A'ish" who embraced Islam and became a companion of the Prophet.

Others considered this statement a reference to two Christian slaves named "Yasar" and "Jabbar" who possessed a book in their own language and would occasionally read it aloud.

Some have also suggested the possibility that it refers to Salman al-Farsi, while we know that Salman came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in Medina and accepted Islam, whereas these types of slanders by the polytheists—especially considering Surah An-Nahl is largely Meccan—pertained to "Mecca."

In any case, the Quran strikes down these baseless claims with a crushing response, saying:

They do not realize that "the tongue of the one they point to is foreign (A'jami), while this Quran is in clear and eloquent Arabic" (لِسانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَ هذا لِسانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ) [1] and [2].

[1]: Yulhidun is from the root ilhad, meaning deviation from truth to falsehood, and sometimes it refers to any kind of deviation. Here it indicates that the slanderers intended to attribute the Quran to a human and call him the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) teacher.

[2]: I'jam and ujmah originally mean ambiguity, and A'jami is said of someone whose speech has a defect, whether they are Arab or non-Arab. Since the Arabs had incomplete knowledge of foreign speech, they addressed others as Ajam!

If their intention by this slander and fabrication is that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) teacher in teaching the words of the Quran was a human being foreign to the Arabic language, then this is the height of scandal. How could such a person produce expressions so eloquent and articulate that the native masters of the language were all powerless before them and lacked the ability to challenge even a single surah?!

And if their intention is that he took the content of the Quran from a foreign teacher, then the question arises: who performed the task of casting that content into the mold of such miraculous words and expressions, before which all the eloquent speakers of the Arab world knelt? Was it a person foreign to the Arabic language, or was it Someone whose power is above the power of all humans—namely, "Allah"?

Furthermore, the content of this Quran—in terms of its powerful philosophical logic regarding beliefs, its ethical teachings for nurturing human spirituality, and its comprehensive social laws regarding various needs—is such that it is beyond human thought. This shows that the slanderers themselves did not believe their own words; they only spoke such things for mischief and to whisper into the hearts of the simple-minded.

The reality is that the Arab polytheists could find no one among themselves to whom they could attribute the Quran. Therefore, they scrambled to find an unknown person whose life was obscure and ambiguous to the people of that region, attributing these matters to him in hopes of deceiving the simple-hearted for a few days.

Beyond all this, the history of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) life never shows that he had continuous contact with such individuals, whereas if they were truly the original innovators, such contact would have to exist. But as the old saying goes: "A drowning man clutches at every straw," they too resorted to such desperate measures.

Forget the time of the Quran's revelation and the environment of Arab ignorance; even today, with all the progress achieved in various fields of human civilization, and all these writings that are a powerful reflection of human thoughts in societies, and all these laws and systems that have been created—the superiority of the Quran's teachings over them is completely evident upon comparison.

Even according to Sayyid Qutb in the commentary Fi Zilal, a group of materialists in the Soviet Union, when they wanted to criticize the Quran at the Congress of Orientalists held in 1954 AD, said that this book could not have been the product of one man's brain—Muhammad—but must be the result of the efforts and endeavors of a large group! They even claimed it is unbelievable that all of it was written in the Arabian Peninsula; rather, parts of it were certainly written outside the Arabian Peninsula!! [1] On one hand, because according to their logic of denying the existence of God and the issue of revelation, they sought a material explanation for everything; and on the other hand, they could not believe the Quran was the product of a human brain in the Arabian Peninsula, they were forced to resort to a ridiculous interpretation and link it to a large population inside and outside Arabia—something that history completely denies! In any case, it is clearly understood from this verse that the miracle of the Quran is not only in its content, but the words of the Quran are also at the level of a miracle. The pull, attraction, sweetness, and specific harmony present in the words and phrasing are beyond human power.

[1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 282.

Then, with a threatening tone, it proceeds to state the truth that these accusations and deviations are all due to the deep-rooted lack of faith in their souls: "Indeed, those who do not believe in the verses of Allah—Allah does not guide them (neither to the straight path nor to the way of Paradise and eternal happiness), and for them is a painful punishment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ لا يَهْدِيهِمُ اللَّهُ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ). For they are so entangled in prejudice, stubbornness, and enmity toward the truth that they have lost the merit for guidance and are prepared for nothing but a painful punishment!

And in the final verse, it adds: "Only they fabricate lies who do not believe in the verses of Allah, and it is they who are the real liars" (إِنَّما يَفْتَرِي الْكَذِبَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ). They are the ones lying, not you, O Muhammad, because despite seeing all those verses, clear signs, and proofs—each more evident than the last—they still continue their slanders.

And what lie is greater than for a person to cast accusations against men of truth and create a barrier and obstacle between them and the masses who are thirsty for the truth?

Key Points

The Ugliness of Lying from the Perspective of Islam

The final verse is among the soul-shaking verses that speak about the ugliness of lying and place liars at the level of disbelievers and deniers of divine verses. Although the specific context of the verse is lying and fabricating against Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the ugliness of lying is generally personified in this verse.

1- Truthfulness and Fulfilling Trusts

Fundamentally, in Islamic teachings, extraordinary importance is given to the issue of truthfulness and the struggle against falsehood and lying. Some examples are summarized and listed below:

  1. Truthfulness and fulfilling trusts are two prominent signs of faith and human character; indeed, their indication of faith is even superior to and greater than prayer.

Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) says: "Do not look at the length of a man's bowing (ruku') and prostration (sujud), for that is something he may have become accustomed to, such that if he were to leave it, he would feel lonely. Rather, look at the truthfulness of his speech and his fulfillment of trusts." [1] Mentioning these two together (truthfulness and trust) is because they share a common root; for truthfulness is nothing but trust in speech, and trust is truthfulness in action.

2- Lying is the Source of All Sins

In Islamic narrations, lying is counted as the "key to sins." Ali (عليه السلام) says: "Truthfulness leads to righteousness, and righteousness leads to Paradise." [1] In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has placed locks for evil, and He made the key to those locks wine (since the main barrier to ugliness and evil is the intellect, and alcoholic drinks disable the intellect). Then he added: Lying is worse than wine." [2] Imam al-Askari (عليه السلام) says: "All wicked things have been placed in a room, and its key is lying." [3] The relationship between lying and other sins is from the perspective that a sinful person can never be truthful, because truthfulness would lead to his disgrace; to cover the traces of sin, he must usually resort to lying.

In other words, lying sets a person free to sin, while truthfulness limits them.

Coincidentally, this truth is perfectly personified in a hadith narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): A person came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: "I pray, but I also commit acts contrary to chastity and I tell lies! Which one should I give up first?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Lying." He committed in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) never to lie again.

When he left, satanic whispers for an unchaste act arose in his heart, but he immediately fell into thought: if the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asks him about this tomorrow, what should he say? If he says he did not commit such an act, that would be a lie; and if he tells the truth, the legal punishment (hadd) will be carried out upon him. In this way, regarding other wrongdoings, this way of thinking and subsequent self-restraint and avoidance arose for him. Thus, giving up lying became the source of giving up all his sins.

3- Lying is the Source of Hypocrisy

This is because truthfulness means the harmony of the tongue and the heart; therefore, lying is the disharmony of these two. Hypocrisy (nifaq) is also nothing but the difference between the outward and the inward.

In verse 77 of Surah At-Tawbah, we read: "So He penalized them with hypocrisy in their hearts until the Day they shall meet Him, because they broke their word to Allah and because they used to lie."

4- Lying is Incompatible with Faith

This reality is reflected not only in the verse under discussion but also in Islamic hadiths. In one hadith, we read: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) was asked: "Can a believer be a coward?" He said: "Yes." It was asked: "Can he be a miser?" He said: "Yes." It was asked: "Can he be a liar?" He said: "No!" [1] This is because lying is one of the signs of hypocrisy, and hypocrisy is incompatible with faith.

For this same reason, this statement is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام): "A servant does not taste the flavor of faith until he gives up lying, whether in jest or in earnest." [1] In another hadith, a similar meaning is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and at its end, the verse under discussion is cited as evidence.

5- Lying Destroys the Capital of Trust

We know that the most important capital of a society is mutual trust and public confidence. The most significant thing that destroys this capital is lying, betrayal, and deception. A major reason for the extraordinary importance of truthfulness and the abandonment of lying in Islamic teachings is this very subject.

In Islamic hadiths, we read that the leaders of the religion strictly forbade friendship with several groups, including liars, because they are not trustworthy.

Ali (عليه السلام) says in his short aphorisms: "Beware of befriending a liar, for he is like a mirage: he makes the distant seem near to you and the near seem distant." [2] Of course, the discussion regarding the ugliness of lying, its philosophy, the psychological causes of its emergence, and the ways to combat it is very extensive and should be sought in books of ethics. [3]

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, root "S-D-Q", quoting from al-Kafi. [1]: Mishkat al-Anwar of Tabarsi, p. 157. [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 254. [3]: Jami' al-Sa'adat, Vol. 2, p. 233.
