Ayahs

مَن كَفَرَ بِٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِهِۦٓ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُۥ مُطْمَئِنٌّۢ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَـٰكِن مَّن شَرَحَ بِٱلْكُفْرِ صَدْرًا فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠٦)
Excepting someone who is compelled [to recant his faith] while his heart is at rest in it, those who disbelieve in Allah after [affirming] their faith and open up their breasts to unfaith, Allah’s wrath shall be upon them and there is a great punishment for them. (106)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٧)
That, because they preferred the life of the world to the Hereafter and that Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (107)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (١٠٨)
They are the ones Allah has set a seal on their hearts, their hearing and their sight, and it is they who are the heedless. (108)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٠٩)
Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the losers in the Hereafter. (109)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا فُتِنُوا۟ ثُمَّ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ وَصَبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٠)
Thereafter your Lord will indeed be forgiving and merciful to those who migrated after they were persecuted, waged jihad and remained steadfast. (110)
۞ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجَـٰدِلُ عَن نَّفْسِهَا وَتُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١١١)
The day [will come] when every soul will come pleading for itself and every soul will be recompensed fully for what it has done, and they will not be wronged. (111)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse that it was revealed concerning a group of Muslims who fell into the clutches of the polytheists and were forced to recant from Islam and utter words of disbelief (kufr) and polytheism (shirk).

They were "Ammar," his father "Yasir," his mother "Sumayyah," as well as "Suhayb," "Bilal," and "Khabbab." Ammar's parents resisted firmly in this ordeal and were killed, but Ammar, who was young, uttered what the polytheists demanded.

This news spread among the Muslims; some condemned Ammar in his absence, saying: "Ammar has left Islam and become a disbeliever." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "It is not so."

ان عمارا ملاء ايمانا من قرنه الى قدمه و اختلط الايمان بلحمه و دمه:

"It is not so (I know Ammar well); Ammar is filled with faith from head to toe, and faith is mixed with his flesh and blood (he will never abandon faith nor join the polytheists)."

Before long, Ammar came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله weeping. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "What has happened?"

He replied: "O Prophet! It has gone very badly; they did not leave me alone until I spoke disrespectfully of you and mentioned their idols with goodness!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wiped the tears from Ammar's eyes with his blessed hand and said:

"If they pressure you again, say what they want (and save your life from danger)."

At this moment, the verse: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ ... was revealed and clarified the matters regarding this issue. [^1]

Commentary

Those Who Turn Back from Islam (Apostates)

In completion of the discussion of the previous verses, which spoke of the various schemes of the polytheists and disbelievers, the verses under discussion refer to another group of disbelievers, namely the apostates (murtaddin) and those who turn back from Islam.

The first verse says: "Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief—except for him who is forced while his heart is content with faith—yes, such people who open their breasts to disbelief again,"

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

"the wrath of Allah is upon them, and a great punishment awaits them" (مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَ قَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ وَ لكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ).

In fact, there is a reference here to two groups of people who take the path of disbelief after accepting Islam.

First, those who fall into the clutches of illogical enemies and, under their pressure and torture, declare renunciation of Islam and loyalty to disbelief, while what they say is only with the tongue and their hearts are overflowing with faith. This group is certainly forgiven; rather, no sin has been committed by them at all. This is the same permissible Taqiyyah (dissimulation) that is allowed in Islam for the preservation of life and the conservation of forces for greater service in the way of Allah.

The second group are those who truly open the gates of their hearts to disbelief and lack of faith, and completely change their ideological path. These people are afflicted by the wrath of Allah and His great punishment.

It is possible that "wrath" refers to their deprivation of Divine mercy and guidance in this world, and "great punishment" refers to their penalty in the hereafter. In any case, the expression used in this verse regarding apostates is very harsh, heavy, and shocking.

The next verse explains the reason for their apostasy as follows: "That is because they preferred the life of this world over the Hereafter, and for this reason, they once again stepped onto the path of disbelief" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ). "And Allah does not guide the disbelieving people (who persist in disbelief and denial)" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ).

In summary, when they embraced Islam, some of their material interests were temporarily endangered, and since they loved the world, they regretted their faith and returned to disbelief.

It is obvious that such a group, who have no internal pull toward faith, are not included in Divine guidance, for His guidance is contingent upon our will, effort, and struggle, as He Himself has said: وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا (Surah Al-Ankabut, last verse).

The next verse explains the reason for their lack of guidance as follows: "They are those upon whose hearts, hearing, and sight Allah has set a seal," such that they are deprived of seeing, hearing, and understanding the truth (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَ سَمْعِهِمْ وَ أَبْصارِهِمْ). And it is clear: "Such individuals, by losing all tools of cognition, are the truly heedless" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ).

We have said in the past that wrong actions and various sins leave adverse effects on a person's sense of discernment, understanding, and vision, and gradually take away the health of thought from him. The further he goes in this path, the veils of heedlessness and ignorance over his heart, eyes, and ears become firmer. Eventually, his state reaches a point where he has eyes but seemingly does not see, has ears but seemingly does not hear, and the gate of his soul is closed to all truths; the sense of discernment and power of distinction, which is the greatest Divine blessing, is taken from them.

"Tab'" (طبع), which here means "sealing," refers to the fact that

sometimes, to ensure that no one touches the contents of a box or opens its door, they close it tightly and, after closing, secure it with a special thread and place a seal on it, so that if it is opened, it will be immediately known. This expression here is a metaphor for absolute impenetrability.

In the next verse, the result of their work is depicted as follows: "Inevitably and certainly, they are the losers in the Hereafter" (لا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ). What loss and ruin could be worse than for a person to have all the necessary means for guidance and eternal happiness in hand, and due to whims and desires, lose all these assets.

And since, in contrast to the two previous groups—namely those who uttered blasphemous words as Taqiyyah under enemy pressure while their hearts were tranquil and full of faith, and those who returned to disbelief with full desire—there exists a third group who are the deceived ones. The next verse refers to their situation, saying: "Your Lord, for those who were deceived and turned back from faith but later repented and proved the sincerity of their repentance through migration (Hijrah), struggle (Jihad), and patience and steadfastness—yes, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful" (ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَ صَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ) [^1]. This verse is a clear proof for the acceptance of the repentance of an apostate, but the individuals in the verse

[^1]: The pronoun "after that" (بعدها), according to many commentators, refers to "fitnah" (trial/deception), and some have said it refers to the migration, struggle, and patience mentioned before it in the verse.

under discussion were previously polytheists and then became Muslims; therefore, they are considered "National Apostates" (Murtad Milli) rather than "Innate Apostates" (Murtad Fitri). [^1]

Finally, the last verse under discussion, as a general warning, says: "Remember the day when every soul will come pleading for itself" to save itself from the punishment and its painful penalty (يَوْمَ تَأْتِي كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها) [^2]. Sometimes sinners, to escape the clutches of punishment, completely deny their wrongdoings and say: وَ اللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ: "By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists" (Surah Al-An'am, verse 23). But when they see that these lies and deceits are not effective, they try to pin their sin on their misguided leaders, saying: رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا فَآتِهِمْ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً مِنَ النَّارِ: "Our Lord, these are the ones who misled us, so give them a double punishment of the Fire" (and give them our share of the punishment) (Surah Al-A'raf, 38).

But these struggles are in vain, and there "every soul will be paid in full for what it did," without any

[^1]: An "Innate Apostate" (Murtad Fitri) is someone born to a Muslim father or mother and has turned back from Islam after accepting it. However, a "National Apostate" (Murtad Milli) is someone whose parents were not Muslim at the time of his conception, but he later accepted Islam and then turned back from it.

[^2]: Regarding which verb "Day" (يوم) relates to, there is discussion among commentators. Some consider it related to a hidden verb and say the estimation was "Remember them on the Day when..."; others have considered it related to the verbs "Forgiveness" and "Mercy" derived from لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ in the previous verse (but in the commentary above, we practically preferred the first possibility for its comprehensiveness).

increase or decrease (وَ تُوفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ).

And no one will be wronged in the least (وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ).

Key Points

1- Taqiyyah and Its Philosophy

True Muslims, the students of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, possessed an extraordinary spirit of resistance against enemies. As we saw, some of them, like "Ammar's father," were not even willing to utter a single sentence with the tongue according to the enemy's wish, even though their hearts were full of faith in Allah and love for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and they even lost their lives in this path.

And some, like "Ammar" himself, who were willing to say a sentence with the tongue, were still overcome by terror throughout their being and considered themselves responsible and at fault, and they did not find peace until the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله assured them that their action was religiously permissible as a tactic to save their own lives! We read in the accounts of "Bilal" that when he embraced Islam and bravely rose to defend the logic of Islam and support the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the polytheists put him under severe pressure, to the point that they would drag him into the scorching sun and place a large boulder on his chest, telling him: "You must become a polytheist to God."

He refused, and while his breaths were numbered, he constantly said: "Ahad, Ahad" (He is the One God, He is the Unique God). Then he said: "By Allah, if I knew a word more unpleasant to you than this, I would say it!" [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 284.

And we read in the accounts of "Habib ibn Zayd" al-Ansari that when "Musaylimah the Liar" captured him, he asked him: "Do you bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Yes."

Then he asked him: "Do you bear witness that I am the messenger of Allah?" Habib said mockingly: "I do not hear your speech!" Musaylimah and his followers cut his body into pieces, and he remained firm like a mountain [^1]. Such shocking scenes are abundant in the history of Islam, especially in the accounts of the early Muslims and the companions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام.

For this reason, researchers have said that in such cases, breaking the barrier of Taqiyyah and not surrendering to the enemy is permissible, even if it costs a person's life, because the goal is to raise the banner of Monotheism and exalt the word of Islam; this matter had special importance particularly at the beginning of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission.

Nevertheless, there is no doubt that Taqiyyah is permissible in such cases as well, and in cases lower than that, it is obligatory. Contrary to what uninformed individuals imagine, Taqiyyah (of course, in its specific instances, not everywhere) is neither a sign of weakness, nor fear of the multitude of the enemy, nor surrender to pressure. Rather, Taqiyyah is considered a type of calculated tactic for preserving human resources and not wasting the lives of believers for small and insignificant matters.

It is common throughout the world that struggling and fighting minorities, in order to overthrow tyrannical and aggressive autocratic majorities, often use the method of "concealment." They form underground organizations, have secret programs, and it often happens that they disguise themselves as others, and even when captured,

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 284.

they strive hard to keep the reality of their work hidden, so as not to lose the forces of their group in vain and to save them for the continuation of the struggle.

No reason allows that in such conditions, the fighters who are in the minority should introduce themselves openly and publicly and be easily identified and destroyed by the enemy.

For this reason, before being an Islamic program, Taqiyyah is a rational and logical method for all humans who have been and are in a state of struggle with a powerful enemy.

In Islamic narrations, we also read that Taqiyyah is likened to a defensive shield.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says:

التقية ترس المؤمن و التقية حرز المؤمن:

"Taqiyyah is the shield of the believer, and Taqiyyah is the means of protection for the believer" [^1]. (Note that Taqiyyah here is likened to a shield, and a shield is a tool used only in the field of war and struggle with the enemy to protect revolutionary forces).

And if we see in Islamic hadiths that Taqiyyah is called a sign of religion and a mark of faith, and is counted as nine parts out of ten parts of religion, it is all for this reason.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 11; p. 425

Of course, the discussion regarding Taqiyyah is very extensive and this is not the place for its detailed explanation; the only goal was to know that what some say in condemnation of Taqiyyah is due to their ignorance and lack of information about the conditions of Taqiyyah and its philosophy.

Undoubtedly, there are cases where practicing Taqiyyah is forbidden, and that is when Taqiyyah, instead of being a cause for preserving forces, becomes a cause for the destruction or

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 11, Hadith 6 from Chapter 24 of the Chapters on Enjoining Good.

endangerment of the school of thought, or brings about a great corruption. In such cases, the barrier of Taqiyyah must be broken and its consequences accepted, whatever they may be. [^1]

2- Innate Apostates, National Apostates, and the Deceived

Islam does not act harshly toward those who have not yet accepted Islam (referring to the People of the Book) and invites them to Islam with continuous invitation and persistent logical propagation. Whenever they do not accept and are willing to have peaceful coexistence with Muslims according to the conditions of Dhimmah, it not only grants them safety but also takes responsibility for the protection of their property, lives, and legitimate interests.

But in the case of those who accept Islam and then recant, it is exceptionally strict, because this act causes instability in the Islamic society and is considered a type of uprising against the Islamic regime and government, and is often a sign of ill intent, causing the secrets of the Islamic society to fall into the hands of enemies.

Therefore, if such a person's father or mother was Muslim at the time of his conception—or to put it simply, if he is born a Muslim—and he turns back from Islam and it is proven in an Islamic court, his blood is considered permissible, his property must be divided among his heirs, his wife must be separated from him, and his repentance is not accepted outwardly. That is, these three rulings are executed regarding such a person in any case, but if he truly regrets, his repentance will be accepted before Allah (of course, if the offender is a woman, her repentance will be accepted absolutely).

But if the person who turned back from Islam was not born a Muslim, he is asked to repent,

[^1]: For a complete explanation regarding the issue of Taqiyyah, its rulings, philosophies, and various documents, please refer to our book "Al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah," Volume Three.

and if he repents, it will be accepted and all punishments will be removed.

Although the political ruling for an "Innate Apostate" might be perceived as a type of violence, imposition of belief, and deprivation of freedom of thought by those unaware of its content.

However, if we pay attention to the fact that these rulings do not pertain to someone who holds a belief internally and has not set out to express it, but rather only include someone who proceeds to express or propagate it, and in truth, revolts against the existing regime of society, it becomes clear that this harshness is not without reason and does not contradict the issue of freedom of thought. As we have said, similar laws exist in many Eastern and Western countries with some differences.

It is also necessary to note that: the acceptance of Islam must be according to logic; especially for someone born to a Muslim father or mother and raised in an Islamic environment, it seems very unlikely that he has not recognized the content of Islam. Therefore, his recantation and return is more similar to conspiracy and treason than to a mistake and lack of understanding of the truth, and such a person deserves such punishment.

Additionally, rulings are never subject to one or two individuals, but rather the whole must be considered generally. [^1]

[^1]: There is discussion among commentators regarding the syntax of the sentence "مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللَّهِ ...": some have considered it an explanation and clarification for the previous sentence and, so to speak, an appositive (badal) for "الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ", and others have considered it an appositive for "کاذبون". A group has also taken it as a subject (mubtada) whose predicate (khabar) is omitted, believing the estimation was: "Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief... then the wrath of Allah is upon them and for them is a great punishment." In truth, the recompense of the condition was omitted because the following sentence indicates it.

There is a fourth possibility which seems to be the best syntax: the predicate of this subject is exactly what has come in the verse itself and is not omitted, and the sentence "لكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً" is a renewed explanation of the subject, because the exception sentence has created a gap between it and the predicate. Such expressions are also seen in the literature of other languages; for example, in Persian we say: "Someone who becomes a disbeliever after faith—of course, with the exception of those who were put under pressure and whose hearts are full of faith—yes, such a person who has become a disbeliever after Islam is subject to Divine wrath" (Observe closely).

Ayahs

وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا قَرْيَةً كَانَتْ ءَامِنَةً مُّطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيهَا رِزْقُهَا رَغَدًا مِّن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَذَٰقَهَا ٱللَّهُ لِبَاسَ ٱلْجُوعِ وَٱلْخَوْفِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (١١٢)
Allah draws a parable: There was a town secure and peaceful, its provision coming abundantly from every place. But it was ungrateful toward Allah’s blessings. So Allah made it taste hunger and fear because of what they used to do. (112)
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١١٣)
Certainly there had come to them an apostle from among themselves, but they impugned him. So the punishment seized them while they were doing wrong. (113)
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (١١٤)
So eat out of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good, and give thanks for Allah’s blessing, if it is Him that you worship. (114)

Commentary

Those Who Disbelieved and Were Afflicted

We have repeatedly stated that this Surah is the Surah of Blessings—spiritual and material blessings in various fields—and in connection with those, other discussions have been introduced. In the verses under discussion, we read the consequence of ingratitude for divine blessings in the form of a concrete example.

First, It says: "Allah presents an example of a town that was secure and at rest" (ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً).

This settlement was so safe and secure that its inhabitants lived in it with total peace of mind, never forced to migrate or move away (مُطْمَئِنَّةً).

In addition to the blessing of security and peace, "its provision coming to it in abundance from every quarter" (يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ).

"But it—meaning its inhabitants—denied the favors of Allah, so Allah made it taste the garb of hunger and fear because of what they used to do" (فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ اللَّهِ فَأَذاقَهَا اللَّهُ لِباسَ الْجُوعِ وَ الْخَوْفِ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ).

Not only were their material blessings perfect, but they also enjoyed spiritual blessings, namely the presence of a messenger of Allah and his heavenly teachings: "And there had certainly come to them a messenger from among themselves, but they denied him" (وَ لَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوهُ).

"So the punishment seized them while they were wrongdoers" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَ هُمْ ظالِمُونَ).

By observing such vivid and clear examples, do not walk in the path of those heedless, oppressive, and ungrateful people regarding divine blessings: "So eat of what Allah has provided for you [which is] lawful and good. And be grateful for the favor of Allah, if it is Him that you worship" (فَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ حَلالًا طَيِّباً وَ اشْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ).

Key Points

1- Is it a Parable or a Historical Event?

In the above verses, when speaking of a prosperous and blessed region that fell into insecurity, hunger, and misery due to ingratitude, the term "mathalan" (an example/parable) is used. On the other hand, the verbs mentioned in this verse are in the past tense, which indicates the occurrence of such an event in reality.

Among the commentators, there is a discussion as to whether the goal was to present a general parable or to describe a specific external reality.

Proponents of the second possibility further debate where this region was:

A group believes it refers to the land of Makkah, and perhaps the expression "يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ" (its provision reached it abundantly from every place) strengthens this possibility, as this expression indicates that the region was not a producer of required materials but rather they were brought to it from outside. Furthermore, it is highly consistent with the phrase "يُجْبى‌ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَراتُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ" (the fruits of all things are brought to it) mentioned in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 57, which certainly refers to the land of Makkah.

However, the problem is that historically, such an event is not clearly seen regarding Makkah—where it was extremely blessed and secure one day, and then severely overtaken by famine and insecurity the next.

Others have said this story relates to a group of the Children of Israel who lived in a prosperous region and were afflicted with famine and insecurity due to ingratitude.

Evidence for this is a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: "A group of the Children of Israel had such a luxurious life that they even made small statues out of food items and sometimes cleaned their bodies with them. But eventually, their situation reached a point where they were forced to eat those same contaminated food items, and this is what Allah said in the Quran: 'ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً مُطْمَئِنَّةً ...'" [1] Other similar narrations have been cited from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام and the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, though one cannot rely on the chains of all of them; otherwise, the matter would be clear. [1]

There is also the possibility that the above verse refers to the story of the people of "Saba" who lived in the prosperous land of "Yemen." As the Quran recounts their life story in Surah Saba, verses 15-19, they had a very prosperous land, full of fruit, secure, and pure. Due to pride, rebellion, arrogance, and ingratitude for Allah's blessings, their land was so devastated and their population so scattered that they became a lesson for all.

The phrase "يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ" does not necessarily mean the region itself was not productive; rather, "every place" could mean the surroundings of that city and land, and we know that the products of a vast region are usually transferred to a central city or village.

It is also worth mentioning that there is no obstacle to the verse referring to all of these instances.

In any case, since history remembers many regions that suffered such a fate, no major problem remains in interpreting the verse. Although the lack of sufficient certainty in determining the exact location has led some commentators to consider it a general parable rather than a specific region, the literal sense of the verses does not fit this interpretation; rather, the expressions all suggest the existence of a concrete external reality.

[1] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 91 (Note that the above Hadith is from Tafsir al-Ayyashi, and the Hadiths in Tafsir al-Ayyashi are mursal [disconnected]). [1] Same source.

2- The Relationship Between Security and Abundant Provision

In the above verses, three characteristics are mentioned for this prosperous, happy, and blessed region: first, security; then, confidence in the continuation of life there; and after that, the issue of attracting provision and abundant food. In terms of natural order, these appear as links in a chain of cause and effect as presented in the verse. For unless there is security, no one finds confidence to continue living in a place, and until these two exist, no one is interested in production or organizing the economic situation.

This is a lesson for all of us and all those who wish to have a prosperous, free, and independent land: one must address the issue of "security" before anything else, then make the people hopeful about their future in that region, and subsequently set the economic wheels in motion.

However, these three material blessings reach perfection only when they are harmonized with the spiritual blessing of faith and monotheism. For this reason, in the above verses, after mentioning the three blessings, it says: "وَ لَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْهُمْ" (And there had certainly come to them a messenger from among themselves) commissioned to guide them. [1]

3- The Garb of Hunger and Insecurity

Interestingly, in the above verses, when describing the fate of these ungrateful people, it says: Allah made them taste the "garb" (clothing) of hunger and fear. That is, on one hand, hunger and fear are likened to clothing, and on the other hand, instead of "wearing," the word "tasting" is used.

[1] Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 12, p. 388.

This expression has prompted commentators to reflect more deeply on the point of this metaphor:

Of course, some metaphors, such as "tasting a garment," might not be common in the Persian language and might seem strange, but in another language like Arabic, it conveys a subtle point. As "Ibn al-Rawandi" said in response to "Ibn al-A'rabi" who asked him, "Can a garment be tasted?": "Even if you doubt the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam, you cannot doubt that he was an (eloquent) Arab." [1] In any case, this expression indicates that, firstly, famine and insecurity enveloped them so much that it was as if it surrounded them from every side like a garment, touching their bodies. Secondly, this famine and insecurity became so tangible to them that it was as if they were tasting it with their tongues. This is evidence of the ultimate poverty, misery, and lack of security that fills a person's entire existence and life.

In truth, just as in the beginning, the blessing of security and prosperity had filled their entire being, in the end, due to ingratitude, poverty and insecurity took its place.

4- Ingratitude and the Wasting of Divine Favors

In the narration cited above, we read that this affluent people became so caught up in pride and heedlessness that they even used useful and respected food items to clean their soiled bodies, and for this reason, Allah afflicted them with famine and insecurity.

[1] Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 20, p. 128.

This is a warning to all individuals and nations who are immersed in divine blessings, so they may know that any form of extravagance, waste, and squandering of blessings carries a penalty—a very heavy penalty.

This is a warning to those who always throw half of their extra food into the trash.

A warning to those who, for a table where three or four guests are invited, prepare colorful dishes equivalent to food for 20 people, and do not even give the leftovers to hungry human beings.

And a warning to those who store food in homes for personal use and in warehouses to sell at higher prices until it rots and spoils, yet are not willing to provide it to others at a cheaper rate or for free!

Yes, all of these carry punishment and penalties before Allah, and the least punishment is the withdrawal of these favors.

The importance of this issue becomes clearer when we realize that food resources on earth are not unlimited. In other words, corresponding to these materials, there are needy and hungry people, and any excess or negligence regarding them leads to the deprivation of a group of them.

For this reason, Islamic narrations have paid strict attention to this issue, to the extent that we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "My father used to be upset if he had to wipe his hand, which was soiled with food, with a cloth; rather, out of respect for the food, he would lick his hand, or if a child was beside him and something remained in their bowl, he would clean their bowl. He himself would say: 'Sometimes a small piece of food falls off the table and I search for it to the extent that the house servant laughs (wondering why I search for a tiny bit of food).' Then he added: 'A people lived before you to whom Allah gave abundant blessings, but they were ungrateful and destroyed food items pointlessly, so Allah took His blessings from them and afflicted them with famine!'" [1]

[1] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 91 (The end of the Hadith has been summarized and paraphrased).

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٥)
He has forbidden you only carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah. But if someone is compelled [to eat any of that], without being rebellious or aggressive, indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (115)
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَا تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ هَـٰذَا حَلَـٰلٌ وَهَـٰذَا حَرَامٌ لِّتَفْتَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ (١١٦)
Do not say, asserting falsely with your tongues, ‘This is lawful, and that is unlawful,’ attributing lies to Allah. Indeed those who attribute lies to Allah will not be felicitous. (116)
مَتَـٰعٌ قَلِيلٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١١٧)
[Their share of the present life is] a trifling enjoyment, and there will be a painful punishment for them. (117)
وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا مَا قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١١٨)
We forbade to the Jews what We have recounted to you earlier, and We did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves. (118)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٩)
Moreover, your Lord will indeed be forgiving and merciful to those who repent after they having committed evil out of ignorance and reform themselves. (119)

Commentary

Liars Shall Never Prosper

Since the previous verses discussed the pure blessings of Allah and the gratitude for these blessings, the verses under discussion follow them by speaking of real prohibitions and unreal prohibitions that were banned through innovation (bid'ah) in God's religion, so that this cycle may be completed.

First, it says: "Allah has only prohibited for you four things from animal food sources: carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and animals slaughtered in the name of other than Allah" (إِنَّما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَ الدَّمَ وَ لَحْمَ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَ ما أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ بِهِ) ^1. We have detailed the philosophy behind the prohibition of carrion, blood, and pork under the commentary of verse 173 of Surah Al-Baqarah (refer to Volume 1 under the same verse).

However, the contamination of these three is hidden from no one today. Carrion is a source of various microbes, and blood is more contaminated than all other parts of the body in terms of microbial activity. Pork is also a factor for several types of dangerous diseases. Beyond all this, as we mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, consuming pork and blood—aside from physical harms—leaves harmful psychological and moral effects on humans through their influence on hormones (carrion also carries the harm of consuming blood in addition to other harms, as it was not slaughtered and the blood did not exit it).

As for animals slaughtered in the name of other than "Allah" (where the names of idols were invoked instead of Bismillah as we say today, or no name was mentioned at all), the philosophy of its prohibition is certainly not hygienic, but rather moral and spiritual. We know that in Islam, the reason for something being Halal or Haram is not solely its hygienic aspects; rather, a portion of prohibitions is purely spiritual and related to the purification of the soul and moral issues. Sometimes, things are even prohibited for the sake of maintaining social order. The prohibition of animals slaughtered without the name of God is also due to its moral aspect, as on one hand, it is a struggle against the creed of polytheism and idolatry, and on the other, it is a turning of attention toward the Creator of these blessings.

Furthermore, from the overall content of this verse and the subsequent verses, it is understood that Islam recommends moderation in the consumption of meat. It neither prohibits this food source entirely like vegetarians, nor does it allow the consumption of every type of meat (even lizards, crabs, and various worms) like the people of the Age of Ignorance and a group of so-called civilized people of our time.

Answer to a Question

A question arises here: the above verse limits prohibited animals or prohibited animal parts to four categories, while we know there are many other prohibitions, such as the meat of predatory animals and various sea creatures except for scaled fish. Even in some other Surahs of the Quran, the number of prohibitions mentioned exceeds four (Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 3). Therefore, why is it restricted to four things?

The answer to this question, as we stated under verse 145 of Surah Al-An'am (Volume 6, page 15), is one point: the restriction here is what is termed "relative restriction" (hasr idafi). That is, the purpose of using "Innama" (only), which is for restriction, is to negate the innovations that the polytheists had regarding the prohibition of certain animals. In fact, the Quran says: "These are prohibited, not those which you claim."

It is also possible that these four subjects mentioned in the Quran are the primary, fundamental, and root prohibitions (even "the strangled" [munkhaniqah], i.e., animals that were choked and the like mentioned in verse 3 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, are included in these four because they are considered carrion). However, other prohibitions regarding animal parts or types of animals like predators are of a secondary degree, and thus their prohibition was issued in the Sunnah of the Prophet. In this way, the restriction in the verse can be a "real restriction" (reflect on this).

At the end of the verse, as is the Quran's method in many instances, it states the exceptions, saying: "Those who are forced to eat prohibited meats (for example, if they are left without food in a desert and their lives are in danger), provided they use it only to the extent of saving their lives and do not exceed the limit, there is no blame upon them, for Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَ لا عادٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

"Bagh" or "Baghi" is from the root "Baghy," meaning "seeking," and here it is interpreted as seeking pleasure or deeming Allah's prohibition as lawful.

"Ad" or "Adi" is from the root "Adw," meaning transgression, and here it refers to someone who consumes more than the necessary amount of these meats during a time of necessity.

Of course, in the narrations reached to us from the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, "Baghi" is sometimes interpreted as "oppressor" and "Adi" as "usurper." "Baghi" has even been defined as one who rises against the Imam, and "Adi" as a thief.

These narrations may indicate that the necessity for prohibited meats usually occurs during travels; if someone travels for the purpose of oppression, anger, or theft and becomes desperate for such meats, although he is obliged to use them to save his life, Allah will not forgive this sin for them.

In any case, these interpretations do not contradict what was mentioned in the general concept of the verse and can be reconciled.

The next verse explicitly explains the discussion regarding the groundless prohibitions of the polytheists that was implicitly mentioned before, saying: "And do not say about what your tongues describe as a lie, 'This is lawful and this is prohibited,' so as to invent a lie against Allah" (وَ لا تَقُولُوا لِما تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ الْكَذِبَ هذا حَلالٌ وَ هذا حَرامٌ لِتَفْتَرُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ) ^1. This means it is an obvious lie emanating only from your tongues that you declare things lawful or prohibited on your own (referring to the livestock, some of which they prohibited for themselves, some they considered lawful, and a portion of which they dedicated to idols).

Has Allah given you such a right to legislate? Is this not an invention and a lie against Allah?! Or have superstitious thoughts and blind imitation driven you to such innovations?! In verse 136 of Surah Al-An'am, it is clearly stated that in addition to this declaring of Halal and Haram, they would set aside a portion of their crops and livestock for Allah and a portion for the idols! Strangely, they would say: the portion set for the idols never reaches Allah, but the portion for Allah reaches the idols! Therefore, if the portion for "Allah" was damaged, it would not be compensated from the idols' portion, but if the idols' portion was damaged, it would be compensated from "Allah's" portion! They had many such superstitions.

Also, from verse 148 of Surah Al-An'am: "Those who associated partners with Allah will say, 'If Allah had willed, we would not have associated partners, nor would our fathers, nor would we have prohibited anything,'" it is well understood that they claimed this right for themselves to make things Halal or Haram, and believed that God agreed with their innovations (thus, they first created an innovation and established Halal and Haram, then attributed it to God, committing another fabrication) ^1. At the end of the verse, as a serious warning, it says: "Indeed, those who invent lies against Allah will never prosper" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ).

Fundamentally, lying and fabrication are the source of misery and lack of prosperity, regardless of whom they are about, let alone when they are directed at the Great Allah, where the sin and its evil effects are doubled.

^1: The structure of the sentence "وَ لا تَقُولُوا لِما تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ الْكَذِبَ" is such that the "Lam" is for causation, "Ma" in "Lima tasif" is "Ma Masdariyah," and "Kadhib" is the object of "Tasif," which together means: "Do not say 'this is Halal and this is Haram' because of your tongues' description of the lie."

The next verse explains this lack of prosperity as follows: "With these actions, they enjoy a brief enjoyment in this world, but in return, a painful punishment awaits them" (مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ). This "brief enjoyment" might refer to the dead fetuses of animals they deemed lawful for themselves and consumed, or it might refer to the satisfaction of their sense of selfishness and egoism achieved through these innovations, or that they used these means to strengthen the foundations of polytheism and idolatry, keeping the people occupied with them and ruling over them for a while—all of which was a "brief enjoyment" followed by a "painful punishment."

^1: For this reason, in the verse under discussion, the fabrication against God, mentioned with the "Lam," is introduced as the result and end-goal of their innovations (reflect on this).

Here, a question may be raised: why were things other than the four mentioned above prohibited for the Jewish people?

The next verse seemingly addresses this question, saying: "And to those who are Jews, We prohibited what We related to you before" (وَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا ما قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ).

This refers to the matters mentioned in verse 146 of Surah Al-An'am: "And to those who are Jews We prohibited every animal with undivided hooves; and of the cattle and the sheep We prohibited to them their fat, except what adheres to their backs or the entrails or what is joined with the bone. By that We repaid them for their injustice. And indeed, We are truthful."

In reality, these additional prohibitions served as a penalty and punishment for the injustices and oppressions of the Jews. Therefore, at the end of the verses under discussion, it adds: "And We did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves" (وَ ما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ).

In Surah An-Nisa, verses 160 and 161, we also read: "For wrongdoing on the part of the Jews, We made unlawful for them [certain] good foods which had been lawful to them, and for their averting from the way of Allah many [people], and [for] their taking of usury while they had been forbidden from it, and their consuming of the people's wealth unjustly."

Therefore, the prohibition of some meats for the Jews was a form of punishment, and the polytheists could never use it as an argument.

Furthermore, the things the polytheists had prohibited were neither in the Jewish religion nor in the Islamic religion; they were merely innovations rooted in superstitions (the verse under discussion may also point to the fact that you have done something that is not consistent with any Heavenly Book).

In the final verse under discussion, as is the Quran's method, it opens the doors of return to those who were deceived or are regretful, saying: "Then, indeed your Lord, to those who have done evil out of ignorance and then repent after that and reform, indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful" (ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَ أَصْلَحُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

It is noteworthy that, firstly, it considers "ignorance" as the cause of committing sin, because ignorance is the main factor in many sins, and it is such individuals who return to the path of truth after gaining awareness—not those who follow the wrong path knowingly and out of arrogance, pride, prejudice, or stubbornness.

Secondly: it does not limit the issue of repentance to heart-felt repentance and inner remorse; rather, it emphasizes its practical effect, considering "reform" and "compensation" as the completion of repentance. This is so we may remove the false idea from our minds that thousands of sins can be compensated with the single phrase "I seek forgiveness from Allah." No, errors must be practically compensated, and the parts of the human soul or society damaged by sin must be repaired and restored. This is true repentance, not mere lip service.

Thirdly: it emphasizes this matter so much that it repeats with the phrase "indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful" to stress that being included in divine forgiveness and mercy is only possible after repentance and reform.

In other words, the reality that the acceptance of repentance must follow remorse, compensation, and reform is expressed through three expressions within one verse: first by the word "Thumma" (then), then "after that" (min ba'di dhalik), and finally with the word "after it" (min ba'diha), so that those who are stained and commit sin after sin while saying "we hope for Allah's grace, forgiveness, and mercy" may cast away this incorrect thought.

Ayahs

إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ حَنِيفًا وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٢٠)
Indeed Abraham was a nation [all by himself], obedient to Allah, a Hanif, and he was not a polytheist. (120)
شَاكِرًا لِّأَنْعُمِهِ ۚ ٱجْتَبَىٰهُ وَهَدَىٰهُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٢١)
Grateful [as he was] for His blessings, He chose him and guided him to a straight path. (121)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٢٢)
We gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (122)
ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ أَنِ ٱتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٢٣)
Thereafter We revealed to you [saying], ‘Follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, who was not a polytheist.’ (123)
إِنَّمَا جُعِلَ ٱلسَّبْتُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٢٤)
The Sabbath was only prescribed for those who differed about it. Your Lord will indeed judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they differ. (124)

Commentary

Abraham was an Ummah (unto himself)!

We have stated that this Surah is the Surah of explaining blessings, and its purpose is to stimulate the sense of gratitude in human beings in a way that motivates them to recognize the Bestower of all these bounties.

In the verses under discussion, mention is made of a perfect example of a grateful servant of God, namely "Abraham," the hero of monotheism. This is particularly inspiring for Muslims in general and Arabs in specific, as they consider him their first leader and role model.

Among the outstanding qualities of this great man, five attributes are mentioned:

1- At the beginning, it says: "Indeed, Abraham was an Ummah" (إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً).

Regarding why the name "Ummah" (nation/community) was given to Abraham, commentators have mentioned various points, four of which are noteworthy:

  • Abraham possessed such a personality that he was an Ummah unto himself; for sometimes the radiance of a human's personality increases to such an extent that it transcends one or two individuals or a group, and his personality becomes equivalent to a great nation.

  • Abraham was a leader, a role model, and a great teacher of humanity, and for this reason, he is called "Ummah." This is because "Ummah," in its passive participle sense, refers to someone whom people follow and whose leadership they accept. Of course, there is a specific spiritual connection between this meaning and the first one, because whoever becomes a leader of truth and sincerity for a nation shares in the deeds of all of them, and it is as if he himself is an Ummah.

  • At that time, when there was no other worshiper of God in his environment and everyone was submerged in the quagmire of polytheism and idol-worship, Abraham was the sole monotheist. Thus, he alone was an Ummah, and the polytheists of his environment were another "ummah."

  • Abraham was the source of the emergence of a nation, and for this reason, the name Ummah was bestowed upon him.

There is no issue with this small word, "Ummah," encompassing all these great meanings. Yes, Abraham was an Ummah, a great leader, a nation-building man, and on that day when no one in his social environment spoke of monotheism, he was the great herald of Tawhid. [^1] In Arabic poetry, we also read: It is not beyond Allah's power To gather the entire world within one individual!

2- His other description was that he was "a servant obedient to Allah" (قانِتاً لِلَّهِ). 3- "He always walked in the straight line of 'Allah' and the path of truth" (حَنِيفاً). 4- "He was never of the polytheists," and the light of "Allah" alone filled his entire life, thoughts, and the corners of his heart (وَ لَمْ يَكُ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

[^1]: In the Islamic hadiths regarding Abd al-Muttalib, we read: "He will be resurrected on the Day of Judgment as a single Ummah, possessing the splendor of kings (of justice) and the countenance of Prophets." (Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 139). This was because he defended monotheism in an environment of polytheism and idol-worship.

5- Following these characteristics, he was ultimately a man who was grateful for all of Allah's blessings (شاكِراً لِأَنْعُمِهِ).

After stating these five attributes, the verse proceeds to mention five important results of these qualities:

1- "Allah chose Abraham for prophethood and the delivery of His message" (اجْتَباهُ). 2- "Allah guided him to a straight path" and protected him from any slip or deviation (وَ هَداهُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

This is because Divine guidance, as we have said many times, follows the merits and worthiness that a person demonstrates, for nothing is given to anyone without reason.

3- "We gave him good in this world" (وَ آتَيْناهُ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً). "Hasanah" (good) in its broad sense encompasses every kind of goodness, from the station of prophethood and messengership to material blessings, righteous children, and the like.

4- "And in the Hereafter, he is among the righteous" (وَ إِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

Even though Abraham was at the head of the lineage of the righteous, it nonetheless says he will be among the righteous. This is a sign of the greatness of the station of the righteous, that Abraham, with all his status, is counted among them. Is it not true that Abraham himself had requested this from Allah: "My Lord, grant me authority and join me with the righteous" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 83).

5- The final privilege that Allah gave to Abraham in return for all those outstanding qualities was that his school of thought became an inspiring one, not only for the people of his time but forever, especially for the Islamic Ummah, such that the Quran says: "Then We revealed to you to follow the religion of Abraham, the pure monotheist."

(ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ أَنِ اتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً) [^1] Once again, it emphasizes that Abraham was not of the polytheists (وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

In view of the previous verses, a question arises here: if the religion of Islam is the religion of Abraham and Muslims follow the traditions of Abraham in many matters—including the honoring of Friday—then why do the Jews consider Saturday (Sabbath) as a holiday and cease work? The last verse under discussion addresses this question and says: "The Sabbath (and the prohibitions of the Sabbath) was only prescribed for the Jews as a punishment, and even then they differed about it." Some accepted it and completely stopped working, while others disregarded it (إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ).

The story was that, according to some narrations, Moses عليه السلام invited the Israelites to honor and cease work on Friday, which was the tradition of Abraham. However, they refused it on some pretext and preferred Saturday. Allah then prescribed Saturday for them, but coupled it with severity and restrictions. Therefore, you should not cite the Saturday holiday as a standard, for it had an exceptional and punitive aspect. The best proof of this is that the Jews differed even regarding this day of their own choosing; a group honored it, while another group broke its sanctity, engaged in business, and were caught by Divine punishment.

There is also the possibility that the above verse relates to the innovations of the polytheists regarding animal foods. For after mentioning this in previous verses, the question arose: why were there prohibitions in the Jewish law that are not in Islam? The answer was given that those were punitive.

Once again, the question arises: why, for example, was fishing on Saturdays forbidden for the Jews, which is also not forbidden in Islam? Again, the answer is that this was considered a type of punishment and penalty for them.

In any case, this verse is linked to the verses of "The People of the Sabbath" (Verses 163-166 of Surah Al-A'raf), where the account of their story, the prohibition of fishing on Saturday, the opposition of a group of Jews to this command, the Divine trial, and the severe punishment they suffered was discussed (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, pp. 418-428).

Incidentally, it should be noted that "Sabt" (Sabbath) originally means ceasing work for rest. Saturday was called Yawm al-Sabt because the Jews ceased their usual business activities on it. This name then remained for this day in Islam, even though it was not a day of cessation from work.

At the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ).

As we have previously pointed out, one of the objectives realized on the Day of Judgment is the removal of differences in all fields and the return to absolute monotheism. For that day is the Day of Manifestation, the Day of Appearance, the day of uncovering secrets and inner thoughts, the removal of the veil, and the revealing of the hidden.

[^1]: "Hanif" refers to one who turns away from deviation toward truth and uprightness. In other words, he turns his eyes away from deviated religions and methods and turns toward the straight path of Allah—the religion that is in harmony with primordial nature (Fitrah). Because of this harmony, it is considered the straight path. Therefore, in the expression "Hanif," there is a subtle allusion to the innate nature of monotheism.

Ayahs

ٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَٱلْمَوْعِظَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ وَجَـٰدِلْهُم بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١٢٥)
Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. (125)
وَإِنْ عَاقَبْتُمْ فَعَاقِبُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَئِن صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِّلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٢٦)
Should you retaliate, retaliate with the like of what you have been made to suffer, but if you are patient that is surely better for the steadfast. (126)
وَٱصْبِرْ وَمَا صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُ فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (١٢٧)
So be steadfast, and you cannot be steadfast except with Allah [’s help]. And do not grieve for them, nor be upset by their guile. (127)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّٱلَّذِينَ هُم مُّحْسِنُونَ (١٢٨)
Indeed Allah is with those who are Godwary and those who are virtuous. (128)

Commentary

Ten Important Ethical Instructions in Dealing with Opponents

Throughout the various verses of this Surah, diverse discussions—sometimes gentle and sometimes stern—have been held with the polytheists, the Jews, and opposing groups in general; these discussions became particularly deep and intense in the recent verses.

At the conclusion of these discussions, which marks the end of Surah al-Nahl, a series of important ethical instructions regarding logical confrontation, methods of debate, the manner of punishment and forgiveness, and the way to stand against conspiracies and the like are presented. These can be termed the "tactical principles and methods of struggle against opponents in Islam," serving as a general law applicable at all times and in all places.

This program is summarized in ten principles which, according to the verses under discussion, are as follows:

1- First, He says: "Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom" (ادْعُ إِلى‌ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ).

"Wisdom" (Hikmah) means knowledge, science, logic, and reasoning. Originally, it carries the meaning of "prevention" or "restraint," and since knowledge, logic, and reasoning prevent corruption and deviation, they are called wisdom. In any case, the first step in inviting toward the Truth is the use of sound logic and calculated reasoning. In other words, reaching into the inner thoughts and minds of people, setting them in motion, and awakening dormant intellects is considered the first step.

2- "And goodly exhortation" (وَ الْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ).

This is the second step in the path of inviting to the way of Allah; that is, utilizing human emotions. This is because "exhortation" and "admonition" have a more emotional aspect, and by stimulating them, one can direct the vast masses of people toward the Truth.

In reality, wisdom utilizes the "intellectual dimension" of human existence, while goodly exhortation utilizes the "emotional dimension." [^1] Restricting "exhortation" with the attribute "goodly" (hasanah) perhaps indicates that admonition is effective only when it is free from any harshness, superiority, humiliation of the other party, or provocation of their sense of stubbornness. How many admonitions have had the opposite effect because they were performed, for instance, in the presence of others accompanied by humiliation, or because the speaker's sense of superiority was sensed? Therefore, exhortation leaves its deep impact only when it is "goodly" and implemented in a beautiful manner.

3- "And debate with them (the opponents) in a way that is best" (وَ جادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ‌).

This third step is specifically for those whose minds have been previously filled with incorrect matters; their minds must be cleared through debate so they may find the readiness to accept the Truth.

Naturally, disputation and debate are effective only when they are "in a way that is best" (بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ‌), where truth, justice, correctness, honesty, and sincerity govern it, and it is free from any insult, humiliation, falsehood, or arrogance. In short, all its human aspects must be preserved.

At the end of the first verse, He adds: "Indeed, your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path, and He knows best those who are guided" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ‌).

This is an indication that your duty is to invite to the path of Truth through the three calculated methods mentioned above; however, as to who will ultimately be guided and who will persist in the path of misguidance, only Allah knows, and that is all.

It is also possible that this sentence is a reason for the three instructions above; meaning that Allah has given these three instructions for dealing with the deviated because He knows what factor influences the misguided and what the means of guidance is.

4- Thus far, the talk was about participating in a logical, emotional discussion or a reasonable debate with opponents. However, if matters go beyond this and a conflict arises where they resort to aggression and transgression, here He instructs: "And if you punish, then punish with the like of that wherewith you were afflicted" (وَ إِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ‌), and no more than that.

5- "But if you endure patiently, it is better for those who are patient" (وَ لَئِنْ صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِلصَّابِرِينَ‌).

In some narrations, we read that this verse was revealed during the Battle of Uhud, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw the painful state of the martyrdom of his uncle, Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the enemy had not been content with killing him, but had torn open his chest and side with strange cruelty, pulled out his liver or heart, and cut off his ears and nose). He became very agitated and distressed and said:

"O Allah, to You belongs all praise, and to You I bring my complaint, and You are the One whose help is sought against what I see."

Then he said:

"If I gain victory over them, I will surely mutilate them, I will surely mutilate them, I will surely mutilate them" (and according to another narration, he said he would mutilate seventy of them).

At this moment, the verse was revealed: (وَ إِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ وَ لَئِنْ صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِلصَّابِرِينَ‌).

Immediately, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I will be patient, I will be patient" (اصبر اصبر)! Even though this moment was perhaps the most painful moment the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله experienced in his entire life, he regained control over his nerves and chose the second path, which was the path of forgiveness and pardon. And as we read in history regarding the Conquest of Mecca, on the day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله triumphed over these hard-hearted people, he issued a general amnesty and fulfilled his promise made at the Battle of Uhud. [^2] Truly, if one wishes to see a supreme example of magnanimity and human emotion, one must place the story of Uhud alongside the story of the Conquest of Mecca and compare the two.

Perhaps no victorious and dominant nation has ever treated a defeated people the way the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims treated the polytheists of Mecca after their victory—especially in an environment where revenge and malice were woven into the fabric of their society, and grudges were inherited from generation to generation, and where failing to seek revenge was considered a great disgrace.

The result of all this magnanimity, forgiveness, and pardon was that this ignorant and stubborn nation was so shaken and awakened that, as the Quran says: "They entered the religion of Allah in crowds" (يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْواجاً).

6- This forgiveness, pardon, and patience will have a definitive effect only if performed without any expectation—that is, solely for the sake of Allah. Therefore, the Quran adds: "And be patient, and your patience is not but through Allah" (وَ اصْبِرْ وَ ما صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ‌).

Can a human being endure and remain steadfast in the face of such soul-burning scenes that set one's entire being on fire without a Divine power and a spiritual motive? And accept all this distress in his soul without losing patience? Yes, this is possible only if it is for Allah and by the success granted by Allah.

7- Whenever all these efforts in the field of preaching and inviting toward Allah, and in the field of forgiveness and patience, do not prove effective, one must still not become hopeless or discouraged, nor should one show restlessness or grief. Rather, one must continue preaching with even more composure and calmness. Thus, in the seventh instruction, He says: "And do not grieve over them" (وَ لا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

This grief and sorrow, which is certainly due to their lack of faith, might produce one of two phenomena in a person: either it discourages him forever, or it leads him to restlessness, impatience, and lack of composure. Therefore, the prohibition of grief is actually a prohibition of both phenomena; meaning, in the path of inviting toward the Truth, neither be restless nor be hopeless.

8- Despite all these qualities, the stubborn enemy may still not cease their conspiracies and may engage in plotting dangerous schemes. In such circumstances, the correct stance is what the Quran says: "And do not be in distress because of what they plot" (وَ لا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ‌).

No matter how vast, calculated, and dangerous these conspiracies may be, they should not drive you out of the field, nor should you imagine that you are in a tight spot or besieged. For your support is Allah, and by seeking help from the power of faith, steadfastness, perseverance, intellect, and wisdom, you can neutralize these conspiracies and render them null.

The final verse under discussion, with which Surah al-Nahl concludes, refers to the ninth and tenth programs, saying:

9- "Indeed, Allah is with those who fear Him" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا).

"Piety" (Taqwa) in all its dimensions and its broad concept, including piety in dealing with opponents; meaning that even against the enemy, one must observe Islamic ethical principles. One must treat prisoners with Islamic conduct, observe fairness and politeness with the deviated, avoid lies and slander, and even on the battlefield—as stated in the principles of Islamic warfare—piety and Islamic standards must be maintained: the defenseless must not be attacked, children and the elderly must not be harmed, even animals must not be destroyed, fields must not be ruined, and water must not be cut off from the enemy. In short, piety and the observance of the principles of justice toward friend and foe alike must generally be practiced and implemented (except for very rare exceptional cases).

10- "And those who are doers of good" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مُحْسِنُونَ‌).

As the Quran has stated in other verses, sometimes one must respond to evil with good, and thus make the enemy feel ashamed and embarrassed, for this act turns the most hostile of enemies (أَلَدُّ الْخِصامِ‌) into a "devoted friend" (وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ‌)! Excellence and goodness (Ihsan), if performed at the right time and place, is one of the best methods of struggle. Islamic history is full of manifestations of this program: the behavior the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله showed toward the polytheists of Mecca after the conquest, the treatment he gave to Wahshi, the killer of Hamzah, the conduct he had with the prisoners of Badr, the behavior he showed toward the Jews who inflicted various types of harassment upon him, and similarly, the examples of this program in the life of Ali عليه السلام and the other great leaders, all testify to this Islamic instruction.

Interestingly, in Nahj al-Balagha, in the famous sermon of Hammam—who was a pious, worshipping, and vigilant man—we read that when he requested a comprehensive instruction from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام regarding the attributes of the God-fearing, the Imam contented himself with mentioning only this verse and said:

"Fear Allah and do good, for 'Indeed, Allah is with those who fear Him and those who are doers of good'." [[^1]]

Although the thirst and demand of this lover of Truth were not quenched by this brief statement, and the Imam was forced to provide a more detailed explanation—delivering the most comprehensive sermon on the attributes of the God-fearing, exceeding one hundred traits—it can be inferred from this initial short answer of the Imam that this verse is, in fact, a summary of all the attributes of the God-fearing.

A careful examination of the ten items of the above program, which define all the major and minor lines of dealing with opponents, shows that all logical, emotional, psychological, and tactical principles—in short, everything that causes influence over opponents—have been utilized.

Yet, despite this, Islam never says to content yourselves only with logic and reasoning; rather, in many cases, it becomes necessary to practically enter the field against the enemy's conspiracies, and if necessary, resort to force against violence, and counter-plot against conspiracies. But even at this stage, the principle of justice and the principles of piety and Islamic ethics must not be forgotten.

If Muslims had applied this comprehensive program against their opponents, perhaps Islam today would have covered the entire world or a major part of it.

Final Word on Surah al-Nahl (The Surah of Blessings)

As we stated at the beginning of the Surah, what attracts the most attention in the collection of verses in this Surah is the mention of various Divine blessings—material and spiritual, apparent and hidden, individual and social. It is for this reason that the name "Surah of Blessings" has been chosen for it.

An examination of the verses of the Surah shows that about forty blessings—large and small, material and spiritual—have been mentioned throughout the verses of this Surah, which are listed below. We emphasize that the goal is, first, to teach the lesson of Monotheism (Tawhid) and express the greatness of the Creator, and second, to strengthen the love and affection of humans for the Creator of these blessings and to stimulate their sense of gratitude:

1- The creation of the heavens (خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ‌). 2- The creation of the earth (وَ الْأَرْضَ‌). 3- The creation of cattle (وَ الْأَنْعامَ خَلَقَها). 4- Using their wool and skins for warmth/clothing (لَكُمْ فِيها دِفْ‌ءٌ). 5- Utilizing the benefits of animals (وَ مَنافِعُ‌). 6- Using their meat (وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ‌). 7- Benefiting from the "beauty of economic independence" (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها جَمالٌ‌). 8- Utilizing animals for transportation (وَ تَحْمِلُ أَثْقالَكُمْ‌—وَ الْخَيْلَ وَ الْبِغالَ وَ الْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوها). 9- Guidance to the Straight Path (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ‌). 10- The descent of rain from the sky and providing drinking water from it (هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً لَكُمْ مِنْهُ شَرابٌ‌). 11- Cultivating pastures and grazing lands with it (وَ مِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ‌). 12- The growth of crops, olives, date palms, grapes, and all kinds of fruits by its blessing (يُنْبِتُ لَكُمْ بِهِ الزَّرْعَ وَ الزَّيْتُونَ وَ النَّخِيلَ وَ الْأَعْنابَ وَ مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ‌). 13- The subjection of the night and the day (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ). 14- The subjection of the sun and the moon (وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ). 15- The subjection of the stars (وَ النُّجُومُ‌). 16- The diverse creatures created on the earth (وَ ما ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُهُ‌). 17- The subjection of the seas to utilize their meat and jewelry (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَ تَسْتَخْرِجُوا مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها). 18- The movement of ships upon the waters (وَ تَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ‌). 19- The creation of mountains which stabilize the earth (وَ أَلْقى‌ فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ‌). 20- The creation of rivers (وَ أَنْهاراً). 21- The creation of connecting paths (وَ سُبُلًا). 22- The creation of signs to recognize the paths (وَ عَلاماتٍ‌). 23- Using the stars for guidance at night (وَ بِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ‌). 24- The revival of dead lands by rain water (وَ اللَّهُ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها). 25- Pure and pleasant milk that comes from between blood and digested food (نُسْقِيكُمْ مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِهِ مِنْ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَ دَمٍ لَبَناً خالِصاً سائِغاً لِلشَّارِبِينَ‌). 26- Products obtained from the fruits of the date palm and grapes (وَ مِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَ الْأَعْنابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَ رِزْقاً حَسَناً). 27- Honey, a healing food (فِيهِ شِفاءٌ لِلنَّاسِ‌). 28- Granting humans spouses from their own kind (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً). 29- The blessing of children and grandchildren (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَ حَفَدَةً). 30- Various types of pure provisions in their broad sense (وَ رَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ‌). 31- The blessing of hearing (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ‌). 32- The blessing of sight (وَ الْأَبْصارَ). 33- The blessing of intellect and heart (وَ الْأَفْئِدَةَ). 34- Houses and permanent dwellings (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَناً). 35- Mobile homes (tents) (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ جُلُودِ الْأَنْعامِ بُيُوتاً). 36- Tools and furnishings made from the wool, fur, and hair of animals (وَ مِنْ أَصْوافِها وَ أَوْبارِها وَ أَشْعارِها أَثاثاً وَ مَتاعاً إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌). 37- The blessing of shades (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلالًا). 38- The blessing of secure shelters in the mountains (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْجِبالِ أَكْناناً). 39- Various types of garments that protect humans from heat and cold (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ). 40- Armor and battle dress that resist the strikes of the enemy (وَ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمْ بَأْسَكُمْ‌).

At the end of this section of blessings, He adds: (كَذلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ‌): "Thus does He complete His favor upon you that you may submit [to Him]."

The Purpose of Detailing the Blessings

It goes without saying that enumerating the various Divine blessings in different verses of this Surah or other verses of the Quran is not for the purpose of "reminding of favors" (mann) or gaining prestige and the like, for Allah is higher and greater than all of these, and is independent of everyone and everything.

All of these are in the path of constructive educational programs—programs that lead a human being to the final stage of possible perfection from both spiritual and material perspectives.

The most eloquent proof for this subject is the very phrases that appear at the end of many of the preceding verses, which, despite their variety, all lie in the same line of human cultivation and education.

In verse 14, after mentioning the blessing of subjecting the seas, He says: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌) "that you may be grateful."

In verse 15, after mentioning the blessing of mountains, rivers, and roads, He says: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ‌) "that you may be guided."

In verse 44, after mentioning the greatest spiritual blessing, namely the revelation of the Quranic verses, He says: (وَ لَعَلَّهمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌) "and that they may reflect."

In verse 78, after mentioning the very important blessing of the tools of cognition (hearing, sight, and intellect), He says: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌) "that you may be grateful."

In verse 81, after referring to the completion of the Lord's blessings, He says: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ‌) "that you may submit."

And in verse 90, after stating a series of instructions regarding justice, excellence, and the struggle against indecency, evil, and oppression, He says: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ‌) "that you may take heed."

In reality, in these six instances, five goals are pointed out: 1- Gratitude, 2- Guidance, 3- Reflection, 4- Submission to the call of Truth, 5- Remembrance and taking heed. Without doubt, all are linked together: a human first reflects and thinks. Whenever he forgets, he remembers. Following that, the sense of gratitude toward the Bestower of blessings awakens in him. Then he opens a way toward Him and is guided. Finally, he submits to His command.

In this way, the five goals mentioned above are the sequential links of human perfection. Undoubtedly, if this path is traversed correctly, it yields fruitful results, and no room for doubt remains that enumerating these blessings—sometimes collectively and sometimes individually—has no goal other than building the Perfect Human.

O Lord! Your endless blessings have surrounded our entire existence, and we are drowned in Your favors, yet we have not known You.

O Allah! Grant us an understanding and vision that opens the path to Your gnosis and love, and a success that rushes to our aid in this winding path and brings us to the destination of the truly grateful.

O God! You know our needs better than anyone, and You recognize our innate requests better than we do ourselves; grant us that which makes us as You wish, and bestow upon us that which makes us better than what people imagine.

O Deity! Now that this part of the commentary of Your great Heavenly Book concludes, it is the last day of the month of Sha'ban, on the threshold of the month of mercy, the month of Ramadan; include us all in Your special mercy and do not withhold from us the success to complete this commentary (Indeed, You are the All-Hearing, the Responder).

End of Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh (End of Surah al-Nahl) End of Sha'ban 1401
